Academia is an enjoyable cute management game where you build a school and as your success grows, you go up in the school rankings, get better funding and expand the school. You can also sell your school and build a new one if you so choose.

I played this title as part of my quest to play my entire library and I'm glad I did. However, do yourself a favour and don't take the game too seriously, it's cute, fun  and a bit of a time waster, not for hardcore management vets who want 100% efficiency and all that jazz.

I really enjoy simulator games. I was playing prison architect and stumbled across this game which have a similar style to them and like it a lot. The game itself is fun to play. The updates so far have been quite nice and I'm excited for more stuff to come out like maybe clubs after school and more decorative options. The only complaint is some issues with the kids not eating even thought there's a giant cafeteria with enough food for them. Other than that, the games great.
Pretty nice game, but also REALLY boring. After you build your dream school it's over. You can't do anything anymore.

If you liked prison architect and are into tycoon type games where you are building a school, then this is the game for you. It's simple but has a good research tree and keeps you busy. Great attention to small details like being able to build an all boys school or an all girls school and free building. I'd only say I wish they allowed you to assign certain rooms. Like the classrooms, you have to assign by grade level. But for instance, the computer lab can be used by all grade levels. But I can only assign it as a computer lab. I can't assign it to only seniors or freshmen. So If I place them all the way over with my senior classrooms, the freshman from the other side of the school are taking forever to walk to that side and use the computer labs. Otherwise, it's an excellent game that will keep you busy and be fun in the process.

The game is  A W E S O M E.
You can customise the location of the school. Unfortunately you can't change the shape of the logo but you can change what's on there. There's a build phase after you've finished making the uniforms, school name, motto and choosing the principal. The build phase is where you can start building your school including the facilities you'll have there along with researching different topics to become the best. Researching is basically upgrading. You can unlock better teachers, Eco -friendly options with electricity and water, able to unlock more land, maxing out your schools student population to 700, workers work faster, more furniture, walls, floors, plants, fences and gates. The zoning feature is great because you can stop students from interacting with teachers and workers in certain places while the feature also tells you the basic criteria to build the room you want to build. Goons will try and ruin your school, you can easily remove them by completing the detention rooms research. Students are quite unique compared to the staff, they confess their love to other students, they can rebel, they all have different traits, they follow the schedule you make for them alongside having the capability to become ill. The rebelling is that they try skip school, smoke in the bathroom, bully students or are just rude. You can choose what to do as punishments for each type of rebellion. (The only way they are caught is by having monitors at your school.) Overall, you get a great looping experience, every 9 days a new year starts, you compete rival schools while getting ranked for the effort your students put in during their exams. The only downsides to this game is lack of events, school trips, workers bugging out and not doing a single task for either a full hour or whole day, ( I usually just re-spawn the workers.), lack of land to own, too long of days (even on full speed, maybe add another fast forward button?), I also dislike how you can't just view all the students from a certain year. This is because I want to favourite all of my Seniors before they leave so that they feel appreciated and motivated to do more work. Another issue is that the school pet becomes basically useless by the mid to end-game the pets objective becomes obsolete as you have more facilitates to boost your students happiness rates. My final issue with this game is how you can't "start" a game unless you use the normal mode instead of the sandbox that gives you unlimited money but I solved this issue by using a mod. If you enjoy simulator / tycoon / slice of life type games you will enjoy this game's addictive game-play.
The game is pretty fun and the devs are constantly making improvements. Also they have incredible support if you have any questions or feedback. If you are interested go ahead and get it.

Really good. I would like to see multiple floors and things like fire drills and actual fires, with staff having to respond. Also, it would be nice to see special events such as sports day, and competitions. Really good game though, with loads of opportunities. Would recommend

Imitation is the sincerest form of flattery but this game takes so much inspiration from Prison Architect that I think there's a legal case to be made. So many things are lifted directly from PA such as staffing (build office zones, hire staffers like lawyers that occupy them one to an office after you research them), research and grants, zones, building methodology (little worker guys hover around Delivery zones then go do it) that it's all jarring in the end. Unfortunately it's all done with an inferior UI that feels like it belongs more in an alpha than a finished product. It lacks polish and detail and seems to achieve the bare minimum while making it hard to see important facts like how your teachers are assigned, or alert you to problems like subjects with no teachers covering them.

What the game really seems to suffer from though is a shallow gameplay loop. You have a build phase at the start of the year and then you kick off the year and then...there's just not a lot to do. Your kids go to class, your janitors clean, your monitors grab the ones smoking in the bathroom, and short of doing some tweaks to logistics there's not a lot of conflict going on here for you to diagnose. Prison Architect solved this by challenging you with things like contraband, intra-prisoner rivalries, riots, and other events, but Academia feels like the team never quite hit on a solid answer to what should keep the player occupied. Some of it feels like roadblocks they threw up to slow down your kids' improvements just to keep you plugging away - for example, a Track improves your kids' P.E. class...but only for Freshmen.

I do feel like the underlying idea here is great. It just needed a more solid UI and core gameplay loop and to break free of the Prison Architect source material that it was obviously, incredibly inspired by.

I absolutely adore this game, and even played on a family computer under a shared account before purchasing this for myself eventually, It's a great time sink, and very easy to grasp if you are just getting into building/management games or if you just want something to chill and screw around with. My only problem with this game, is it seems it was given up on by devs. I really wish they put out more updates/added more. I understand games like this can only go so far but I feel as though this one wasn't pushed just far enough. Regardless though, absolutely awesome game!


TLDR; Great game for chilling, just wish they updated it more than they did

Game is shallow and I easily lose interest. Would be worth at half the current price.

Gave the game another 5 hours, still don't like it. Too shallow and animations are not that great for the price.

[b] Overview [/b]
If you’ve ever wanted to design a school from the ground up, you’ve come to the right place. Academia: School Simulator gets you in at the conception phase as you lead your institution from establishing its name and emblem to achieving Ivy League greatness. Every step along the way involves you and the decisions that you make including not only the construction and design of its structures but also in who is employed there and the environment and overall routine of those who populate your center of learning.

[b] The Founding Of Your Very Own School [/b]
When you first start your game you are brought to the school customization screen. Here you decide on your school’s name, motto, emblem, and student uniform as well as the difficulty settings for your game. There are a number of options to mess around with to your liking and you are certainly not lacking in freedom of choice, though I would have liked even more options; the addition of teacher uniforms and biomes, for example, could do wonders for further differentiating one school from the next. However, even in its current state, the customization on this screen is more advanced and in-depth than one might assume and it was an enjoyable and intuitive experience that created a first impression that made me quite optimistic about the game that I was about to buckle down and play for several hours.

[b] The Ribbon Cutting Ceremony [/b]
No, you’re not actually getting a ribbon-cutting ceremony. Once your school is founded you will find yourself looking at a large, empty plot of land graced only with a road and a significant number of trees and bushes. This is where you will spend the vast majority of the game, the exception only being at the end of each year with a brief summary of your institution’s performance. Your first step will be the construction of a principal’s office and starting with this the keys to the castle are in your hands and within moments, you’ll be introduced to three of the most integral aspects of Academia: School Simulator: grants, admissions, and staffing.

Grants are tasks that you can complete for additional funding that payout both in advance and upon completion. This type of funding is very important to the future success of your school and the tasks may include anything from hiring a few janitors and placing a few trash cans to researching and constructing a library.

Admissions are key to the success of your institution as they are the primary source of your income. Each year, including the first, you are informed of the number of students that have applied to attend your school and you may choose to accept as many of them as you have the capacity for. Every student comes with an upfront tuition cost that is immediately added to your treasury upon the start of the school year as well as having the effect of increasing the subsidy that you receive daily as long as they continue to remain in attendance.

Your staff is just as important as your student body as your school will simply not function without them. Most of the staff in the game are generic currently, for example, one cook is exactly the same as the next cook regarding their wages and efficiency although they may have varying names and appearances. Your teachers are the one exception to this rule and they are a major one; every teacher has a set of six skills, one for each subject that is taught at your school, and this determined how capable they are of ensuring that your students achieve the highest grades that they can. The better your students perform, the more your school is rewarded, the more its prestige grows, and the larger the number of candidates who seek to join your ranks the following year will grow. Teachers are separated into three categories as well: Bachelor’s, Master’s, and Doctorate degrees that offer significantly different levels of efficiency in their teaching as you might imagine.

[b] But How Are The Graphics? [/b]
The graphics and audio are not by any means going to push any system to its limits unless you are breaking out your Commodore 64 for the experience. I enjoy the simple art style and it never caused me to feel as if my immersion had been broken, as a matter of fact, the simplicity played in its favor by always keeping me on track and avoiding any distractions that came up. The music and sound effects were above average as well and worked well for the game, there weren’t many but they set the mood as calm and relaxed, similar to something that you might hear in a game like Stardew Valley. In short, both the graphics and the audio work well for the game but they most likely won’t be blowing anyone’s mind.

[b] What Is Solid? [/b]
I played through nearly all of the content in this game twice on the default settings before writing this review and there are a number of features that are enjoyable. The first time, I made a ‘classic’ school that was a single building and designed to be a standard prep school and chose to use the British school uniforms (Gianni Bato Prep); the second time, I decided to make a military academy campus that was split into several buildings and the students were required to wear military dress (Marshmallow Military Academy). I was happy to see that both were efficient and that neither seemed to be a worse option than the other. I also enjoyed the variety of room types that you can unlock for your school, everything ranging from libraries to AV rooms to a variety of sports-focused rooms and so on; I never felt that my options were limited in this regard. The reports that are available to you were some of the most streamlined and efficient ones that I have seen in a game of this type, I always knew exactly what needed to be improved to keep my students fulfilled and out of trouble.

During the time that I played I encountered only one real issue with the game and it was one that was text-based. One of the grants on the list had a requirement that was not accurate for the technology required to unlock the building that was the only other requirement to receive the completion funds. I notified the developers via Steam and received a response within only a few hours of sending it that included a genuine ‘thank you’ and it added another layer of optimism for me that this game is destined to be great in the future.

[b] What Can Be Improved? [/b]
As contradictory as it sounds after reading my earlier praise, I believe that additional rooms would certainly add to the longevity of the game. Although my first school took several hours to get to the point of unlocking all of the content for building, on my second run I had it all constructed on my campus within an hour and a half.

I would also suggest additional challenges that arise during the course of your institution’s career. My first year with Gianni Bato Prep was fairly challenging but by the time the windfall of funds from admissions arrived at the beginning of the second year, I never had any financial difficulty again. I still very much enjoyed the casual nature of building and managing a growing school but there was nothing left that offered a challenge. My experience with Marshmallow Military Academy was simply casual from start to finish after having learned the nature of the game previously.

[b] Verdict [/b]
My overall recommendation on this game currently is to pick it up if you’re a fan of management and construction games like Prison Architect, these games are very similar in their feel even if their themes are quite different. The game that is currently available to be played is certainly playable, I didn’t feel that I was grinding through the hours to experience enough to write this review, I genuinely enjoyed every second in the game. It could certainly use more content, though, and within less than a dozen hours, I had experienced nearly everything that the game had to offer.

So the usual caveat of “It’s an Early Access Game” applies here. You take that on board when you buy the game so early. You can definitely tell that it has roots from Prison Architect as it has that same charm, look and feel about it – just without the gritty nature. It’s fairly easy to pick up and play without instruction, even if it does seem a little daunting at first: But if you’ve played the likes of Prison Architect, Sim Airport, Another Brick In the Mall etc then you will be fine with this. I’ve clocked up an hour on the game and enjoyed that hour – There’s not much to do in the game at the moment, other than a few classes, cafeteria and support functions; which you’ll master all of that in no time at all - so an hour is pretty much all you need **RIGHT NOW** but that’s OK. It all runs as you expect it, was stable enough and never once did I think “Oh God, I’d rather play something else” before I felt like I was done.
Right now, it’s not an addictive game like Prison Architect, Another Brick or Sim Airport is.. but that’s purely due to the size of the game right now and not because of the quality of what has been released so far. If this game ends up being anything like these other games are, then you’re on to an epic early backer.

I recommend the game, because it will scratch that building/management itch for a while.  It's a pretty basic game, build a school, hire teachers, fill the needs of the students.  Too me though, it's so basic, after a good few days of playing, you've done everything, and it just becomes sitting there watching the same thing over and over.  To me, the game lacks anything that would be "fun" or could make some real thinking.  There are no sports in this school game, no football team, which is pretty much the backbone of high schools today.  There are no competitions of any sorts, no chess team, no glee club, no debate team, no track team, nothing.  You just have students that come, teachers that teach, and then random stuff might happen, like a student leaves because he can’t find a toilet or there isn’t enough food.  Teachers don’t get better or worse, your schools don’t compete at all.  After you have your school running, there really isn’t anything to do.  There isn’t even a graduation in the game, no ceremony, just at the end of the year, you get a grade card, you can see how each student did, and that’s it.  The price is a decent price and there is enough enjoyment for around 10-15 hours, but it is very basic without a whole lot of depth.

It is dang hard. I am sure I would get used to it if I kept trying. I go in a cycle of starting a new school, trying to get it built, realizing halfway through that I don't have enough money to finish the build, delete a bunch of stuff to get some money back to do the research for bank loans, then realize I don't have the money to hire an accountant to secure the bank loan, crash out and shut off the game for a few months. I literally have never even started the school year in over 10 hours of play time. Even when I get the perk that makes everything cost less... Can't figure it out and I don't want to stop trying to figure it out.

So, the thing to keep in mind about this game is it is always going to be in the shadow of Prison Architect. Its mechanics are extremely similar, such as with the grant system, and while that's an amusing, if bleak, commentary on the education system, it raises an important question: What does this offer over Prison Architect? And my answer to that is you don't have to deal with anything relating to Paradox, such as the launcher, and the setting is far less bleak than Prison Architect. However, unlike Prison Architect, your income is far more fixed, which means from what I can tell, you can end up in a situation where you have to wait around a bit to do certain kinds of upgrades. 

Hence, I would say this game is definitely worth getting, but maybe not at full price. I do wish it had gotten expansions, if they had to be DLC, for say, stuff like boarding schools, but judging by how most of Prison Architect's DLC worked out, maybe it's for the best this game never went for that. This isn't a bad game, to be clear, it's just again, one that's extremely in the shadow of a game related to it in a way that can make it hard to judge as its own thing.

I liked the idea behind the game, and I even enjoyed it for a moment, but after playing through two campaigns with generally negligible differences at moderate difficulty, I quickly realized that I had purchased a game that I would maybe play for an hour or two, and would collect digital dust in my library.


Pros - I enjoyed the event scenarios that generated random perks and drawbacks. Stuck in traffic? A police officer will help you out and gain you some logistics points (basically this game's action points), but the scandal will follow you around. A well-connected individual approaches you for some favors for his son? Expose him and gain an uptick in public opinion or help him for added fundraising perks.

Cons - Once you've played through the game and learned the mechanics, there is very little challenge to it. Scandals only matter for a few thousand voters and do not seem to impact the election unless it's close (it's never really that close). If you have the money and logistics, the election is easily won. The animal politicians are superficial sprites. They do not give you individual strengths and weaknesses; they're only the representation of your character. You can have a peaceful, religious lion (which matters little except for the preferred campaign platforms for each district) or an aggressive, money hungry mouse. 

If I had discovered this game for $5 on my cell phone, I might be tempted to recommend it, but for $14.99 on the PC, I think you will thank me for recommending that you spend that money elsewhere.

I don't normally write reviews, this is my first one in fact.  But I saw some of the negative reviews and just thought I would put my two cents in.  To start, yes it is a pretty straight forward game.  You won't find much depth here compared to Democracy.  However, what is there is quite fun.  I've only played through a few campaigns so far, but I enjoyed all my experiences.  My first playthrough I just sort of got used to how turns worked and to efficiently use my staff.  I abandoned that game and started a second, and I was pretty easily able to get a landslide victory in Autumn Island (USA basically) on the games default settings.  

Looking for more of a challenge, I tried a harder difficulty campaign on Winter Island (UK plus Ireland), with a different opponent (you can choose their personality, clean, corrupt, pragmatic or unpredictable.  This effects what kind of staff they hire and how they play the game.)  Anyway, my third game was wildly different from my first.  In the first two games we started out on the east and west coast of Autumn Island and pretty much used the whole time to lockdown states near the start with minimal fighting in the middle of the country over any state.  

On Winter Island however, the two most populous starting provinces are relatively close, and there is a choke point province between them that can be blockaded to make it difficult for the opposing party to move.  Suddenly I was having to send my staff through neutral and red territory, wasting turns trying to get to a specific province.  The point is, if you choose the easiest map on the default difficulty, win easily and decide to shelve this game, you are missing out.  Solid 8/10 in my opinion.

I'm an ultimate newbie here, but here's my experience with the game so far:

1. Customization - I'm very particular about details, so I really, really liked customizing every bit of the traits, skills, and weaknesses of my Lion and staff (my favorite so far is Eduardog the Preacher) and my platform. You also get to choose the length of the campaign, starting funds, and difficulty of your AI opponent. (Since it was my first game, I opted for the shortest campaign, the highest amount of funds and an easy but corrupt AI opponent.)

2. Difficulty - it took me some time to get all the actions (e.g. bribing, rallying, raising funds, giving gifts to district patrons), but the tutorial helped a lot. The game uses funds and logistics as requirements for each action, so the challenge lies in effectively using these resources to build your reputation on the island.  Random situations and events will pop up mid game, leaving moral dilemmas. I originally wanted a clean campaign for myself, but when I started losing funds, I found myself agreeing to under-the-table deals for additional funds and patron relationship. (I used influence to help a Patron's child enter a school when he/she didn't pass the entrance exam. (yikes) I also loaned from a cute Shark!)

Given that I won by only 100,000+ votes (whew!) against a corrupt donkey in the Autumn Island (the easiest map), I really am looking forward to trying out the difficult maps and competing with their best AI.  

Has anyone won against a Crazy - level opponent in the most difficult island? 

3. Strategy from beginning to finish - this game really has strategy written all over it. Every action requires studying the available data about the districts (e.g. population, available funds, concerns, relationship with the patrons) and planning tactics for your next turns. If you prefer research-like tasks like I do, then you might want to try the game out. Also, all actions have a corresponding variety of advantages and disadvantages, so the outcome will really depend on your own strategy. (I gained Patron's support after helping his child, but this made me vulnerable to scandals. Mr. Shark loaned me a significant amount, but he billed me with huge interest during my next turn.)

I found Giving Gifts to Patrons helpful in minimizing the logistic costs, but this meant spending more for the gifts at the beginning. I remember opting to change one of my staff to a Bunny who has great taste in gifts for some bonus points. 

Overall, the customization, sound, appearance, and mechanics made it entertaining for me. :) I’ll see if I can win against a difficult AI without using dirty actions on my next game!

P.S. In giving gifts to patrons, developers might want to consider adding another slider for the percentage. I just thought that this way, there won't be a need to move the fund slider back and forth just to reach my desired percentage.

Political Animals is a game about the campaign trail. The music and art is cute. And the animal sounds are adorable. I'm a huge fan of the Positech-published strategy games that have come out in the last couple of years. During the Steam Summer Sale, I purchased Democracy 3, which was a little too accurate and frustrating when it came to political simulators. Every path I took seemed to end in death.

I am happy to report that this is a much more cheerful political simulator. Croccy may be a cutie, but don't be fooled by his appearance. He will bribe, scandalize, and steal away your voters to make sure that you don't win. Of course, my chosen candidate, Panda, could have done the same, but I guess I'm just one of the few honest politicians left. :P

If I had to complain about one thing, it's that I haven't figured out how to make the reporter point out any of my opponent's scandals, but that's probably user error. Otherwise, there are multiple ways to win over votes for multiple playstyles. I just wish it were multiplayer so that I could play it with friends!

I had fun, but lots of stuff doesn't work. The logistics bonus trait for one, which you can see right on the main screen. If they didn't fix that when it's easy to see, did they leave other bugs? Yes. Many oher modifiers don't work either.
Similarly some staff aren't as useful as others. Not even when you're trying to synergize for a specific strategy, It's not effective.
I want to like this but it's not a finished product.
Political Machine 2016 had bugs too so that one's not an alternative either.
If you can get it for free you can have fun for a few hours, but I can't recommend paying for it.

I recommend the game when it's discounted, like £1. 
It could worth more if the developer cared of it. But they don't care. They haven't fixed a serious achievement bug for years.
But if you don't mind the achievements, just the fun, this game is funny and playable for a few days. It gives you a view into the world of politics in a comedic way. I'm happy that I've bought it, but that also that I have it for cheap as it doesn't worth more in this way, that the developer has abandoned it.

(Disclaimer: received the product for free and also did some beta testing)

Put in about 2 hours of gameplay, here are my thoughts.

The game is a lightweight simulation, almost bite-sized. It is advertised as such so a plus there. The game takes you through a political campaign with a variety of critters with odd personality quirks and staff with specializations. The game plays fairly straight-forward, with your making moves with your campaign staff, then the AI or other human player. You go back and forth until the campaign end.

The game tries to simulate and emulate real-world politics, and it does an okay job of it. I found the random events to be a  little not-so-random even after two playthroughs. The animals, unfortunately, are little more than skins as you can alter personality quirks as you wish. This makes replaying the game to unlock the other animals to be kind of pointless, unless you want a new skin. While there is a sense of replayability in the game, with the various time lengths, budget amounts, and overall "dirtyness" the AI can have, it is difficult to say how many times you'd play through it.

The super cartooney nature of the game will definitely be off-putting to some people. I personally would've preferred slightly less elementary-school level cartoon feel to the animals. Still, the graphics combined with the PC nature of the text the game is suitable for all audiences and anyone with a hankering for politics might get a kick out of it. Also too anyone that likes to min-max and find perfect strategies for the win conditions may enjoy tinkering with the game to figure out that perfect strategy to achieve election.

There aren't any oddities that really would hamper me from recommending the game, although I feel like the pricetag is kind of high for the lack of variety the game has. I'd tell people to get it on sale, even at 10% off, to take some of the sting out of it (at the time I'm posting this, the Steam Summer Sale has it 90% off. Great time to grab it.)

Check out my Youtube video for my playthrough of the game:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Giy6y4LLM5I

Political Animals is an awesome boardgame-esque election campaign simulator with a twist: adorable anthropomorphic animals. In it, you wage war for the hearts and minds of the inhabitants with sorties across equally cute islands (shaped after real countries) in a race to raise the most votes during a campaign period of 15 to 30 days.

Brought to you by the artist behind Spacechem and Prison Architect as well as the coder behind Warrior Defense and the publisher behind the Democracy series, Political Animals has a laid-back charm that draws you in with its character - it's like Richard Scarry's animal Busytown with a Pixar-esque adult twist: You can choose to play it clean with your campaign or pull out all the stops with bribery, intimidation, scandals, backstabbing, blackmail and the works. Random events are funny too. While you can win the game with one playstyle, the ability to mix up play styles and customize your character and "roleplay" a game is a large part of the fun.

Unfortunately, the game is currently single player only (with mind-boggling-to-me AI as a game developer given the depth of the game). The ability to play with friends (or strangers), role-play, trash talk and trade insults would have upped the ante tons. Hopefully it's something in the cards for a future patch if the game makes enough for a round 2 of development.

All in all, I give Political Animals an 8.5/10.

Political Animals, developed by Squeaky Wheel and published by Positech Games, offers a unique blend of turn-based strategy and political satire set in a vibrant world populated entirely by anthropomorphic animals. The game places you in the role of a candidate in a fiercely competitive election, where every decision—from public speeches to backroom deals—shapes your path to power. What sets Political Animals apart is its ability to distill the chaotic and often morally ambiguous world of politics into an approachable, bite-sized strategy experience, combining charm with a surprisingly sharp critique of political tactics and campaign dynamics.

At its core, the gameplay revolves around managing your campaign through a series of turn-based actions on a hexagonal board representing the political landscape. Players must carefully allocate limited resources—such as campaign funds, staff, and political capital—while making choices that influence voter opinion and media coverage. Whether you’re delivering speeches to rally supporters, negotiating with lobbyists, or strategically deploying your campaign team to key regions, each move demands foresight and adaptability. The game introduces a range of candidate types, each with unique skills and political ideologies, encouraging players to experiment with different approaches, whether it be populist, authoritarian, or grassroots campaigning.

One of the game’s most compelling aspects is how it balances accessibility with strategic depth. While the mechanics are straightforward enough to welcome newcomers to strategy games, there is enough nuance in managing alliances, sabotaging opponents, and responding to scandals to keep more experienced players engaged. The political decisions you make often come with trade-offs, forcing players to consider the ethical implications of their actions. For example, you can choose to maintain integrity and focus on positive campaigning, or resort to smear tactics and bribery to secure votes, reflecting real-world political dilemmas. This moral dimension adds layers of complexity and replayability, as different choices lead to varied outcomes and endings.

Visually, Political Animals embraces a bright, cartoonish art style that cleverly contrasts with the sometimes cutthroat subject matter. Each candidate is vividly designed as a distinct animal, embodying various stereotypes and political archetypes—ranging from cunning foxes to stubborn bulls—adding humor and personality to the gameplay. The colorful environments, lively animations, and playful music create an inviting atmosphere that tempers the game’s satirical edge without diminishing its critique of political machinations. This approachable aesthetic broadens the game’s appeal, making it suitable for players who might be intimidated by more complex political simulations.

Despite its strengths, the game has some limitations. The campaign length is relatively short, which while making the game easy to pick up and play, may leave some players wanting more depth and longevity. Certain elements, like the AI behavior and some campaign events, can feel repetitive after multiple playthroughs. Additionally, the game’s humor and satire, though clever, might not land equally well for everyone, especially those deeply familiar with the intricacies of real-world politics who might find the caricatures somewhat simplistic. However, these issues do little to detract from the overall experience, especially for players looking for a light-hearted yet thoughtful take on political strategy.

In summary, Political Animals successfully combines engaging turn-based strategy with a witty exploration of political campaigning, wrapped in an accessible and charming package. Its blend of ethical choices, resource management, and tactical gameplay provides a fresh perspective on the cutthroat nature of politics, while the anthropomorphic cast and vibrant presentation keep the tone fun and inviting. Although it may not satisfy players seeking a deep or highly realistic political simulation, it stands out as an entertaining and insightful game that both educates and amuses. For anyone curious about the messy art of winning votes—without taking themselves too seriously—Political Animals is a welcome and memorable experience.

Game customization options include candidate selection (including traits and special abilities), political platforms, game map (caricatures of real-world locations), staff members, and opponent. A variety of map overlays are available to show different data, but some areas of the map are crowded. Funds and logistics (actions per turn) are used to execute orders: move to a new district, rally to promote a specific issue, campaign for votes, buy a gift to increase a patron’s rating, raise funds, bribe for votes, or a special action. Higher patron ratings make fund raising, campaigns, and other actions more effective in a district, while captured districts (80% patron ratings and 80% of the vote) are harder for the opposition to claim. Beyond initially claiming a couple of districts, strategy involves promoting issues in additional districts to persuade undecided voters and taking advantage of enemy scandals to swing the vote. Events create unpredictability that can unravel any strategy. The AI is good at the game, although it does perform unncessary actions (like performing actions in districts they can’t win, or raising money on the last turn). Although the theme makes it look like a casual game, Political Animals does have some sophisticated political gameplay.

Like any game there quite a few parts to this, and i can definitely say that the game is deceptively strategic. The cute artstyle and music really put me into a relaxed mindset and that gave me quite a lul when i started, but after being destroyed quite handedly, i found myself taking notes and reading a lot more on what the game is communicating. 


I'ts a lot of thinking ahead and planning your moves, and in that manner its very much a amazing title if you enjoy strategy with various pathways to victory.

The customization also doesn't disappoint with various characters ,abilities and options available all around, but always doled out in interesting bits and unlocks. 

My issues though, do exist - the game is quite buggy. I've had 2 crashes to desktop and various situations where the game become unresponsive by not letting me end a turn (or do anything else). also the music options reset every time you boot up the game and all the graphical option can be summed up by the world "unity launcher".

Now, i cant speak (and that's why it's not a full review) yet on the strategy and how much there is of that, or if it turns gimmicky with "1 path to victory" or if the Ai scales properly, not can i comment on the balance or gameplay information being displayed being sufficient. 

but the first 2 hours were enjoyable for sure!

Ruinarch is one of those conceptually solid games where no one ever thought about what the gameplay loop was going to be. 

The basic idea is: "what if you're an evil mastermind in the shadows who can twist the minds of the locals in the nearby villages to slowly go insane and eventually do all the killing for you." Like you can literally program them to be serial killers with one of the archetypes, which is moderately funny, or make them all kleptomaniacs so they get into brawls over who stole whose stuff.

But the thing is... like... there's no pushback, at all. Yeah, the villagers will occasionally kill the kleptos and serial killers so you need to make some more but... there's no event where, after ten spree killers, a cleric shows up and starts protecting people in the village, perhaps curing them of your influence(thus, perhaps, forcing you to use the other villages or other means to in some way take him out so you can progress). Nor do the villagers ever put two and two together and realize that there's a reason they've all started becoming narcoleptic and half their neighbours are vampires. They only ever attack you if you put your lair somewhere they encounter during their normal wanderings.

You also don't ever have to be subtle, because all powers are just applied by clicking a button. It's not like, say, you're headquartered out of a spooky, but seemingly abandoned, ruin, and you need to be subtle or risk attracting attention, and maybe deal with investigators and/or adventurers who are simply greedy.

My point is, really, that the only challenge this game has, isn't based on needing you to think, or have quick reflexes. All it requires of you is patience, which is... a real letdown.

Also why the hell doesn't it have a minimap? That's an insane oversight. 

The villagers are also SUPER unappealing since they're just random clusters of huts, they don't grow according to a road grid or... anything that makes them look not messy and randomly generated.

I'd suggest that it might get better, being in early access, but it appears that they're on step 8 out of 9 on their roadmap, so I wouldn't expect fundamental changes like "actual gameplay" to get added before then.

I played just enough to make it seem this wasn't some weird review. Look, the game is good. I just made the entire town kill and eat each other by locking them in a cave. You're playing Rimworld, but this time you're the one who plays Randy Random and you just sow chaos and try to exterminate them. I can't recommend this game enough

EDIT: 8 hours deeper, and the game is still good

Steam refuses to post my actual, longer review, so here is the short version.

Ever wanted to play Dungeon Keeper/Overlord but with more depth? Influence the innocent goody-two shoe-s until they turn on eachother, or crush the adventurers in an ambush with your horde of monsters?  looking for a game where you are the evil demon plaguing the peaceful town?
Keep looking, because this is not it.

Don`t get me wrong, the premise is promising, but this is more of a torture sandbox, where you can rain fire on them from the start, just make sure you spend some time following the guy around you turned into a vampire with 0 effort to collect all the manna crystals.

It would have been far more satisfying if you had to put into any actual effort into anything. Say, look for existing vices to tempt a villager into becoming your cultist (Giving the succubus something to actually do for starters.), instead of grabbing them and brainwashing them just like that. Have to direct your cultists or your monsters to steal something so the villagers would get framed with theft, instead of just being able to take and place stuff. If you had to subtly influence anyone, or take some actions to corrupt them, instead of just clicking "afflict with psychopathy" to get your instant serial killer, no need to even add water or boil them a bit.

Instead, it is a resource management and waiting game, where you have one resource to manage, and the waiting is what is supposed to make you vary your methods.

I understand that it is what it is, but if there is ever a Ruinarch 2, consider making it a game of your actions, instead of waiting for the evilness battery to charge so you can fry the ants with the magnifying glass.
developer_response: Thank you for your suggestions! I think these are good ideas that we need to seriously consider not just for Ruinarch 2, but for Ruinarch itself as it goes through Early Access.

review:
I took a chance on this game early on. I like to support innovative ideas in their infancy. I know more often than not I'll be disappointed, but it's still worth doing. I see the potential in this game but the developers have stated their intention to wind down on updates game while they work on their next game.

3 years, 16 "major" updates, and not a whole lot has changed. I was content to watch the updates go by, seeing the game get better slowly, watching the devs get a handle on things, but it seems they thought they were approaching a finished game much faster than I did. 

These developers cannot be trusted with early access. A premature 1.0 is worse than abandoning the game in early access, at least the latter is honest. Whatever Maccima Games works on next, I'll certainly approach with a more critical eye, if I approach at all.

Remember Rimworld? Ruinarch is basically, this time, watching your computer play Rimworld. As it goes about its day, managing the settlement, your job is to scheme, sow demonic chaos and bring doom to what it created and make NPC join your "Dark Side", or whatever you prefer to call it, willingly or not.

• Be a destroyer, and just burn it all with spells and raiding parties, leveling NPC cities and creating a hellish landscape.
• Be seductive, and force settlers into being lustful, distrusting, backstabbing degenerates. Kick back and watch as they go at each other's throats.
• Be a djinn / genie and whisper them promises of power and dark gifts, forcing them in the direction you want: overthrow the current ruler, or abandon their families, homes and country.
• Be a cult-loving lord, as you command your bring NPCs to worship you. Send demons to steal people in the middle of the night (or in the bright of day, if you are so bold), and bring them to your torture chambers. Extracting their essence is one way to go about it, or you might attempt to convert them and then return back to the city, and watch where that leads.
• Be a plague-bringer, corrupting and concocting various poisons and afflictions and let those consume the kingdoms.
• Or combine every method in whatever manner you'd prefer.

How much you like this game is going to depend on your approach:
[olist]
[*]If you are the one to be strongly motivated by objectives set in front of you, then know this: it doesn't take very long to reach the winning condition in the current state of Ruinarch. It's not particularly hard either, if you choose to go full-on and spam raids, fire and an assortment of straight-to-the-point spells. You basically can, at any time, even if you choose to make it harder for yourself through difficulty settings.
[*]If you just want to fool around and break a few sand-castles, then it will either become boring pretty fast, or you will find it a therapeutic binge, at times when you feel like going on a bender. 
[*]If you set your own objectives and like to experiment, then you will find the flow of events in Ruinarch appealing. Just like in Rimworld - only with less micromanagement, mostly focusing on making sense of relations among the NPC, and how to turn them in your favor. Catering to production chains and NPC schedules is non-existent.
[*]If you enjoy the storytelling factor the most, then, even in the current early state, the game is going to enamour you for hours.
[/olist]

1 and 2 - I'd avoid the game for now, unless you feel like putting your money into supporting these indie developers.
3 and 4 - Give it a try, you might be right at home here. Although the game is somewhat raw, there's fun to be had.
If all fails - praise Gaben and hit refund withing 2 hours. I suggest you give it a try and make your own impression

I personally play it like Crusader Kings in reverse. With zero "direct-hit" spells (I don't rain fire, thunder and plague), no petty household squabbles, only giving NPCs natural negative traits (like wroth, laziness, gluttony, promiscuity, but never forcing them to do anything), and, as the society falls apart - converting them into cults and destroying kingdom after kingdom. I also find it very rewarding, when some good characters manage to give a strong fight against these attempts and/or insurmountable odds - and even sometimes prevail. Losing can be very fun here.

And what stories emerge from it!

Just an incredible game ^_^ Opposite of Rimworld, you want to make the lives of the heroes as horrible as can be! Tons of creative ways of wiping out civilizations with your evil powers. I definitely recommend this game and think it's amazing as is, and what it could grow to. . Damn, that's just excellence.

Update to Review!:
Devs have done a lot of beautiful work in optimizing and making the game look better, so definitely happy about all they've done!

1.) Still looking for a few things that I hope will be eventually added! An Example would be to have one of the Demon-types that you choose in the beginning be a Fallen Angel instead. In this way, you would essentially not want to destroy the lands and the people there, but to convert the land and to find the Mother who will bear the 'Anti-Christ' character, a Demon King that begins a new age of darkness, if you can keep them alive long enough.

2.) Sort of a nit-pick, but I would love the Devs to work on simulating multi-story buildings! Towers, Castles, Walls, etc, would be such a great flavor to see more visually. That, and a Hierarchy-System of Nobility to Lowborn castes and what their homes look like, more appropriate traits/skills, and the difference between the rich-Castle roads and their Knights - to poor alleyways of shanty huts, starving, and criminal.

Skip to the TLDR if you don't want to read the in-depth analysis.
Pros:
- It's a god game that lets you play as a villain. Your goal isn't the happiness of your worshipers, but the sadistic destruction of everything that is. In short, there's nothing else quite like it on the market and I love it. This isn't as drab and boring as just dropping a meteor on them (Which you can actually), but it's so much more in depth than that. You can learn about things about the villagers such as love affairs or criminal actions. You can then send your demons to inform their spouses or guards. Subtle things like that are a huge selling point for me. The possibilities of how you can torment these people is hilarious to me.
- You can turn people into vampires and werewolves, raise them from the dead as zombies and skeletons when they die, drop a necronomicon for kicks and wait for somebody to pick it up and become a necromancer, or infuse villagers with the power they need to slay their foes... for a price. You can then just as easily take it away. The other thing about this that adds so much depth is that each villager has their traits and personality. There's something magical about turning a priest into a vampire and watching them try to cope with their affliction. On the other hand, vampire and lycans can embrace their new life style and form their own vampyric / lycanthropic covens and rule their own territory. It's incredible.
- Your powers over life and death, manipulation and subterfuge, pain and joy makes it a very fun sandbox to just.. play with. On that note, there are options that let you customize every playthrough exactly how you want. Want to just have all your powers and just have the world to play with? Great, pick sandbox mode and the "Cheats" that get rid of all the costs. Want a challenging experience? Well you can make it so that every time you get caught making somebody suffer, angels and nature spirits will get pissed off and try to save the world from you.
-Bugs and crashes: Don't exist
-Graphics: Charming and my potato pc can run it and it still is easy on the eyes.

Okay, cons:
- I played for 10 hours and I feel that I have just about experienced all that there is to experience as of 2/3/2022. The release date of the full version is said to be sometime mid to late this year with a whole lot of new additions AND modding has been confirmed after 1.0 later this year, but it is not a feature yet as of today. Buy it with the knowledge that you'll get 10-20 hours of gameplay before you experience everything and get filled with wanting more.
- Audio: They're working on it and next patch it should be much better. Right now it's OKAY, but definitely lacking.

TLDR:
It's a fantastic game and I loved every minute of it. It's early access and it shows, as I only got about 11 hours of enjoyment out of it before I felt like I'd experienced everything. It's constantly being updated and full release is expected later this year, so you won't be waiting too long for more content, especially since modding support is guaranteed shortly after 1.0. I recommend it in its current state if you like being a villain. Truly unique and even without a lot of the content that isn't added in yet, it's a great game to enjoy on a weekend with a bottle of booze. Cheers~

Bluntly unfinished, theyve spent the last year at least leading up to 1.0 adding nothing of real significance just reshuffling what was already there into new configurations. Very sad to see they shoved it out the door like this.

This is a software toy, so please bear that in mind.  For me, I grew up on the old Maxis games, before EA bought them out.  Those were software toys.  Even The Sims was designed that way.  If you're wanting a typical "game," and you're thinking of RTS or something, sure, I can see why you might be disappointed, but the "toy," in this case--not a city simulator or an ant colony simulator, nope--  The toy, in Ruinarch, is a Fuck Around With Everyone toy.  Again-- not a game.  A toy.

All humans seem to have this in-born desire to fuck with things smaller than them.  This can manifest in some horrendous ways.  Or, you can just play this! :D  Normally, I'd have some guilt and cognitive dissonance, but Ruinarch does a good job de-personalizing it.  You're not "toying" by experiencing the icky emotions that come with messing with realistic people.  You're toying by playing with fake beings programmed to act in people-like ways and seeing how they react.

You've done it, too, right?  You save your game, then just go nutty to see what happens, try out weird dialog choices just to see what the characters do, how the designers and AI account for it (or not)...  Except, I usually feel guilty, reload, etc.

Well, now there is a whole game built around it.  Just mess with people til your heart's content, set the parameters, and go for it, guilt-free and "That's what you get for stepping out of line!" gameover-free!

Don't let the reviews scare you away.  If what I wrote sounds fun, you will enjoy this.  I nearly passed (I'm broke) after reading reviews, but I'm so glad I went for it.  I've been having a blast just messing with the first type of demon you can play, and I haven't even tried out the others yet.  I'm also looking forward to when they implement some broader progression or campaign, or scenarios, but I don't even need it.  It's great fun to start from scratch, build up my demonhood, and mess with people all the way.  By the time you build your powers up, you can go way overboard and cause total cause, or you can be more measured and drag it out.

I need to have a life, but I just want to keep playing.  And now I am a little sad, because instead of writing this, I could have been playing.

PS  I'm Autistic, and while all Autists are different, I find this one extremely pleasingly stimmy.  Your experience may vary, or you too may find it to be the case.

In its current state, I'm unconvinced. The game has a strong foundation and some exciting mechanics, but there are areas that need to be improved upon.

One of the most interesting mechanics in Ruinarch is the afflictions system, which can trigger different outcomes based on the player's actions. However, at times, it can take a while to see the result of triggering certain afflictions. Similarly, while the game has a lot of promise, some features feel bare-bones, and the usefulness of cultists could be improved, such as having them serve a larger purpose in creating hostile tension amongst their fellow villagers and spreading the player's influence by converting other villagers.

While Ruinarch has some shortcomings, it has excellent replayability due to the nature of the villagers and special abilities chosen, making each playthrough feel different. However, the game could benefit from more pushback from the villagers or a mechanic where non-cultists somehow find out about the player's presence and have mini-wars erupt between the two factions.

Overall, Ruinarch has a solid concept and promises more content in the future, but in its present state I feel it falls well short of what it aspires to be. It is a game worth keeping an eye on, especially for fans of colony sims and games similar to Dungeon Keeper. It having already gone to 1.0 does make me wonder why it didn't sit in the oven a bit longer. As I said before -- in its current state, I'm unconvinced.

Ruinarch is a sinfully delightful idea in the middle of its execution. It is playable and enjoyable already, but the vision that the designers have for it is grand and still very much in-progress. Very notable however, is they are dedicated to putting in the work that will make this vision, no matter how lofty, come to fruition.

From the very beginning, there are secrets, interactions, and features that are quite honestly, nothing short of inspired. Whatever simplicity you think would exist in a 'destroy X villagers' Game, the developers have embraced with one hand while in the shadows they work to create a system capable of satisfying even the most twisted of imaginations.

Envision, if you will, a middle-of-the-line Village Simulator, the pride and joy of a sibling perhaps, and one day they log in to tend to their crops and interpersonal relationships to find that their game has been modded with a second-player overhaul allowing you to poison those crops, compel virtual husbands to abandon the warmth of their beds for the sinful attentions of the town's most illustrious of faith, and control the very dangerous chaotic elements that used to be fair only by the cruel indifference of randomness. 

This game is as simple as "casting lightning on John while he is standing in a puddle" and as complex as "relentlessly torment his newly insomniac widow with whispered secrets until she begins to poison the town; herself". 

I bought this game on a whim after seeing one of those early-access streamers quite simply fail to achieve chaos in the inventive ways that I immediately recognized were possible. I am now invested in the very clearly vast future of this project. A glimpse of their roadmap reveals that this is only the beginning, and the three major updates to the game available at the time of this review each bettered the game in clear, monumental ways. 

If today the limitations seem choking, or the experience feels too unpolished to thoroughly enjoy, I have no doubt that you can return in a few months to see the already enjoyable game brought to even new heights and breadth.

Did you ever want to make the life of your D&D players painful?
Did you ever want to let out your tad sadistic fantasies and make them come to fruition?
Do you hate that "one person" and do you want to make him a brainless cultist?
Do you dream of destroying those tree hugging pointy ears, burn down their forests and re-enact the burning of the world tree?

Well look no further because this is the game for you!

Change peasants into zombies, laugh at the misery of those plebeians that called you a madman!

Want to destroy a marriage by changing one into a vampire or burn them in front of their significant other?

Fire and magic are your friends!

Want to be the one that brings the FUN to them, like when you have FUN playing Dwarf Fortress? (Giant golems)

Drop some horrors onto them, and enjoy the mayhem!

Want to throw a stone at that idiot that told you that the planet was round?

Sticks and stones might not break his bones but your meteor will surely do the trick!

Did you ever want to make gossiping mothers?

Spread some rumors and watch the puny mortals fight with each other for your amusement!

May your great deeds be blessed and may you have fun setting the universe on fire! (Gloryhammer pun intended)

Graphics may look old but the gameplay sure isn't, just like all those newborns that are ready to be brainwashed!

It's basically a perfect Daniel simulator! (look up the definition on Urban dictionary, the one submitted on April 22, 2008 by aaaaghhh)


EDIT: 
More races will be added in the future. The game has 3 building alongside your main building with their own functionality, not many monsters or people types.

There is already a good amount of affliction,s traits and spells in the game, but it will be expanded like the developers said.

I am playing on custom right now and it is a lot of fun while watching or talking with someone, though it might get repetitive, so if you are not completely sure if you want this game look up some game-play, so that you won't get disappointed or if you want to wait until this game is a bit more fleshed out (pun intended).

One review sold me - Rimworld but you are Randy
A ton of fun albeit a bit easy, but I love the flexibility and dark humor of crafting plans to dispatch villagers
I would like to see more and harder challenges, balancing of powers/abilities (looking at you zombies) and more buildings. Also villager events, like priests or templars that can consecrate/purge the evil limiting options and requiring actual defense
Overall 11/10 - thank you!

This game is at best a bare bones sandbox. It's not that there are no good ideas here, in fact I think it has great potential, but it is held back by many issues.

The first is that the AI sucks. Townsfolk will just wander into the wild and get killed by monsters sometimes. I was playing the Lich, and my necromancers wandered into sight of the town whilst trekking towards some random cave. A cultist I had just walked into a monster den and died, for seemingly no reason. This completely destroys the social relations system in the game. You'll end up with towns full of people have 0 relationship points with each other because all the people they actually liked are dead.

Base building is also kind of awful. You can't move your portal, and humans can just build right next to it randomly while expanding, or wander by it while adventuring. There's no defence structures, you just have monsters that can guard your structures. Oh, I guess there are walls? I don't know, I was hoping for a little more.

Finally, there's no overarching goal. There's a list of tasks, and you just have to finish a certain number of them to win. The problem is, none of them feel that connected. Cool, I killed an elf, infected 10 people with plague, summoned a bone golem and made some one a serial killer. None of that feels like a lich building towards a master plan, it feels like a cartoon villain messing with people.

There is some functionality here, so it's not the worst game in the world. If you find fun by tinkering with a sandbox, then maybe you can enjoy something here. However, there are many other games out there with more polish and content than this one.

27 hours in, and I've had a lot of fun moments. The game isn't bad by any means. That said, it's a bit overpriced at the moment.
As an RTS game, it feels lacking due to how carefully you need to play and how limited your control over the AI, yours OR the villagers, is. This is not helped by the AI not being particularly clever. The early game is grindy and slow and the late game always goes the same.
As more of a sandbox experience, however, I still don't feel quite in-control enough to really enjoy picking on the little villagers. Sure, I could just go Ravager, turn on infinite mana, and then nuke them from orbit, but then that'd call down angels and shit and it's just not worth it.

I'm going to go look for a god game that allows for more options to nurture characters.

How many times have you read the words "abandoned" and "developer disappeared"? Well, here's another one. 
They updated it on May 30th and then vanished. 

Still reading? Didn't deter you? You just need that monstrous fix of playing a powerful villain like I did? 
Ok, let's talk about the actual game:
After a couple of years of having this game, since Early Access, I feel like I've given Ruinarch enough chances. 

Here's the short of it: 
It's not what it says on the tin. 
It severely lacks content, features and the depth you would expect from a game that tries to mix Rimworld with Dungeon Keeper. 
The only challenge in this game is not getting bored halfway through a playthrough. 
It still feels like Early Access and in fact, it's gotten WORSE since Early Access.

Unless the devs come back from the cold and bring with them some massive update, Ruinarch was an interesting concept that never made it to full game status and never delivered on its potential or promises.

Very unfortunate, as playing evil demonic overlords is a very underrepresented genre. 
My advice? Look up the original Dungeon Keeper. It's old but it's still the best at what it did.

My expectations where for a basic Rimworld game, where you kinda play god and you create cascading events across a world, like the ones you normally suffer through randomly in Rimworld.

What I played was... not any of that.



[list][*]Theres no overworld, its all contained on a very small map (even on its largest setting).

[*]Theres no cascading events like in Rimworld, not even really any events at all.

All I ever saw was NPCs die in various ways, nothing else really happens to them.

They also dont really interact with each other outside of killing each other, and different factions (at least the 3 I tried, humans, vampires and werewolves) dont seem to interact with each other at all (but maybe thats because in the game those 3 are all human and vampirism and lycanthropy are negative traits, nfi, but thats a pretty bog standard fantasy setting if you ask me), but even if they did I dont expect them to do anything more than kill each other.

[*]Your influence is marginal at best... you cant really influence NPCs outside of working to get them killed, because thats all that can happen to them at the end of the day.

No inducing a love affair between two rival villages to cause conflict, no, you (seemingly pointlessly) lower opinion here, you spread some plague there, drop spells around the place and revive a corpse occasionally.

[*]Each time you unlock a new spell you think of the amazing possibilities... that dont exist... like poisoning a well...

Will the townies go looking for herbs to get rid of the poisoning or will they seek outside help or will they source water from a nearby lake and brave the wilderness, no, it just makes them a little sick which lowers their HP and after some time the poison goes away.

Every spell is just like that, "Oh this is cool! Oh wait, no its not...".

[*]I really dont get the point of having a base... it would be one thing if it acted like Dungeon Keeper or Evil Genius where you lure people into it so you can gain something from defeating them within, or if you were building your own evil empire to conquer the world like some classic fantasy setting featuring your typical demon lord, but no.

Your base is just some anchor to punish you when youre being too mean to the NPCs, and you defend it like some TD game.

Its all so basic that every building could just be a menu and you wouldnt be missing out on anything.

[*]Resource gathering, SUCKS! Oh my god does it blow hard...

You lose any orbs you dont pick up so you have to focus on griefing a particular NPC, one at a time, to generate resources and then pick them up manually by hovering over them.

Not that the resource grind is particular hard or has any sort of tier system or chain, it just makes you focus on your boring, flaccid abilities that let you interact with townies.

The entire system is just reinforcing this huge weak sole core element of the game.
[/list]


There are SO MANY good games to take inspiration from, theres Rimworld, Majesty, Evil Genius, Dungeon Keeper, Settlers, and every similar game that came before or after that show exactly how certain things should be done, but instead it seems like Ruinarch is trying to blaze its own trail where trails already exist.

Ruinarch doesnt give you the feeling of being an evil mastermind like in Evil Genius or Dungeon Keeper... it doesnt have the economy chains of Settlers... it doesnt have the cascading events of Rimworld... It doesnt have the level of interaction that Black & White has... it doesnt make you feel like an arch evil conquering the lands like in Dominions...

The problem is one of three things... either the game is no where near ready to be played, in which case dont hold your breath because most games that go into EA half baked never come out, or the developers dont know what they are trying to make, or lastly the games just plain boring... pick one.

This game has a unique concept unlike any other I have seen. Basically, you play as an evil overlord which can be one of the three classes: puppetmaster, lich and ravager. All of the classes have different abilities and capabilities. Your ultimate goal as an evil overlord is to wreck havoc on all factions that are not allied with you.

When I bought the game, I thought that I would be able to spend many hours playing the game and it turned out to be the opposite. The amount of enjoyment that can be earned is very limited. I played all of the three classes and I experienced almost the same thing every playthrough: There is almost no challenge whatsoever, the villagers are always wandering, doing nothing to interrupt you from achieving your goal, which is the opposite of what is supposed to happen and it so happens that even if you don't try to eliminate the villagers, they happen to kill each other, which of course ruins the goal of the game.

In its current state, I would not recommend this game, as the playtime you will get from the game does not match the price. Perhaps, the game will change in the future and will allow more replayability, but until then, I don't think I will be playing this game anymore.

edit: Wow, really? The old portraits had so much SOUL and a COHESIVE ARTSTYLE to them and now you're forcing me to look at an AI art gallery. This is BS, i hate all the idiots who want AI art to replace human art. I WOULD RATHER HAVE AN INCOMPLETE SET OF PORTRAITS FROM A REAL ARTIST. I would change my review to negative but then i'd lose the stats on it.

The concept is fantastic but in practice this is like the skeleton of a game, as you might expect from early access. There's not enough quirks for the NPCs to have, there's not enough spells, not enough afflictions, each overlord's skillset is poorly conceptualized and not very thematic either. You can play maybe 4 hours and see everything there is to see, which is kind of bad considering there's no random map generator in the game yet. You can save your game now, which you couldn't on release, but the lack of content is still there and needs some more work. Wait a few months and see how it shapes up.

CORRECTION: I originally thought this game was by the same developers behind Academia  School Simulator, a good game by its own right but woefully behind its release schedule. Ruinarch's developers have corrected me so I update this review.
developer_response:
Hi! All valid points. But to clarify one point, Squeaky Wheel published this game, and Maccima is the developer. We are two separate developers working on separate early Access games independently, so our attentions are not divided in any way whatsoever. 

That said, all your issues are valid, and we hope you'll give the game another shot in the future!

review:
It's not "finished", in a sense of the word. Some aspects of the game are waaay overscoped, while others are totally barebones and it's not [i] really [/i] functional. 

The villagers have all these complex relationship and economy mechanics, which affect what you do hardly at all. The game is about bringing ruin to the village, supposedly, but the systems aren't very functional and the villagers are liable to bring ruin to themselves without you doing much of anything. Your abilities are sometimes way too powerful, and other times pointlessly weak.

Beyond that, the villagers don't respond to your actions meaningfully. The game randomly increments a counter when you take certain aggressive actions and when it gets high enough, sends a wave of fairly easy angel enemies to attack your base... area... thing, and that's it. The villagers themselves barely care at all.

Sadly, the scope was way too big and the developers couldn't finish work on it, since it wasn't performing that well. Now they're working on a new game, which is nothing like this one and has a far more realistic scope. Oh well...

You could go on about all the half-working systems and how badly it all plays for ages, but at the end of the day I did spend a fair while on it back when I played it, so I can't pretend it's valueless. Just keep your expectations fairly in-check, and don't expect it to be a 'game', it's really more of a simulator.

The game had a lot of promise, its such a shame to see it go to waste. They were going to add a lot more races like dwarves and I think faeries too. It's had some questionable UI changes throughout it's time in early access and now the implementation of A.I art replacing all the original good artwork, its weird to see stuff like that regress. Hell, they seemed to even forgotten or scrapped the small missions you were able to do, like having a village being assailed by undead or dealing with a pesky elven village who are all unnaturally protected.

Ruinarch is interesting and fun, but too sparse on content to recommend at this point. I unlocked all the scenarios in about 10 hours of play and I didn't find a lot of challenge. For example, I accidentally set my portal in the second scenario directly beside the town yet still managed to wipe them all out. 

Easy to imagine the game coming together nicely, but it's not quite worth $20 presently.

I really like the core concept of the game, but it has a long way to go. Primarily, visual and information clarity would be #1 on my list of gripes, given just how busy everything is and how visually everything just kind of blends together. Graphically everything is simply too flat, and nothing important 'pops'. Tracking the actions of every villager seems really un-necassary and leads to a lot of useless information being thrown at you crowding out the important stuff. A lot of fairly important bits of game mechanic knowledge and things like resources are fairly un-intuatively buried in the UI, which looks and feels dated in terms of design. 

By far the most interesting idea here is manipulating individual villagers and general conditions to initiate the collapse of a community indirectly. I think that's the area that the game needs to focus on going foward, and could probably do with dropping or back-seating a lot of the more 'direct' methods, or gating them behind using cultists to initiate them. Tempting villagers with faustian bargains, recruiting them to actively aid you in a lovecraftian sort of way would definately give this game a very unique niche and identity.

While it is clearly still a work in progress, this game is currently a lot of fun.  It plays like a sort of reverse Rimworld crossed with Dungeon Keeper.  A settlement of NPC settlers builds and grows a town, and you, more or less, either actively try to destroy them or just mess with them while you upgrade your main building to "Ruinarch" (sort of a tech victory).

The best part for me is the little stories that play out.  Here's an example from the game I'm working on right now:

Uther was a crafter in the village.  He was lazy by nature, so I afflicted him with gluttony and narcolepsy as well.  This made him the most obnoxious person in the village.  Eventually, I went to check up on him, and see he'd left it entirely, living as a vagrant in the woods.

Uther had also picked up, at some point, a necronomicon I had left lying around.  So, out of curiosity, I sent out some minions to nab him.  I threw him in my prison, and left him there for some time.  Eventually, I decided to brainwash him into a cultist.  When it was done, he marched out, necronomicon in hand, and started building an undead horde in a nearby temple, raising the previous occupants (already corpses) immediately.

Well, I couldn't have him suffering from nacrolepsy and gluttony, so I cured those (an ability I had to upgrade as it turned out Uther was naturally magic resistant and I had to overcome that), and he merrily spent his days studying the necronomicon and raising the dead.  Huzzah!

Fast forward a bit, and I find a wolf corpse.  I thought I'd just drop it off at Uther's temple, but when I zoom over there, I see him all alone, no undead minions at all.  I was somewhat disappointed, thinking the skeletons had a time limit or some such, and then... alerts started blaring about an attack on the village.  The cheeky idiot had sent his entire undead horde to attack the village, unprompted.

It was a bloodbath.  Better than half the town was wiped out.  The empty cemetery was suddenly full.  I was assessing the damage, and then I got a notification.  Uther, now that he had a cemetery to work with, had just strolled out and started raising all the recently dead in it, and immediately began attacking the town again.  I actually spawned in a migration because I had some project NPCs in the town I didn't want to see wiped out, and together, they overcame the necromancer, slaying Uther and putting an end to his undead army.

In a happy coincidence, a vampire I had created became the new town leader.  Not that being a vampire gave them any allegiance to me or Uther for that matter (in fact, another vampire I'd made was overwhelmed, attempted to become a bat and flee, but was then run down by Uther's forces.)

So, fun little sandbox game.  Looking forward to what they'll do next!

I actually liked this game. it was fun, and I enjoyed playing and scheming. Extremely disappointing to see the game abandoned, and the devs using ugly AI images that the game definitely did NOT need. DO NOT BUY

I've played this game for 6 hours and I hate single player games...

However, picture this:

I start a game, yay undead, kinda slow, getting bored, realize "Oh hey, I can level up my portal and get more shit"
Upgrade portal, get new stuff, keep upgrading, keep getting new stuff
Mess with villagers
Villagers going crazy

When did a boar join a village?
