Leader leaves faction to become necromancer because lol
Check later
Boar is leader

Wait what? A pig is leading a human kingdom of 9
10/10, would recommend

Ratman married the widowed ex-leaders wife
Wife dies of disease from ratman
+1 to recommend, 11/10

As all other reviews say: the game's amusing for a couple hours, and then it goes downhill fast.

This was an awesome idea of a "Reverse Rimworld where YOU are the Storyteller", be Randy Random!
Except things needed more polish and more time in the oven before calling it a 1.0. It still feels largely some form of demo, with the actual cool features falling short of feeling complete, and no real campaign to give a feeling of progression or purpose. 
A big shame really, as this would've otherwise become one of the niche indie gems to scratch a very particular itch.

I'll give it a thumbs up cos I hope it keeps going, but make no mistake the game is a mess right now. There is no sense of balance to anything that happens or is done by the player. The game feels like a sandbox where you do things for novelty rather than strategy - if that's the aim of the devs, to be a "storyteller"-game rather than a game-game, then there are other changes to make instead.

Your actions, and even random events, have way too unpredictable an impact. All the afflictions and schemes you impose are overshadowed by random occurrences. Or you could just lightning-bolt the whole village and laugh at the weak angels that come to raid your base, ending the game quickly and without any real challenge.

I think the game would work better if they did away with the demonic base-building (it's bad and bare-bones anyway) and instead focused on the cultist aspect with the player building their own cult from the villagers on the map. More depth to this aspect of gameplay would really open the game up. The game needs something to build and create be the focus of the gameplay, as the gaming equivalent of kicking sandcastles is going to get old real quick. I found myself ignoring the uninspired (and way too easy) map objectives and just trying to build successful cult villages, and not using any overly offensive abilities either. 

Rind now it's kind of a rough novelty wherein you can waste some hours creating a handful of amusing situations, but it has potential so I don't regret the purchase and hope for more from this game in the future.

Every patch moves this game away from the experience I originally bought : kill and ruin the lives of innocent villagers.

The game was fundamentally reworked so that you have to walk on eggshells to not kill anyone (instead of not draw too much attention too quickly). Fun levels plummeted with that simple game design change.

That is the risk of Early Access.

A fun game that is definitely in early access.

Edited: 7/30/2021. The game has been significantly changed since I posted my first review, and its' unfortunately not all for the better.

The Good: 
- Villagers have been overhauled significantly, and their interactions with each other and the world are much more interesting. They earn money to buy resources, have more robust individual trades, craft items, and all sorts of other fun stuff.

- The way monsters are used has been vastly improved. Rather than just spawning them next to a villager and letting them have at it, they are formed into parties, led by one of your demons, to accomplish a specific purpose. Marauder parties from the marauder building break stuff and attack villagers to sow chaos, snatching parties kidnap villagers or monsters for imprisonment, and defense parties protect your base full-time. It's a bit monotonous to reform the parties repeatedly if you make heavy use of them, but this alone has made Ravager a lot more fun.

- The map is now all connected instead of a bunch of separate squares. That's pretty damn nice. The new way the building are set up is great also.

The Bad:
- This movement towards more structure has unfortunately come at the expense of the customisable sandbox aspect that made this game so interesting to me. All loadout customization has been removed; you pick your archetype, are given a predetermined set of powers/buildings, and then unlock new ones by upgrading your portal over time. In practice, this forces you to play a specific way that the devs want you to, at the expense of replayability and fun. Want to use plagues and build up lethality over time? Too bad, you have to get your portal to level 5 to even start using plagues, and at that point you could have enough points to instantly make it kill everyone and overrun the world with zombies at the click of the mouse. It feels unsatisfying and bad, and leads us to our final point:

The Ugly:
- The victory conditions have changed to be all about upgrading the portals. Killing all the villagers no longer ends the game, which is almost good as by the time you reach the final portal level (8) you can (and probably did) overrun the world 10 times over. I just played a game as the Lich where my Zombie plague wiped out everybody. Zombies are walking the empty streets, the wilderness, everywhere, but the game isn't over, because my portal is only level 5. I'm reduced to spamming "Incite Migration" to summon new villagers (who instantly get killed by a horde of zombies) to try and squeeze out a few more points to try and upgrade the portal. Again, this is another consequence of the devs trying to force the players to play the way they want us to, which apparently is to just not kill villagers. I feel like the a name change to "Annoyarch" would better reflect the gameplay direction at this point.

Original Review:
Pros:
-Very open ended. Your objective is to kill or subvert every villager, any way you can, and that's it. There's no structure or story built for you, you build your own. More linear gamers will not enjoy this, but I do.
-Offers a variety of different playstyles, with some flexibility offered by each. Subvert villagers to worship you, prey on their flaws to turn them against each other, or incite wars between factions as the Puppetmaster. Craft a deadly plague, afflict villagers with Lycanthropy or Vampirism to sow discord, or raise legions of the undead as the Lich. Finally, gather armies of monsters, rain destruction with powerful spells, or summon powerful demons as the Ravager.

Cons: 
-Feels incomplete, which is of course going to happen in an early access title but still needs to be said. Certain areas of the game feel like they could go much further, like customizing your cult and cultist. Right now they are basically just regular villagers who generate mana for you sometimes. I'd like to be able to give them dark boons to customize them, like perhaps plague immunity for a playthrough focused on plagues.

-Balance is strange. By far the easiest and most reliable is the Lich using the Bio Lab to create plagues. It feels like Plague Inc. lite- increase infectivity, then symptoms, then death and you're golden. Create a friendly necromancer and activate the zombie plague and sit back until you win. On the other hand, using the Puppetmaster feels overly difficult. There are percentage chances to convert cultists based on how unhappy they are or if they have the evil or treacherous trait, which means even if you put in a painful amount of work setting them up your brainwash can still fail and leave you with nothing to show for it. Even when you finally succeed in a brainwash, they can be exposed as a cultist and killed or eaten by a monster and force you to start the process over. Coupled with the aforementioned lack of depth to cults can make Puppetmaster extremely frustrating.

Conclusion:
I personally think this game is overpriced for what it currently offers- this feels like more of a $15 game than a $25 game. That said, if you want to create your own stories as a scheming demon looking to destroy the world, this is the place to find it.

First game i played puppeteer and first thing i did was kidnap and brainwash a woman thinking nothing of it but turned out she was a Village Leader with a Psychopath trait that kills only males, She proceeds to convert every woman secretly to worship me and become like her a Psychopath that kills only males...........So i brainwashed a lady named Karen and she started a cult of Karen's hellbent on killing every single male.

Also her first kill was her husband.

This one hurts.

I was a great game, fairly "early on" into the development it had a fun, if unfinished progression. Like many reviews say its a similar but "opposite" style of game-play to rimworld. You pick one of 3 evil/god styles and go from there.
About 2 years ago they completely reworked the progression and removed a bunch of features and now its just stale.
Dev's completely abandoned it about a year ago from the time this review has been posted.

It was fun, and awhile back I can say i would have recommended it wholeheartedly. But these days, I cant really say I do, not for even half price. Maybe if its on sale for 2 bucks but, its just not really worth it to me anymore.

If I was able to un-update the game to right before the re-work, id even say buy that. But in its current state, with no signs of any sort of future development, there really isn't a price I'd justify for it.

RIP Ruinarch, you were great, and had so much more potential.

I got this a few years ago, after seeing a play through. The game was interesting and I looked forward to what would be added next. Sadly, not much was added in the time since purchase. Humans and elves were the only factions to deal with then and are so now. There are a few new monsters and spells but not much in the way adding further depth to the game. There are new buildings, but nothing that really change how the game is played. The campaign was removed as well as the multi-stage large maps. The concept is/was great, but it feels far too shallow.

This isn't really worth the time or money, it was a great idea that was unfinished.

After a few rounds, out of curiosity, I loaded a game, turned the speed up, and did nothing. It devolved into complete chaos on its own, and rather quickly.
If you actually use your abilities it becomes such a convoluted mess that you have no idea what happening, and not the fun kind of "everything becomes chaos". Carefully influenced characters can go off the rails because a new migrant didnt like them, and half the time they just end up killing each other to the point your cooldowns feel wasted. By the time you've unlocked the more fun abilities, there's just not much point because eveyone is already having 73 things happening.  
 It sounded fun, but in execution your actions just end up being kind of meaningless.

I bought this game over 2 years ago when it was in very early access. I remember putting it down because it was very simplistic without much to do. I hadn't played since 1.0 so I figured now was the time. Let's see how far this game has come.

The sad truth is Ruinarch hasn't come far at all in any way that I've noticed. I just played for 20 minutes and it's just as I remember. Cast a couple of spells, build a structure or two, cast more spells, build more structures, repeat this process until the people are dead. There's no struggle. It's definitely a god game but you're quickly omnipotent.

This could be helped if there were rival gods to struggle against. As it stands, a 20 minute run feels unfulfilling and the game seems so underwhelming considering how good the concept is and the price. It's a $10 game at best. Sorry but that's the truth.

I voted "not recommend" due to it's current state. The game is fun the first hour or two, but after that you have seen it all. It still needs a ton of work.
It's fun to turn someone into a Necromancer or a Wolf and watch them slaughter other villagers, but the tasks involved in doing this compared to how long you need to wait for the result is imbalanced. 

Then their is some wierd stuff that doesn;t make sense: 
Tristran was a peasant turned Necromancer who raised an army of the dead at the nearby graveyard. Evelyn who is Tristan's wife, last seen Tristan as a had working farmer. She goes for a stroll and all of a sudden comes across her husband who is now a black cloaked, skeleton army commanding necromancer on his way to attack the village. 
Before he continues, they have a nice chat together and Tristan reputation in Evelyn's eyes even increases. Apparantly is doesn't matter Evelyn (who is not evil) one bit that her hubby changed from a hardworking farmer to a genocidal skeleton raising Necromancer overnight. He then wents off killing the whole village and the skeleton finally attack Evelyn and kill her. Makes sense right?

Some stuff is not even fun. Let's take vampirism. I had the thought of turning a villager into vampire through some ritual, let it loose on the village where it will sucks people dry and create a cabal of vampires. 
In game you click a villager, click "afflict" and click vampirism. That's it, the villager is now a vampire. What the vampire does now is drink people's blood when they sleep and that's it. As long as noone sees it, nothing "bad" happens. The person being sucked dry is lethargic for a while and that's it. Not cabal of vampires being created etc. Some of this sounds logical ofc, but that does not make it fun gameplay and their is hardly any storytelling involved.

There is some fun stuff though, like the zombie virus. You inflict someone with the virus, although through the same lame mechanic as vamprism, and then wait until it dies. Then it will turn into a zombie at night and attack other people. They in turn can be infected and so it spreads and it spreads. While this is fun to look at once, gameplay wise it's still lame. With a bit of luck, you wipe out the entire village (thus winning the game) with 3 clicks....

I know this is an early access, but the game is far from being a real game. It's more like a tech demo where you can use some spells, erect some "buildings" and manipulate some stuff, then an actual game.

I hope that the developer will turn this into an actual fun game. Let's say the story teller side of a Rimworld. But for now it's far from it, especially considering it's price.

At first, I thought this was the "Devil Simulator" I've always wanted. But the longer I played, the more frustrated I became with this game. I'll start with the Good things, follow up with the Bad, and leave you with my suggestions for improvement.

The Good

-The AI is tremendously well made, just as advertised. Each NPC has different traits, different relationships with other NPC's, and adjusts it's behavior over time as it interacts with the other NPC's. The level of care and attention that went into this facet of the game is dumbfounding, considering this is, at it's core, "God Simulator meets Sim City". As an example, two NPC's might begin a game with a friendly relationship, but one of them dislikes music and the other loves to sing. If they cross paths frequently enough while the one is singing, the relationship deteriorates into apathy, then annoyance, then hatred. And then things might get violent.

-The process of turning a villager into your loyal cultist is simple, but it takes time. You have to break the victim before you can control their minds, through torture, starvation, and the anguish of turning all their friends and family against them are needed to ensure success. And if that still doesn't work, just keep doing it until they snap and go on a frenzied killing spree. Even if you fail to make them a follower, you can still utterly break their mind. :)

-The Biolab and Plague mechanics are... shockingly satisfying. It's almost on par with the famous game, "Plague Inc.". The execution is simple: infect a few people, spend disease points, infect more people, spend more disease points, infect all the people, spend more disease points, rupture their internal organs and turn them into zombies, the works. This is easily the most fun and entertaining thing about this game, second only to the AI interactions.

Unfortunately, this is the end of the good. Now, onto the Bad. 

The Bad

-TRITON. FUCKING. TRITON. THAT GODDAMNED FISH MAN IS THE BANE OF MY EXISTENCE. WHY IS HE THERE. EVERY TIME, EVERY FUCKING TIME HE POPPED OUT OF THE WATER, IT WAS LIKE THE GAME THROWING SALT IN MY FUCKING EYES AND MOCKING ME. HE COMES OUT OF NOWHERE, MURDERS A RANDOM PASSERBY, AND VANISHES UNTIL HE DECIDES TO MURDER AGAIN. KILLING HIM ISN'T POSSIBLE. CAPTURING HIM ISN'T POSSIBLE. AND GETTING EVERYONE TO STAY THE HELL AWAY FROM HIS POND IS ***VERY*** NOT POSSIBLE, FOR REASONS I WILL EXPLAIN BELOW.
Fixed: I haven't seen that stupid fish man for a long time now. I assume the devs got rid of him :)

-Your ability to command the cultists under your control can be... tenuous. Despite being enslaved to your will, they will still prioritize their own health and well-being over obeying you, meaning if you command your starving cultist to abduct a villager at a critical moment, it *might* obey you, only to stop halfway through the task to go eat something. Assuming, of course, that it obeys you at all. There were many, MANY times when I needed a cultist to perform an abduction, or convert another villager at a VERY specific moment, but instead of rushing to obey, they almost always ignored my commands until it was far too late to get the intended result. 
Fixed: Cultists now obey my orders without hesitation, and abductions have been outsourced to your minor demons, so you don't have to risk your precious followers anymore

-Creating a horde of monsters and demons is an excellent idea for a game like this, but the actual execution is just painful. I don't have much to say about the monsters, other than it's somewhat frustrating to be limited to whatever happens to be roaming the island; If there are no wild trolls in your map, you won't be able to unleash trolls on the villagers. As for the demons, the developers completely dropped the ball. You are limited to 1 of each demon type at a time. If you want to invade the world with a small army of 38 demons, you will be disappointed. I would understand it if they were incredibly powerful, but they aren't. Some of them don't even fight, they just make people sad. 
FIXED: Monsters and demons now deploy in groups, you are now able to train your own monsters that aren't native to the world map, and you can field a LOT more of them at once

-I spent most of my time playing as the Puppetmaster style, and I have to say, the description is completely wrong. "Pull the strings from behind the curtain and let the world devolve into chaos without lifting a finger." I lifted many fingers. I did nothing BUT lift fingers. The set of powers you're given makes it clear: "You are meant to manipulate, control, and command others" But the mechanics of the game make that next to impossible to do in a way that's fun. Honestly, this is mostly because of my next complaint.
FIXED: Now that I don't have to kill all the villagers to win, playing as the puppetmaster is a lot more fun. I'm finally able to just sit back, relax, and watch the villagers tear each other apart.

-To the developers, I say this: THE COOLDOWNS. SERVE. NO. PURPOSE. All they do is create an arbitrary waiting time on abilities that don't need them. For instance, consider the "Seize Villager" ability. Every time it is used, two things happen: the 300 second countdown begins, and the threat level goes up. the "Seize Villager" ability, and others like it, do not NEED a timed cooldown, because the game's Threat Level ALREADY SERVES AS A COOLDOWN. Using the natural time limit imposed by the threat of Divine Judgement makes the game far more fun and challenging than putting these fucking timers on everything.
FIXED: The cooldowns are still there, but Divine Retribution has been redone It's no longer tied to the *number* of actions you take. Instead, it's tied to how many people you kill, and how much stuff you destroy. In other words, if what you do doesn't directly kill someone, the angels aren't coming. :) 

and now, onto my suggestions:

-Although independent Vampire/Werewolf/Cultist kingdoms are an excellent idea, they don't really add anything to the game other than a few alterations to what they consider a "crime". In my opinion, the game would benefit from giving each of the members of these deviant societies some kind of truly unique, noticeable behavior that embodies what they belong to: Perhaps the Vampire kingdoms should routinely abduct villagers and keep them in "feeding cages" for food. Perhaps the cultists should send out raiding parties, specifically to abduct and convert other villagers, without me ordering them to. Food for thought.

-A big part of what makes the Biolab/Plague and the AI Interactions so fun is Progression. Each produces distinct, dramatic results as time goes on: a sick rat causes rampant vomitting and seizures, and eventually a zombie apocalypse. A negative relationship means spreading rumours, stealing, cuckolding (yes that's in this game), and eventually fistfights, poisoning, and murder. That kind of progression does not really exist anywhere else in the game. There's nothing special about having 20 skeletons, It's just 1 skeleton, copied 19 times. The demons, the cultists, the monsters, the base buildings, all are excellent ideas, but they never become anything... more. What you see, is what you get. 
FIXED: Just about everything has a progression system in place now. :)

In conclusion, This game is really fun. It takes patience, but once you get the ball of evil rolling, it doesn't stop. It just gets bigger and more evil, and you get to watch all the mayhem it causes.

!Ruinarch is discontinued and is clearly not finished!
Hence I will not recommend buying it as of this moment.

Ruinarch is a small fun indie game that is basically an opposite colony sim. The idea of the game is to slowly corrupt the towns around you until they fall in disarray. From just chucking meteors on someones house, to poisoning crops, to establishing murderous cults, to spreading vampirism or lycanthropy. You are provided with multiple options to slowly make people incredibly miserable. Which then in turn fuels your ways to fulfill your elaborate plots.

I had a lot of fun playing this game. But after 7 hours of playtime I have seem to have run out of content to explore. For the price of this game you can buy multiple other games with more content and developers that didn't abandon it. It is such a shame that the developers abandoned this game since its gimmick is unique and a lot of fun. 

Overall I would give this game a 3/10 due to its high price, low content and no future development.

It has the skeleton of a great game, but never quite achieved that potential so it just lives in perpetual mediocrity. If it's on sale, the game can be a fun time sink for a little while, there's fun little stories that develop as the game goes on which you can take an active part in and have fun with it. I was one of the backers of That Which Sleeps who saw this recommended as a game that, unlike that dreaded kickstarter, would be finished. Technically they were right, the game has been officially 'released'. Just sucks every fun evil god game falls into the same issue of never quite being loved enough to finish. Sad.

Updates since i bought this a year ago have been disappointing. It is boring, and the features and the direction the team is going looks more like investing in revenue draw than player experience. Unfortunate for a game that had high potential and an exciting concept.

Kidnapped and brainwashed a guy to become my cultist and made him cheat on his wife with the local huntress. I cursed him with lycanthropy and his wife caught the two having an affair right before he turned into a werewolf and the huntress murdered him. The wife had a mental breakdown which led to her trying to murder the huntress but dying in the process. The local peasant girl was so disturbed seeing the body she fled only to be caught by giant spiders and dragged back to their lair to be eaten alive. Upon seeing all this, the remaining two villagers were so stressed they had heart attacks and died.

What I'm trying to explain with this story here is that Ruinarch has the premise to become an AMAZING game, but it's not quite there yet. My biggest gripe is that right off the bat they give you all the tools to sow destruction with without having to work for them which leads to the game feeling far too easy and the villages providing no sense of danger. Starting off weak and having to unlock abilities via a tech tree like Rimworld by slowly causing mischief would be appreciated. 

As of right now I would highly recommend putting this game on your Wishlist and revisiting it in a few months. The premise is there and they have all the tools to create a great colony sim/story telling game. I will be watching this game closely.

I'm very torn. on one hand I love the idea of a satan-sim but the bugs are strong here.
I love to grow large cults and play long, complicated games rather than just kill people, however this shows the weakness here. The longer the game, the more bugs build up. Groups will wander into caves and stay there and starve to death. cultists randomly turn into abominations. And finally suddenly the entire population will just stop moving and either starve to death or become immortal.
EDIT: since the writing of this review, these issues have largely been resolved. 

A for effort, but its got issues.
developer_response:
I would like to apologize for the issues you encountered. There's just too many situations that can happen in the game's simulation and sometimes we fail to anticipate some of them.

Given the funds we gained from early adopters, we were able to recently hire a part time dev and a couple of testers. Once they've settled into their roles and our development pace stabilizes, I expect that people will encounter less issues.

review:
The gamer outrage is strong in this comment section. The specter of reviews claiming the game is 'abandoned by devs' and 'unfinished and buggy' hangs over it. Neither claim is true, by the by.

Ruinarch is fine, guys. Its functional. And the last dev post was 23 may, literally a week ago. The one before that was in april. If this game is abandoned, I'm a monkeys uncle. It's unfortunate because there are many actually abandoned games and you should be angry as consumers and customers when that happens. It's just that the Ruinarch devs didn't do you gamers any harm, as near as I can tell - and you savvy pro geniuses lash out at them anyway. Check your fire, guys.

Now, as to my actual review, the game made positive impressions on me. My last 2 playthroughs were without bugs or crashes. Playthroughs can be quite short, as most objectives can be completed with a good afternoon of play and you don't need to finish them all to achieve victory.

However gameplay wise, I have a strong feeling of mehhh. The different objectives make it hard to pursue groups of objectives simultaneously, so what happens in practice is that the villages just fall into this kinda tedious chaos. The vampires hate the lycantropes hate the cultists hate the undead and the plague kills everyone equally. Don't get me wrong, chaos is fun, plans falling apart is fun. It's just that at some point you feel like you've seen all the map has to offer, and then you have to play 2 more hours on it because your necromancer keeps suiciding into a village to raise the furthest possible grave on the map.

Overall, recommended if you want a game where the core gameplay is doing stuff like locking your Sims in the bathroom until they pee themselves. Otherwise, very neutral review/wait for a sale type game.

It feel incomplete. There are 3 overlords that you can play as, but they don't really differ from each other. There really isn't a ton of story telling and you can hit the end goals off screen which feels anticlimactic. You can easily finish a run in a couple hours which may or may not be a good thing. This just didn't feel good as a completed game.

Ruinarch has a cool concept but is very unfinished and for now I can't recommend it. It's too buggy and villages are to easy to destroy and I need to help them to rebuild it's just not very fun. I had a lot of fun with creating werewolf dens or trying to create cult or vampires lords but it's just very confusing and buggy. I just can't recommend it now maybe after few updates it gets better.
Was a cool concept, had a lot of potential. Was confirmed as abandoned by the DEV's. Not worth getting in it's current state. Quite a sad outcome for a cool game. :/

Ruinarch:  A God Game With A Lot Of Promise

This God Game asks the question what happens if you get to be the Great Evil plaguing the world.  It does a great job of letting you answer that very question.  Most God Games are fairly typical:  get followers, use the power of prayer to do miracles, spread your influence, trounce other gods in the way.  

This game is refreshingly the opposite.  You don't need followers, you need misery.  Sweet, sweet, they are making a Pitch Perfect 4 kind of misery.  Misery is what powers you to do some rather nasty things to those inhabiting the world.  All the while you are trying to avoid attention as much as possible.  Rain down meteors, lighting, toxic fart clouds to often and villagers and angelic powers will take notice.  This results in people coming to look for who is responsible for yet another Pitch Perfect movie, err all the disasters you have been raining down.  Like free DVDs of Pitch Perfect 3.  No that power isn't in the game.

But that doesn't mean you can't have followers.  You can get cultists to follow you and carry out your dark will.  You can also get otherwise good villagers to do terrible things by sowing discord among them.  Hid someone's beloved guitar in another villagers home, share compromising information with the villagers that would be hurt the most by it, 

Successful players will find that by starting off doing little evil yet hardly detectable things will allow them to erode order and good will.  You can make use of local monsters to cause them to attack the village.  So imagine getting a few villagers exiled, who just happen to be the best fighters the village has to offer, and then suddenly getting a horde of beasts to flood into the village.

On that note if you are playing with multiple villages why not orchestrate a war?  In Ruinarch you can.

I'm really only scratching the surface here.  There is a ton of potential is this game is developed and the developers have an eye to delve deep on this one.

8 out 10.

I love the idea of this game, but I'm not a fan of the execution. The game is boring, and there aren't many clues about how you're supposed to play. Frustratingly, the villagers have a bad habit of randomly running off and getting themselves killed. Several times, I've lost hours worth of effort when my town commits suicide, leaving me no one to torment.

Fun for what it provides, but 20,99€ is a bit pricy.

Right now, it's on sale, which is a good time to pick it up and try it out.

It's still a long way from finished, though, so bear that in mind.

This game is great fun, ill be honest I've never really gotten into colony builder games so comparisons to things like rimworld actually put me off but THIS is hillarious. So my first run without training wheels (Non tutorial) I picked the puppetmaster archtype, had a bit of fun early on giving random people kleptomania, gluttony, unfaithful for the lols but then i stumbled on my favourite afflictions

Vampirism: Exactly what it sounds like, your vampire will periodically knock out villagers and try to eat them, this can be problematic as they arent too smart about it and can end up trying to KO someone in the middle of a family dinner.. take note vampirism can also be passed on (Not often) so if your careful you can create a vampire town quite easily. Please note vampires need blood to survive and CANNOT feed on other vampires, if there are no humans around they will desperately hunt and butcher animals but still cant eat them turning them into frenzied slaughterers of the living... I even had one butcher the corpse of a neighbour....

Gluttony: very good if you have poison spells but its true use is to combine with vampirism... oh yes these little killing machines will feed 3 or 4 times a day easily and if your lucky start a vampire epidimic...

Paralysis: The ultimate in dick moves, you can either do this to an active member of the town whose spouse will then look after and feed them... OR you can afflict someone whose just entered the town who no one will go looking for, leaving them to starve to death... OR the premier monster move, put this on a vampire... the locals will still try to feed them food... which they can;t eat and are unable to hunt due to the paralysis....


What I'm saying is this game allows you to be a complete monster, don't even get me started on brain washing a town leader into being a cultist or afflicting a town member with physcopathy and turning them into a serial killer.... 

Unleash your inner monster!

Buyer Beware, AI Icons, dropped features, and abandoned development.

This game was promising, with a great concept.  The devs took feature after feature out, eventually slapping together a dry 1.0 version.

ehhh its not horrible but its overpriced. an aggressive player can conquer a map in an hour or so, and unlocking a zombie plague means the game essentially plays itself.
I had fun with it. The game made you feel like a powerful dark god who was tormenting the mortals below. Retaliations were kind of a bitch but can be handled if you dont mind cheesing spells and capture commands. It was a good game for people who liked power fantasy, but it is definitely overpriced...
And then the latest patch came out.
They took out the ability to start with a village that already worshiped you, a move that made absolutely no sense because it was all your choice if you wanted one or not. They nerfed werewolves so they relied on you upgrading Lycanthropy (even if they were naturally spawned werewolves) so they don't just get turned into normal doggos and enslaved by the first druid that sees them. And they added a bunch of npc traits that can cripple you if you don't find them and kill them as soon as possible, a pain in the neck for larger maps and larger towns, because finding out who can cripple you just by existing costs resources, resources that said people will happily drain the moment they stub their toe or you interact with them the wrong way. Course, you can make all abilities free when you are creating your world...but some of them can lock out your abilities entirely, and in a sandbox game, locking the players out of choice is a stupid move. And if you do decide to give yourself even a little challenge by having your actions cost mana, accidentally interacting with a npc with a certain trait will drain all of your mana instantly, meaning you have to now stop and wait for it all to recharge before you can try and kill them.
And a sandbox game is what this is the best for. It's not indepth enough to be like a civilization builder. You are less like an evil overlord and more like a chaos god from warhammer. This game is more fun when it lets you do whatever the hell you want...
But what bugs me isn't the difficulty, or the malarky npc traits, or even how hard it is to defend your base when it is inevitably attacked.
I want my pre-made cultist town back.
It's small in the grand scheme of things, but as it stands making your own cult is a long, boring affair that involves having to kidnap people one by one and trying to convert them to your religion with a pitiful 3-5% chance of success. After that, you can order your hidden cultists to preach to their friends and family, and risk getting them arrested for demon worship (tho they supposedly patched that last part out). If they do get burned at the stake, then you just wasted alot of time and patience. 
It's an okay mechanic. No real complaints there...
But it takes forever just to get a decent sized cult, and each member gained is gambling with the life of a member already had, assuming you don't just kidnap everyone and waste time trying to convert them via torture. So, naturally you probably won't get to see all the things a demonic cult can do unless your a patient and lucky gamer, which is probably the best way to approach this game really...
But all of this wasted time can be skipped over with pre-spawned cults made during world creation. Which was patched out recently. You could have a bunch of mortal minions at your beck and call, with an entire village to guard your portal and wage war in your name right off the bat, letting you see what cults can give you much more easily than doing a long process that you may not even find worth your while. And seeing as how this is a sandbox game, having all of this already set up for you gave you more options on how you wanted to play. Like helping an evil, demon worshiping kingdom tear down heaven loving heroes. It was more fun this way, and I feel like the removal of this feature is a sign that the game is going to become less like a sandbox in the future, which is a poor direction to take at this stage.
TL;DR last patch boosted difficulty in a stupid way. Give me my damn cult villages back. Not worth no 25 dollars.

Lots of potential, but the game is underwhelming at this time. 

Some suggestions for the dev:

- Have some of the more powerful direct abilities require more "infrastructure" work to unlock. Eg. Meteor and the like requiring several disciples to cast. 

- The graphics. It's hard to focus on anything in particular as everything is bright and full of contrast. Simplify your color pallet, reduce the saturation on unimportant items. 

- Too many useless options. How useful is it really to be able to interact with a pile of garbage? A bush? I suggest making these items non-interactable until you include some form of interesting interaction with them in the game. 

I have high hopes for this game, but it's very early and needs a lot of TLC. I'm glad my money went towards the developer in hopes of making this game reach its potential.

It's still a little bare bones, and the graphics are pathetic, and the interface is abyssmal, but the foundation here, the social simulation, has tons of promise.  Already well-capable of creating engaging emergent stories, it sounds like the devs are actively working on improving the social engine first, which is already quite sophisticated, even tracking what information is known by each character and how they know it.  This is a tall order, and they've clearly bitten off enough to make many teams choke, but if they avoid scope creep and stick to the original vision, this game could end up being a truly rare gem.

I spent 45 minutes methodically turning the town against the village elder, only for a merman to pop out of the water and carry him into the sea one morning while he was fishing. 

10/10

Game has tons of potential. "Sadistic Rimworld" is definitely an accurate descriptor.

Unlike Rimworld however the game is small instances instead of being a long dedicated campaign. I hope to see a longer format for this game at some point. At the moment this is a great foundation and is a fun game to sit down to for an hour.

Ruinarch is a very unique game that has the potential to be great. However at this point of the game's development it is hard to recommend it. I do not think the game offers much in terms of replayability compared to other titles of a similar genre.

One other minor gripe I had was that the tutorial is long and repetitive. I get it if I want to find what happened I go to the log, I don't need that explained to me through 10 different examples.

The game is fun for a few hours and it may be worth it to invest now in Early Access before the price goes up when 1.0 releases. I feel once 1.0 drops this game will be a gem but until then I see myself investing time in other, more complete titles.

This game is awesome in premise and was looking to be very fun, but fell flat when it was just kind of abandoned. 
I picked it up partway through its Early Access phase and for a game still in early access it was pretty fun, just a bit lacking. I overlooked that because updates were fairly regular and added a fair amount of interesting content each time. Every time they updated the game I would play for a couple hours and then drop it because there really wasn't a lot to do yet, but I was very excited for the finished product. 
The finished product, as it turns out, was what I had been playing the whole time. On their final update they pushed it out of early access while adding minimal content, and immediately were hit with backlash from the community saying that it was not close to finished. They responded to this backlash by saying that they will continue adding content only to go back on their word and completely drop the game.

If for some reason this game is ever on sale for $10 or less then maybe consider buying it, but as it is it's completely lacking in content and not close to being worth the $25 price tag. If you wanna be a villain in a sandbox game then just buy WorldBox and drop some nukes or something.

Had a lot of potential; but now is abandon-ware. They stripped the game, hollowed it out and added AI images. Rushed a release just to try to squeeze out a few pennies out of the last buyers. Avoid all games by this publisher. Genuinely, refuse to buy their products. Although, not like their new typing game will go very far, lol.

I have always wanted a game with an excellent AI where there's an open world full of factions trading with each other, working together, fighting, etc. where I can just go around and help them, mess with them, empower them, or ruin them to my heart's delight and watch everything unfold. However, as it stands right now, this game is not it. 

Trust the other reviews. The concept and idea behind the game are great and they're the reason why I bought the game, but the execution is not well done and it does not appear that there will be an overhaul to address the shortcomings through Early Access. I just cannot recommend it to anyone else in its current state, but if you really want to try it out then go for it and note that 2 hours will not be enough to find out the flaws. You will sink your teeth into the game, hoping for the best, and then after 4+ hours you'll find out that the basic gameplay you just played is all there is and it does not get any more in-depth. I will check back on the game later down the road and amend my review if things change because the game's concept is beautiful but, again, the execution is awful.

Some of my complaints: 
- The villagers don't do much at all beyond very basic and superficial progression. 
- As much as I was hoping to get to see macro events, conflicts, territory changes, factions falling, etc. you will find out that it's very difficult to keep track of the overall picture. You do not see full-out wars and battles nor do you see much in the way of organized action. Your scheme to get a faction leader to declare war is disappointing. I'd expect organized attacks, skirmishes, etc. but all you get is a hostile status between the two factions and some fights if they randomly run into each other and that's really it. The logs are "okay" but they seem to be spotty and they do not help you find out what really happened to a dead village without having to go through each villager's log and you will still not understand what really happened. It's very weird. 
- There are no village border/territory markers so you don't know where one starts and where the other ends. 
- Your spells are underwhelming and they feel like it. The UI and user interactions are really cumbersome.
- The basebuilding mechanics are... I don't even know how to describe that mess. Get someone to re-develop it for you, devs, because this is unacceptable. You have to right click each tiny tile to corrupt it and then right click it again to build a wall, but only if there's not a random tile of demonic scenery that pops up which would block your wall's path and which you cannot remove so you have to build a maze around these random obstacles. Frustrating. 
- Researching and upgrading your pool gives you a random set of spells that you can select, you will get unlucky and get useless powers that you will be stuck with for the entire game (I play in Sandbox mode so it lasts a while).
- You can only initiate raids using your monsters if you are lucky enough to get a building that enables raiding when upgrading your pool. If you don't, you're out of luck. Enjoy.

I wish i would like this game.
 I played it since the early days and took a peak after almost every update until now and it just becomes a stale waiting game.

 The beginning is okay, You set up your base collect intel about the villagers and maybe capture some for blackmail or to convert them into cultistst. 
Back in the day there were diffrent modes like kill all villagers or reach your maximum energy.
 
Now there is only: Complete 13 objectives and the objectives never change either, even tho the 3 starting characters are supposed to be for 3 diffrent playstyles this new sytem forces you to play every round VERY similar.

When you enter the midgame you better have youtube or something running because then you have a good grip on everyone on the map and are just working of these tasks hoping that something interesting happens. 
I always tend to release the plague near the end because it cuts down the potentialy really strong and interesting people really fast.

I had a guy that was a monster. He was just a miner but had a 5 in martial arts and kicked the ass of my demons every time, he resistet all my powers etc, really interesting, but then the plague got to him and he became a boring zombie.

But overall it's just a waiting game with small things you might have to react to. 
Someone is summoning a powerful spell ? kidnap them.
Someone saw your base ? kidnap them.
Someone is blessed ? kidnap them.
The above can be replaced with spam them to death with abilitys or make a psycho path that hunts his class aswell (except for the mages cause they are immune to magic during their ritual


The starting character choice is weird because the manipulator is now due to the changes to psychic breaks quite fun, the ravager just rushes towns with demons and the lich just.... exists. 
He is able to turn an evil character into a necromancer but there arn't that many and even when he can turn someone evil with a psychic break or by brainwashing them he has no contol about it so the necromancer will just raid any city and die pretty easily and often when he suceeds they don't even plunder the gravejard for bodys.
You can ofc turn people into vampires or werwolfs with him but that doesn't do much except make them stronger or they just starve to death in the case of vampires because at some point everyone nearby is infected and they have nothing to drink anymore.

It would be great if you could assign towns to cultists because they just spread out and build a tempel across the map from the location of their leader who is the only one that can summon bone golems which are a required task for completion. ( in my last game i had 3 demon cultist towns and it took ages for them to build a temple, which then was placed in the village with a population of 2 and the cult leader was in another village.

Also perfectly normal cultists become extremly easily criminals so they get thrown out of their own faction which is weird, shouldn't they all be fanatics who would do anything to serve me ?
 
You can convince people to join the cult but given that cultists get a hiden evil trait nobody likes them it's kinda hard to let them make friends due to that. I would love to have "sleeper agents" in a faction but it's not really possible

While enjoyable and largely 'finished', it's a shame the game has been left in this state - it has so much potential for story telling (Rimworld style) but most of that capacity is unrealized even after all this time. Still, it's a fun romp if you are psychopathic-minded and would enjoy ruining the lives of innocent villagers. Getting to 100% achievements was quite a slog (and then they added even more!).

It's a great game but still needs quite a bit more to do and more significant interactions with the world and villagers before I could honestly suggest actually paying for this game but if you want something to kill a little time but with very little replay value as is this is for you

Potential that was left to die unfortunately. 

Shallow game-play. A web of relationships and interactions that can take place. There is space for deep planning. Inducing kleptomania into a villager only to steal resources so a defence tower isn't built....only for that villager to go in a rat cave and die because the AI is big on masochism. 
That tower from earlier? It was never built cause the only person knowing how to build in a town got into a fight with another villager and banished from the town. Wait 5 years for more people to come. They won't have any skills. 
You want your cultist village to build a damned temple already? Tough luck. The faction leader really likes to build ritual pyres to burn the people they dislike.
At the same time. Throwing a fireball into a village is a sure way to win. leaving the game feeling really empty, shallow and boring.

It's rare to see a game so deliberately ruined by a developer.
Feature after feature was REMOVED from the game for NO LOGICAL REASON whatsoever, leaving an empty, boring husk of a once-enjoyable game.

I wanted very badly to like this game but it is too small in scope for a "colony sim" to really work.

You choose a "tile" on a map to start your base, which is a pre-built large structure, and you can only build other huge pre-built squares. It's around here that you then realize that each giant square building is an entire tile on the overhead map and that the entire map is actually just what you see on a few screens worth of game. Think if Rimworld's in-game view was the entire world and each building you placed occupied a 20th of it. A 5 tile city is actually just 5 or so buildings.

Your mission is to kill all of the villagers in creative ways, and this is where the game is promising. You can exploit traits, give them afflictions, and then trigger them in fun ways. You can frame people for crimes and set them on one another, turn them into zombies that spread a plague, set fires, lots of cool stuff. But remember how each city tile is one building? that means you have like 7 people to kill before your game is over.

You can't really build any meaningful sort of base since the buildings are just giant squares, the world is tiny, and even if you could build any sort of custom base or you got attached to it it's over as soon as you kill a few people.

The cities should be huge, there should be lots of them,  and you should have to invade new tiles so at least there could be some sort of overarching campaign; give the player something to get attached to and a reason to expand. Having a tile based "world map" is deceptive because the world is really tiny and it implies otherwise.

Love seeing all the defensive reviews saying the game "isn't abandoned" back in June and May when they did the last update.

The game remains unfinished; most mechanics don't go anywhere, it remains buggy and stiff, and like...rimworld is right over there for a few bucks more and hundreds of hours of more fun. Go get that. Seriously.

The game doesn't have any kind of a tutorial (the tips that it calls a tutorial are not a tutorial) and from what I've seen it doesn't have any story at all, not even one you can make your own (like in Dwarf Fortress of Rimworld) and lastly the 3 playable archetypes all kinda look, feel and function the same with only a minor variation between them. It's like, do you want to play as a warrior, a fighter or a barbarian. Well, does it really matter if all they do is hit the thing with the sword? As it stands, this game seems woefully unready for 1.0. Maybe when it's more flushed out, with story, a tutorial and more identity to the 3 arches, I'll give it a positive review. For now though I'd have to recommend against it.

This is just too bare bones right now for me to recommend it. It's selling itself as a "story generator" style game, but the content, interactions, and AI are too limited atm to say anything really interesting happened. I played about 5 hours, trying my best to enjoy it, before finally just saying "No, I'm not having a good time, this just isn't interesting enough."

I'm willing to update my review later if the game improves enough, but I'm not one to review a game well on what it *promises* to be, rather than what it is. Early access keeps getting used for beta or even alpha builds of projects and that's not really what it's for. There should be enough core game in any early access product that even if development is tragically cut short, you can be satisfied with your purchase.

Great concept and decent gameplay, but it's incredibly unfinished. It's $25 for what is about the quality of a pretty good Kongregate game, which can be beaten and most features experienced in a single hour. Narrative styorytelling hasn't really been implemented, and I'm not convinced it can even happen under the current gameplay loop, which is basically just a classic RTS with modern hands-off management features.

I'd recommend this game for around $10, $5, but otherwise it's very Kickstarterish in that you're paying for expectations that might not come about.

Worth the money during a sale. Easy to learn and play. Games usually take a couple of hours to beat because of the number of objectives.

[h2]Pros:[/h2]

* Randy random experience

* Creatures (beasts) are very in-depth. Did not expect my cultist to be abducted inside his own house by a tarantula, only for it to carry him across the map, dumping him inside an abandoned mine-shaft, spinning and then trapping him inside a web, and slowly eat chunks off of him while he cried and screamed in agony (in the middle of this torture session he became catatonic due to being an alcoholic and not having touched alcohol in the last hour). This right here made the game an easy sell.

* A good power trip I guess

* Game is receiving regular updates, not sure if this will keep up after version 1.0 though.

[h2]Cons:[/h2]

* No way to play as the "good guys". Would love to be able to build my village by playing as either a random villager or the village leader, while surviving the Ruinarch. If this were a thing you could probably make a multiplayer around it, though my point is that even as a single-player addition this would be sick. Even if you take this idea and make it DLC I would buy it


* No real difficulty. I feel like it's extremely hard to lose in this game. You plop your portal down in some obscure corner of the map, and no one will ever even go there. If they do decide to go, it's usually a dumbass that wanders off alone (that you imps/skeletons can easily kill), as opposed to a search party finding your portal.

In order to make it "harder" the only thing you can do is increase your cooldowns, reduce your mana, which in turn don't make the game more difficulty: it makes it monotone. Having to wait for that "snatch villager" cooldown to end doesn't put me in a pinch, instead it outright makes it boring. Artificial difficulty that fails miserably.

But if you're a casual like me, and just want that randy random experience, then yeah go for it (during sale). Also game has a few bugs, but nothing game-breaking.


* 99% of the objects on the ground have no description on them. For example, corn raises a villagers strength by 50% when they eat it, except on the corn itself that is never stated. 

* [b]Low replayability[/b] (this one made my think if this was even gonna be a thumbs up). Once you try out the 3 classes, there's no reason to play a fourth game. Objectives are all always the same. 

Map generation feels the same (mainly since there aren't that many different cave types and structures that can generate in the over world, so every map you generate will change minimally in what gets spawned. E.g wolf dens, chickens, cave with either trolls, spiders, wisps or ratmen, a mage tower with either golems or the mobs I stated before, a legendary smithy or whatever it's called, etc, all over and over again, every map).

* Weird collision. Sometimes villagers phase through some of their houses' walls when walking somewhere.

* I expected the AI villagers to feel more... "alive". Not really sure how to describe this one in words, but they feel robotic, clunky. Maybe it's because they're limited to a few actions? It's like you took the rimworld colonist "Thoughts" thing and made it more stale, "less-alive".

6/10

Don't buy this game unless you're really only interested in the flavor of being an evil overlord, but even that is poorly executed and unsatisfying. It's absurdly easy. The games mechanics are disjointed and you can easily win ignoring most of them. Your first play through will get boring fast.

The games design is fundamentally flawed. Instead of fighting off the forces of good while you slowly build enough power to summon yourself into the world, you're just waiting for the the portal to upgrade and, if your so inclined, you can bother some villagers while you wait.

The development road map gives no indication the game is going to get more interesting either. 

The price is laughably high at $25. There's probably not even a sale price that feel worth it to me. The games just not fun.
developer_response:
I apologize if our game description may have given the wrong impression about the game. We did not really set out to make a combat-focused game but rather a more behind-the-scene story generator from the villain's perspective. 

We want the player to have more control over the narrative (manipulating the villagers sandbox style) so difficulty has taken the backseat in terms of development priority. This is changing over time as we are actively introducing more challenges over the next few updates.

We still want to emphasize that players should not expect RTS-type or even Dungeons type evil vs good battles in the game.

review: Bought this game early on and was a lot of fun. Now that I go back to it, it's just tedium trying to finish a world and most of the objectives aren't even explained to you. Why would I need to keep putting down monster habitats so I can RNG my way into an abomination for a PLAGUE-focused victory? Doesn't make any sense. I'd rather have the old kill every villager objective than this confusing mess
Liked the game but sadly it was abandoned.  Buy this if you like the current gameplay loop but the limited content.

A unique concept.  I'm just not convinced there's enough content for the price.  I've got 10 hours in it(not rushing), and feel like I've seen and done about all there is, at half the cost of a AAA title(25$ at the time of writing this), I need more bang for my buck.  We're on phase 9, and I get a little worried when early access games enter that phase of "okay we're mostly done.  Players, tell us what you want."

When I buy a game, I'm not signing up as a beta tester or product designer, I'm wanting the game company to do that.  This game will end up being another one of those Early Access titles, I wish I hadn't bought.  They always have just enough content and allure to get you to trust them, and then a year+ later you're stuck with a short game, that's 'almost' complete.

I bought this game when it was in early access expecting it to get expanded on. After release there has been one update and the game has been abandoned, sad this never went anywhere and they are charging so much for a pretty shallow experience.

Another EA title where I'm wishing there's a "thumbs in the middle" option. 

In its current state, it's kind of a fun "toy" rather than a complete game. There isn't a lot of difficulty or depth going on, you just kinda mess with people. Don't get me wrong, it's definitely fun to mess with the villagers, and you can make your own difficulty by intentionally limiting yourself (elect not to use monsters, or spells, etc), or set your own goals in exactly what kind of torments you want to inflict on the villagers.

But it's not really a coherent game in a lot of ways, and some of the systems don't really make any sense. To give an example: you have "building" options to create things like listening posts (that give you an info feed on what your victims are up to) or a bioloab (to create and upgrade diseases to inflict on the villagers), but you only need one of each building, they build instantly, don't consume much in the way of resources, and with one exception aren't strategic locations you need to defend. You just place the buildings the moment the game starts and forget all about them. While there is a base attack mechanic, it's so easily avoidable that you have to intentionally trigger it. 

There's a few of these types of systems that are just kind of "there", but don't really have any mechanics or use. Perhaps they're foundations for later updates, or perhaps they're deprecated early concepts that the devs have moved away from. 

In any case, while this is enjoyable to play around with, the overall experience as a very loose sandbox doesn't really justify the price currently being charged. I'm definitely interested in seeing where development goes with this, and if they can tighten the experience up in a way that matches the game's "Reverse Rimworld" pitch

I really want to enjoy this game; however, it feels very bare bones or I guess the right word is I don't feel connected to the world. Stuff happens, but I don't feel like I'm really doing anything. Its a unique take, but something needs to be done to actually make tension happen.

Sort of a half-baked game, I really don't recommend buying in this state unless updates begin again from the developer. They have a good thing going here, it just needs more work that maybe they're unable or unwilling to do for whatever reasons.

I want to love the game, because it's unique... but it's barely a game. There's no challenge, even on the hardest settings, and the AI is basically oblivious to your presence.

The basic game mode is to upgrade your Portal to lv7. This is incredibly easy, you can basically win this game mode in 15-20 minutes without any resistance. It's basically just waiting on your X resource to fill up, upgrade, repeat -- win. The other game modes include Eliminate All and Sandbox. If you choose any load out other than Puppetmaster this is incredibly easy by simply spamming spells. This does increase your "retaliation" which will send waves of angels against you which are fairly strong and do potentially pose a risk if not dealt with. However... all you have to do is spam them with spells and they die fairly easily. It doesn't really matter what spells you use and if you pause the game you can spam your spells on them without them even being able to move. The last mode is Sandbox which is more realistic what this game is. The other two modes aren't really a game because there's no real resistance. It makes the game incredibly boring.

However where this game shines is the Sandbox options. You can use creative methods to instill chaos into the world. You can change people's traits to make them steal, become serial killers, afraid of fire, etc. You can use their actions to increase approval or cause disapproval between citizens. You can make them hate each other enough to where they kill each other even wage war. You can make approval go up and then cause an illicit affair, resulting in turmoil between three people.  You can turn people into zombies, vampire, lycans, etc. You can start plagues, poison supplies, launch raids, etc. You can capture and imprison citizens torturing them until you make them a pawn. You can have them spread a cult across the world or heed your orders.

All of that sounds great... but you have to struggle to make any of that happen. Which is why Sandbox is the only real option, otherwise you'd beat the game too fast or kill off the people too quickly to even be able to do anything fun. But even though the Rimworld type open-ended Chaos role playing is the highlight of the game it's not really developed well. The base mechanics of the game are extremely lacking and the AI is far too simple minded. The level of risk is far too low and there's almost no repeatability for play time. The only reason I have 15hrs is I left it on overnight. You could play every single demon load out and game mode in a few hours... but you'd basically just be repeating the same game x6-9 since it's all the same.

sadly it started out with an interesting premise but ultimately fell flat either due to time(highly doubt it) or budget concerns because it seemed like it had to drop or reduce a lot of the far reaching goals that it initially set out. is the game god awful?no. is it good? no. it just kinda exists, which for this kinda game makes it mediocre at best where you'd would be better of getting a different sandbox game. I don't think at this point of writing that it is worth my Canadian 30$ price tag if its on sale for like 10 then yea i guess wasting an afternoon on it could be reasonable but that still may be hard/not worth it.

tl;dr too expensive for what it is get something else like world box or something

I had an absolute blast with this game.  It's quite a funny god-game experience, except you exist to make life miserable or just inconvenient enough that the villagers leave... most of them die.  

However, as of today, there are only 3 demon archetypes, with pretty obvious play styles: The Puppetter is the hands-off Demon of spygames and daily irritants; The Lich is basically the plaguemaster; and the Ravager is just destruction and chaos in the most direct form.  

Unfortunately, the game is a bit imbalanced today.  Each specialty skill/building is not locked to the archetypes.  Therefore, all demons can create a plague, or take an earthquake spell, or start a cult.  I've played 4 of the scenarios, and on 4 out of 5 attempts we just end up killing most of the village with plague.  Very successful, but only sort of fun. I think the most fun I had with plague was making one of the symptoms "poisonous gas..."  The plagued were literally farting poison pools as they went about their day xDDDDD

Starting a cult was the most interesting activity in all the attempts I played, but it's also a bit broken.  As the demon-god, I can directly pick-up someone and attempt to brainwash them to join the cult.  And I can keep doing that after gaining my first recruit.  After you gain cultists, you can send them to nab fellow townspeople to drag back to the cult chamber, but there's no point when I can just do it myself.  Kidnapping them myself though is broken, because there is no chance of failing the kidnap, only chance to fail the brainwashing. 

Nitpicking aside, the game did provide several hours of laughs for myself and stream, but I need to see much deeper gameplay to agree with Steam's recommendation that this can satisfy any Rimworld blues.

Neither the developer Squeaky Wheel Studio nor publisher Maccima Games exist nor actively develop anymore, so don't expect any further support or updates. It used to be much better prerelease, but they started to remove content throughout development. It's still fun occasionally, but I wish the game received better treatment from its developers while they still existed. Unless they suddenly return and begin supporting both of their titles again, I cannot in good faith recommend the game. If you do decide to get it, revert to an older version if you can.

Main issue:
-Game is both too simple and too complex. Initially it feels like there are so many spells, afflictions, buildings, schemes etc that you can pull among all the inhabitants that also have their own personality traits. But later on you realize it all does the same thing and most of these concepts are quite hollow. It took me no time to fully upgrade my portal and have no progression left, only a mess of options to deal with the inhabitants which very rarely would synergize.
-There is no point to you having a base other than being attacked. You build things but quickly run out of buildings to place so you either hide or protect your base which adds no fun to the game. It could have been more thought through for sure. Either turn it into a defense mechanic where villagers are constantly attacking as a different stage of the day where you'd also prepare defenses or just make hiding the base into a better mechanic.

Long story short game has very little to offer. It needs more content but also a reevaluation of certain concepts like the base building, synergy between spells etc.

The style and tone is really weird, too. The character pictures are all plastic-y vaguely anime-looking AI generated images, which does not lend to any cohesive sense of artistic style. Tonally, it's very nihilistic and "MUST DESTROY ALL HUMANS!" misanthropic, but it doesn't quite blow the concept out enough to be funny or absurd. The reliance on actual Christian demon iconography and the level to which it takes itself seriously ends up making for a very juvenile, cringe tone that made me feel embarrassed at times.

The Letter is an episodic Visual Novel game, about a diverse group of people who are affected by a strange curse. The game is full of  decisions the player can take to further the story, which immediatly reminds one of japanese horror classic "The Ring" - not at least because of the black haired ghost woman herself. Besides this resemblance though the story has enough novelty, and most important a good cast of characters as well as a gripping presentation and atmosphere to make you want to play it through.
The decisions mostly impact the relationships between the different characters, which in the end impacts the story as you play through it and finally will lead to different endings for each one, but also can lead to a quick and bloody ending sometimes. This also can happen through some Quick Time Events, which are thankfully optional to play through.

The characters are well enough defined and each of them is presented during their representative playthrough. Regarding the Presentation one can only say that the backgrounds are nicely drawn as are the different faces and poses of the characters, which helps transporting their moods, beside the well done voice actors, who are doing a good job bringing their alter egos to live. The atmospheric music rounds up an good impression and is especially during horror scenes very fitting (together with the grisly done ghost appearances).

Rounding this up "The Letter" has quite some achievements to get, the decisions really influence the game somewhat so more than one playthrough won´t get boring. With around 15 to 20 hours for the intial playthrough you get your moneys worth here for sure. Technically the game is well done too.. despite some missing audio at a few speeches and a few typos here and there.

Conclusio: I am someone who get´s bored easily, but the Letter had me gripped enough to make me invest quite some hours into it to get my first endings.. not exactly those i was aiming for either, but the characters are rich enough that initial likes or dislikes even change during the game. Also i very much enjoyed the thrill this game could provide, together with the voice actors, who really fit their characters (i especially liked the Police Chief/ Bartender speaker ;) ).  So all in all i can really recommend The Letter to players who like a good VN or also Telltale Games.. or just a good ghost story.

Point Scale: 8/10
