The acting is also outstanding; Zach and Luke's actors are particularly amazing.

Also Ashton is best boy, 10/10

Do you enjoy VNs at all? Buy it.
Do you like long and branching stories? Buy it.
Do you like "horror"? Buy it.
Do you want high production value with full voices and animated sprites? Buy It.

The Letter is - quite simply - amazeballs. At least, I think so. Some notes about my experiences with the game thus far (I've finished it 3 times, and have plans for a couple more as I barely have 60% of the achievements) to follow:

1. From a gameplay standpoint, it's relatively basic VN-type stuff.  Dialogue in a box at the bottom of the screen, you read it, and move on to the next. All of the characters (including you) are fully voiced, with the exception of internal monologues and thoughts. Also, the sprites are animated and quite pretty to look at. Where I feel The Letter sets itself apart is the branching storylines.

To begin, you play 7 different chapters, each from a different character's perspective. Your choices in dialogue (and [spoiler]whether they live or die[/spoiler])carries over from one to the next, so a simple choice in Chapter 1 can have far reaching repercussions all the way in Chapter 7. I both love this, and it drives me insane. If you are a completionist, this one may take awhile - especially without a guide. [spoiler]My first playthrough, I had one person in a coma, one die, and the rest live. Then I tried one where everyone lives, and one where everyone dies. Somehow, I managed to screw up that last one and one person still lived. Some are much easier to kill than others![/spoiler] There's also a relationship system, where dialogue choices increase or decrease their ties to one another. This can mostly affect the Epilogues and the endings individuals and pairs receive.

2. The story itself. While the "horror" elements are relatively soft, there is a solid "creepy" vibe to much of the game.  There are a few mild jumpscares, but nothing too intense. In fact, I think the strongest of these is actually [spoiler]the first time you click new game from the menu[/spoiler]. Other than that, the ghost's image and sound design are spot on. My only real complaint is that there are times when the story feels like it stalls out a little with an over-lengthy internal struggle. CGs are well placed and are more interactive.

3. QTEs. While I understand the desire to add "gameplay" to a VN, these feel unnecessary and at times, frustrating to me.  Thankfully, you are able to skip them (though you'll miss out on a few achievements). There are also settings to make them easy ([spoiler]though the number mashing one in the elevator with Ash is still annoying[/spoiler]).

I'm sure there's more, but that's all I can think of as I hastily type this out. I simply wanted to make sure I gave my thumbs up review for the game. I've spent almost 40 hours in this VN and I'm sure I have at least 10 more to go. This rivals The Fruit of Grisaia as my favorite VN, and I think people should know about this gem.

UPDATE [Dec. 19, 2021]: Looking back, this review came off meaner than I would have liked. I'll leave it as it is because I stand by the substance. The Letter's script needed more polishing, though it was fundamentally sound. I also want to emphasize that the game's overall presentation was excellent.

****

I wanted to like The Letter. Too bad, because I wanted to quit starting with chapter 3, and only kept playing to get my money's worth. Couldn't make it past the beginning of chapter 5, so I stopped and just read spoilers online. That's a shame. There's so much going for it. The art is good. The atmosphere is creepy. They do a decent enough job juggling all the characters. 

It's just that everybody rambles. Mind you, I love text-heavy games like Analogue: A Hate Story, and Planescape: Torment. That's why I bought this. The problem is that there's so much "telling" not "showing" here. Narrators outright say who is superstitious, who is a jerk, who is aloof, blah blah blah. What a boring way to tell a story when it makes up a fat chunk of the script. Worse, they essentially repeat lines and sentiments A LOT. Look, I got it the first time you said Zach is a sweetheart. It’s way more interesting to have characters DO stuff instead of constantly reminding me about their personality traits. Repetition can be interesting, but you've got to be clever about it. The Letter wasn't clever. 

Clicking through all the redundant dialogue and narration just made this game feel like a chore. Whoever edited the lines needed to be more aggressive. A leaner script would’ve taken this game to the next level.

Which, again, is a shame. There’s a reason The Letter has so many good reviews. I liked the characters, rambling notwithstanding. The story had me hooked: That’s why I went ahead and looked up the ending(s). The script’s flaws prevented me from enjoying it, however. If I had to compare this game to food, then it’s like eating popcorn with too much salt and butter. A promising meal that became too annoying to eat.



Tl;dr: This game bored me because there were so many redundant lines, and I couldn’t finish playing. Buy it on sale if you buy it at all. You'll enjoy playing if you like the aesthetic and semi-creepy tone, and don’t mind a “tell” style of storytelling.

I have mixed feelings about this game. On one hand, it has an excellent soundtrack (including three beautiful vocal tracks), great voice acting, a fun anime-style opening, and absolutely beautiful CGs, sprites, and animated backgrounds. It has a good story hook, a few interesting plot branches, and some [i]very[/i] well-executed horror scenes. (My favorite of these involves completing a Quick Time Event to slowly close an elevator door while the ghost rapidly crawls toward you.) On the other hand, this game can be incredibly frustrating.

Although it has a branching narrative based on player choice, most of the really divergent branches occur in just the first two chapters. Isabella and Hannah's choices can dramatically change their stories, their fates, and the lives of those around them. However, Ashton's chapter (the second-to-last) always follows almost the exact same formula regardless of who is even still alive at that point in the story. It makes the late game feel extremely tedious and ruins the replay value. It's also kind of jarring sometimes because if Isabella or Hannah dies, the impact of this on the story and their loved ones is major, but if the protagonists of most later chapters (even just chapter 3 or 4) die, their death will not impact the story much and will be barely remarked upon even by their friends.

On that note, I should talk about the characters. This is a very character-focused game, which is normally something I love, but a lot of these characters just aren't [i]quite[/i] deep enough to carry the game the way they need to. That's not to say they aren't likeable, but so much of the game is spent reading characters' lengthy inner monologues and emotional conversations with each other that you kind of need to be more invested in these characters than you would in a game that's more focused on plot or gameplay. And several of these characters really only have a few key traits that [i]everyone[/i] (especially Rebecca) keep pointing out in their own internal monologues. It's not very noticeable at first (except for during Rebecca's chapter - she [i]really[/i] likes to monologue about her friends' quirks) but by the second playthrough, I was getting frustrated that the "character development" seemed to only go so deep relative to how much text I was reading.

Far more frustrating though is the disappointing ending. The regular ending(s) are ridiculously anticlimactic. The true ending leaves a ton of unanswered questions. There's really no satisfying ending to be found, unfortunately, and that drags the rest of the story down in the process. After 60 hours consisting of multiple playthroughs and the true ending, I found myself having to google who exactly the ghost is and why she ended up that way. That's already a bad sign, but I found out that the creators had intended to explain the details of this rather key backstory in a bonus prologue chapter if the game's sales were good and had intentionally kept things vague in the main story for this purpose. Since this is a mystery that is frequently brought up throughout the game, I'm really disappointed that they didn't consider this important enough to put in the existing game as it results in a rather unsatisfying and confusing experience. If you are someone who likes vague backstories in horror so that you can theorize about the mystery yourself, this could very well be a good thing though. It just wasn't for me.

What really impressed me when I started up The Letter was the production value (animated backgrounds, excellent voice acting, good UI, etc.), so I was also really sad to discover that the production value really drops around chapter 6. There are lots of spoken lines that aren't voiced and the Skip button frequently fails to work. This is around when the story gets more formulaic too, so it's especially annoying. You frequently have to manually click through conversations that are nearly identical to the ones from your previous playthrough and where a third of the lines aren't even voiced correctly. It's really just disappointing given how promising everything looked at first...

All in all, I don't really regret the time I spent on The Letter. It's a decent game, but it's unfortunately a bit overambitious and probably would have been better if it cut down on some of the blander dialogue in favor of a better written ending. If you go into this expecting the visual novel equivalent of a mediocre-to-decent Hollywood horror blockbuster, you'll probably be quite happy. If you immediately get excited by the beauty of the game or intrigued by its story, you'll probably be at least somewhat disappointed.

Early thoughts...

Just finished chapter 3 out of 7 and I already spent about 9 hours or so, so each chapter should be around 3+ hours (and late chapter are longer)... which is pretty lengthy I imagine if I will also include the branching paths/narratives and all. The number of achievements (172 of them) says so too. All dialogue is voiced.

So far it's been interesting, engaging, and tense. The scary parts (with the animation) sends me to chills.

Aside from the supposedly many endings this game has, some decisions, relationships, and butterfly effect unlocks mini-events and romance routes. You will also get to control and have point-of-view of multiple characters. Heck, a chapter ending can have a character without plot-armor die. Somewhat reminds me of Until Dawn.

I hope more people will notice this game and pick it up. Looking forward to play more and will give my final thoughts afterwards...

"The Letter" is the best visual novel I've every played, and I'm not making statements like this lightly. I think, the last time I wrote something like that, was in my review for "Danganronpa" and this game... Oh, boy, this game offers so much more than I was expecting.

[h1]What's great?[/h1]

[b]Plot. [/b]Simple, but interesting concept (remember those annoying chain letters?) gets turned into something incredibly complicated and engaging. It was obviously inspired by Asian horror movies [spoiler]pale lady, long black hair all over her face, crawling around and making scary noises[/spoiler] - yes, we've seen it a thousands of times, but it was done in truly outstanding way. You have curse, you have hunted mansion, mysteries of the past, yadda, yadda, all those classic horror elements, but everything works perfectly, thus showing, that cliches aren't necessary a bad thing, as long as you use them to tell a compelling story.

[b]Characters.[/b] There are seven main characters, each one with their own chapter, each with their own issues, interests and decisions, that will impact not only the main story, but their relationship with each other. The best thing any story can do, is make you care about characters. With horrors, it's especially important, since it makes everything much more engaging and tense. I cared about every single one them - Becca, Ash, Belle, Zach, Hannah, Marianne, even Luke. [spoiler] Yes, Luke! Guy, I literally hated during first four chapters, to then completely change my mind and consider him my favorite protagonist - that proves how good the writing for this game is.[/spoiler] Characters are complicated and even though I found some of their behaviors unrealistic, it was never breaking the immersion.

[b]Visuals![/b] Moving sprites. Moving backgrounds. Moving CGs. I generally don't care that much about visual side of games, but here, art is not only incredible, but also adds so much to the atmosphere. [spoiler]Whenever the monster shows up... Wow. I could feel like something is crawling on my skin. [/spoiler]

[b]Sounds. [/b]This is literally the most important thing, when we talk about horror games. "The Letter" has amazing sound design. The music is great, it always fits the atmosphere of the scenes, and sounds, no matter if it we talk about frightening screams or gentle laughs, or some stuff going in the background, make this game incredibly immersive experience. Also, it has voice acting. I don't like to play visual novels with V/A, but I've turned it on during few scenes, and it's good.

[b]Fear.[/b] Fear, just as humor, is very subjective thing. Games like FNAF and dozens of "Fnaf wannabies" made a lot of people believe that jumpscares are just cheap horror tricks, with no substance. I disagree with that - good jumpscare, in the right moment, is something that will make your skin crawl. This game has jumpscares - great jumpscares, may I add. It also has gore. It has psychological horror. It has pretty much everything you could expect from a title like this. Whatever you enjoy, when it comes to horror, you won't be disappointed.

[b]Choices matters.[/b] Finally. Even those little ones, that affects how characters think about each other, how they react in certain situations, and so on. But there are also big choices. The one that affects who survive and who doesn't, how the story will go, and how will it end. "The Letter" offers more branching paths than I've ever seen, with many endings and possibilities. 

[h1]In short:[/h1] 

If you like either good horror stories, or good visual novels, it's a must have. It's absolutely worth the price, visuals are outstanding and pretty much set a new level for other games in that genre, the sound design is amazing, voice acting is good, plot is deep and engaging, with great characters and both darker and lighter moments. Add to that, important choices and branching paths, and you have a nearly perfect game. Or just perfect. I think "The Letter" fully deserves this sort of praise.

The Letter is a horror visual novel that tells the tale of seven people who fell victim to a malicious curse. Causing them to fall upon a series of hallucinations… or was it real?

[h2] Story [/h2]

The game contains seven extensive chapters, beginning with Isabella’s route as the first chapter. Starting at school, Santos was checking up on her friend, Rebecca Gales just before heading to work as a real estate agent. Her current large sale revolves around the infamous Ermengarde Mansion, located on the outskirts of Luxbourne City. It is rumored to be a haunted ghost paradise lurking about. Even various disappearances had been linked to the mansion. The usual cliche ghost stories, nothing special. However, Isabella’s unfortunate fate leaves her noticing a bloodied-up letter filled with two words; “Help me” which fills half the paper, and ending with the sentence “Send this to 5 people or else…” It is after this letter that the other six characters unexpectedly become cursed too. The plot even brings up their past traumas, troubled situations, their relationship, and how each character gets to meet the other even if they don’t relate entirely. Additionally, there’s a whole story behind the mansion and its original owners, though I didn’t get to know the whole details [spoiler] due to causing characters to die in certain branches. [/spoiler]

[h2] Atmosphere [/h2]

The vibrant CGs show a sense of life and sometimes coziness, yet they deliver horror and gore. The art for both backgrounds and characters is nothing short of astounding. I also noticed such beautiful details for various items such as foods! 

[h2] Characters & Voice Acting [/h2]

The starting route order will be as follows: Isabella Santos, Hannah Wright, Zachary Steele, Marianne McCollough, Rebecca Gales, Ashton Frey, and Luke Wright. These are the main characters, along with a few minor ones. In each character's route, we will have access to their stream of consciousness in every event they experience with the others. In simpler terms, we get to see their perspective. There are clear options for both friendship and romance among the various characters.

I usually don't focus much on voice acting in my reviews, but in this case, it stands out fantastically. The voice acting is truly exceptional and is one of the reasons I kept playing. I especially loved the voice of Hannah Wright, portrayed by Amber Lee Connors. Her voice is soft, soothing, and elegant. It’s similar to my favorite British actress Billie Piper (fan since Doctor Who days). I'm not belittling other voice actors; they all delivered high-quality performances equally, and I particularly enjoyed Hannah's and Ashton's routes.

[h2] Gameplay [/h2]

[i] Tap… tap… ttaapp [/i]

It’s a visual novel there’s nothing to do. It’s effortless, but there are QTEs, and it differentiates each time one occurs. You can choose to skip it though. Moreover, there is an extensive range of outcomes depending on the choices made in response to various situations. Each response can lead to multiple endings and affect relationships. A single choice may result in a positive or negative outcome for each character, or it could have a positive impact on one character while negatively impacting another.

[h2] Conclusion and Final Thoughts [/h2]

I really enjoyed it. Nevertheless, it didn’t hook me. The horror part in particular, it’s cheesy. It’s rather an otome for me. I liked the characters and their backgrounds. The writers did absolutely great with each one, which is why I continued to play because I got a huge crush on Ashton Frey, and partly because I wanted to see where my choices would lead, which happened to be a good ending in my opinion. The most boring routes for me were Marianne’s and Rebecca’s. I struggled to finish them the most. I had an expectation however before purchasing it that they’ll be trapped together in the mansion or something; sort of like Corpse Party in that regard. It did but only briefly in the final chapter. Yet the outcome did impress me too nonetheless. 

As I’ve mentioned before, the voice acting influenced me too. It is marvelous! There were quite a bit details that annoyed me, but at the end of the day, I chose not to recommend it not because it is unworthy, oh believe me it certainly is if you want to consider it as an otome. Besides, I wouldn’t have spent 43 hours over it as a VN enjoyer. Anyway, the reason is that it didn’t deliver the horror part right. At least that’s what I think. It’s not scary at all and through my playthough I never once said (that’s fucked up) to myself which is something I always like to say when I see or read crazy shit (excuse my language I’m excited writing this lol). Back to the point, the amount of horror was less than the average and more like slice of life yaddi yadda.

All in all, it’s decent. Great characters, writing, voice acting, illustrations, and nice soundtracks. However, it’s weak in terms of horror. It has the elements set correctly but not the action for me. I would recommend it if anyone would prefer “romance horror VN” otherwise, I wouldn’t. There are better ones such as Death Mark.

Okay, first of all, yes, this game is long, but it took me 300 hours because I mostly played it in Chinese, and I'm not very good at Chinese. Well, I'm a lot better now thanks to this game, but anyway, your playtime should be much lower.

Anyway... is it a well-written, well-developed story with halfway relatable characters? Not really. Is it a fun, campy horror visual novel with good production value and A TON of choice? Yes. It's a (mostly) well-designed game with high production value, good art, and a sufficiently interesting plot with more branches than you can imagine brought down by unlikeable characters, poor writing, bad grammar, and the worst voice acting direction I've ever seen. If you're looking for a campy horror story with tons of choice and don't mind unreasonably long monologues, this is your game.

GOOD:

・The art. There are different art styles for the characters in different contexts (e.g., other characters you're talking to, your character's icon when they're talking, epilogue art), but they're all good. It's a very polished, nice-looking game overall. Luke has an annoying little line on his face when you're talking to him, but otherwise, the art is great.

・The choice. There are TONS of branches. Pretty much every choice matters, either giving you a different ending or changing the characters' relationships. Sometimes the relationship questions are ridiculously obvious and sometimes it seems completely arbitrary which option is the "good" one, but choices certainly matter.

・The branch tree. You get a nice visual for each chapter of all the choices and branches, which is such a great idea. It was quite poorly implemented, but I love the idea.

・The story. Well, there are a lot of problems with the story, and the writing is quite bad, but it's fun as a game. It would not be a good book, but it's good as a game. The characters (might?) grow on you. 

・The soul. It's a labor of love, and you can tell. I have a lot of qualms with the game, but I can appreciate that the devs poured their hearts into it, and I did have a great time with it.


BAD:

・The repetition. Chapters 1, 2, and 4 are basically fine. Chapters 3 and 5 have some annoying repetition. Chapter 6 has a ton of repetition, and Chapter 7 is MOSTLY repetition. My guess is the visual branch tree design caused this because there's limited space to visually represent all the possible nodes, but the game won't recognize identical text on a different node as read, so you'll find yourself having to manually skip text ALL THE TIME once you're far enough in. 

・The grammar. Forget a professional proofreader, this game didn't even get checked by a halfway competent native English speaker. You'll hardly go two lines without encountering some sort of mistake. I played most of the game in Chinese, but I did switch to English at the end, so I could still get a good picture of the English quality.

・The voice acting direction. Johannes and Ashton are the only characters with consistently good voice acting. Rebecca, Marianne, and Zach are consistently awful. Other than Kylie, who sounds like an adult exaggerating the squeakiest, most annoying kid voice in the world, I don't think the voice actors themselves are necessarily bad. I think it's just godawful direction, because the tone always seems at least 10 times more intense than the context. Rebecca and Marianne sound like they overreact with blind fury to literally everything and Zach sounds so annoyingly dense most of the time.

・The characters. I'm not sure what the devs were going for, but I don't think the characters are that likable. Ashton was the only one I consistently liked, but it seems like a lot of people don't like him, and the writers certainly villianize him every chance they get. The game gaslit me for 300 hours about Rebecca being "patient," even though she routinely starts yelling at her friends for no reason at all. Zach is really annoying because the writers won't stop doting on him. Even a character who hates him still says about him, in his first-person narration, "What little anger he is capable of feeling is clear on his face, lining his soft and kind features." Come oooon, is that how you would think about someone you hate? Really?

・The writing. It has all the typical trappings of amateurish writing, including unnecessarily pretentious vocabulary, semicolon misuse, overuse of italics, and inappropriate use of epithets (i.e., random descriptors for familiar people -- e.g., a guy in a first-person narration randomly referring to his wife as "the woman" instead of "she" or her name. Excuse me, do you have amnesia...?). Also, even though the characters have distinct voices in their dialogue, their first-person narration all sounds exactly the same. And the pacing, good god. I'm absolutely here for a slow psychological horror, but the monologues ramble on and on and oooon, bludgeoning you with boring and unnecessary details as you patiently or impatiently wait for something to finally happen. The dialogue is often similarly clunky, especially from Zach and somewhat Luke. 


But for all my grievances in 300 hours of this game, I really enjoyed it. It was a good experience and a very promising start for this studio. So, yes, I can recommend it. Thank you, devs, for all the hard work and love you poured into this game and for the grade-A Chinese lesson.

Beautiful told horror story with wonderful voice acting, but at a crawling pace. 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3006963009

[h2]Story and characters [/h2]
When Isabella finds a ominous letter with 'HELP ME' written in blood over and over again, with ending 'show this to five persons or else...', things start to happen. She shows this letter to her friends who don't believe her. Then strange things start happening to them as well. 

The story is told from viewpoint of seven characters, never to return to that character again. There are four friends (26-30) that start their different careers, there is a sole interior designer, and there is a high class couple that are buying the mansion. The characters are deeply written, and are all smart people. All dialogues are voiced by talented actors. The game is accompanied by wonderful music that emphasise the mood. A horror VN can be very frightening, especially one as patient as this one! 

[h2]Describing EVERYTHING [/h2]
I started this game nearly two and a half years ago, I found it very hard to play because the pace is so slow. The game describes everything, and does this wonderfully! But it's a bit saying the same things over and over again in different words. The game is told from perspective of the current character, and I would accept it for one or two very eloquent characters, but not for everyone. Kudos for the writers being very artistic with words, but you kinda lost me there. I know that many people will adore this proza of beautiful words. I just wish there was a mode to have only the dialogues, then I'd probably replay it because I adore the voice acting! 

[h2]Conclusion [/h2]
This game has quite a few a or b choices, and I dive fully into one path. My ending sucked! I can appreciate obfuscation in a horror game, you can't explain something nonsensical after all, but I got the feeling that I didn't get any answers at all! My final chapter has only 7% explored for some reason, guess I made a choice that excludes a big chunk, but I got a very unsatisfactory ending. 

If you like slow reading, this is for you! 

73/100

I don't like horror movies.

I don't like jump scares.

I don't like quicktime events.

I LOVE The Letter.

Obligatory "I've never written a review for a game on Steam before".

I happened upon The Letter on a casual browse through the Steam store. The beautiful visuals, full English voice acting, and excellent animations made the game look really promising. However, I also saw a tag.

"Sexual Content"

However, it was not accompanied by the all too common "Nudity" tag. Those two are red flags for me. After doing my research and discovering that The Letter is, in fact, not a hentai game, I bit the bullet.

41 hours of gameplay later, and I can convincingly say that not only is "The Letter" the best visual novel I've ever read/watched/played/experienced (whatever the correct verb is), it's also among my favourite works of fiction of all time. That includes movies, novels AND TV shows.

The Great
*Visuals are beautiful. The CG scenes, animated sprites, backgrounds, everything is perfect.
*Voice acting is top notch. Really, fantastic job to everyone involved in the VA.
*Characters are deep, VERY different, and multilayered. Each have their own internal conflicts, perspectives, and personalities. The first person voice of the game really nails it.
*Choices that matter. AND I MEAN MATTER. There are nearly 200 achievements in the game, and even after 41 hours of gameplay, I still don't have them all. I STILL HAVEN'T SEEN EVERY ENDING. And, without spoiling anything, you can get endings that range from "feel-good" to "melancholic" to "truly horrific".
*The horror. As someone who is not particularly a fan of jump scares, there was never a time I felt as though the game offered cheap thrills. The real horror isn't even in these moments (although they certainly add to the tension); it's in what the experience does to the characters.
*Music and sound. Brilliant. Not much to say other than spot on.
*The writing. Apart from some grammar mistakes, particularly involving "would" and "could" (specifically, the absence of them where appropriate), the writing is gripping, and written in the voice of each of the characters. So strong is the writing that, even in the absence of all the other positives, this still would have made for a fantastic novel.
*Visible branches. Ever wanted to know exactly why your decisions mattered, or where something changed? The game provides you with an entire tree which shows the paths you've taken previously, just in case you wanna try something different. Why don't more games do this?

The Less Great
*Especially towards the latter parts of the game, there are missing audio clips, audio clips which play in the wrong place, missing words, repeated words, and other slight inconsistencies. Yangyang Mobile are working hard to correct these errors, so hopefully they'll be fixed soon!
*You can't name your save files, or delete them for that matter. It's a minor thing, but given the amount of other conveniences they've included in the game, it really sticks out like a sore thumb.

If you're looking for a visual novel with memorable characters, legitimate scares, great dialogue, a very branching narrative, choices that matter, awesome visuals, and fantastic music, you're asking for a lot in a game...

But The Letter delivers.

The TL;DR version of this review is that The Letter is hands down THE best English Visual Novel produced to date. My usual rating system for Visual Novels are: Story, Graphics, Audio, Gameplay and Characters.

Story:
This is a horror themed visual novel, and it has some of the best writing I've seen. While the plot may not be the most intriguing ever, it is tightly woven in the sense that it covers - as far as I can see - everything that you may question about the game. For example, there are 7 main characters in the game. What if 2 characters die? What if A, B and D dies specifically? How will it affect the narration? What kind of responses from the characters are expected? You can try any combination you like, and you will notice that there are tidbits here and there in the narration that will fill in the gaps and enrich the experiences. It is an amazing effort.

Normally, ghost stories love confining characters to a single setting - just the mansion! Just the hotel! Cut off all communications for XXX lousy reasons! - but no. The ghost originates from the mansion and haunts the mansion in ways you dare not imagine, but it also hunts the characters over different places fluidly. It is not bound to one place, and the characters aren't bound to one place (well on the larger scale everyone is bound on the city, planet Earth, the universe yada yada but you get my point). 

This makes for a very organic and realistic experience that is even better than most Japanese Visual Novels out there, since those games usually have locations that are so systematically defined it feels like there are only X amount of locations that exist in the whole general setting (i.e. XXX City). The point is, the world does not feel small here. You will be visiting various settings in the city such as a theater, a mansion, a hostel, an apartment, rooms, a police station, a library, a church, and office and so on. The world is big. It is not just X girls' houses / school / your house and the end, and that is great.

Graphics:
I'm honestly in awe at the graphics. While I can't claim that it has the best damn graphics in the entire world, it has a unified color tone, excellent background and character sprites and CGs that are ALL ANIMATED. And boy do they look lovely when animated. The CGs especially are fantastic. The beautiful CGs are beautiful, and the creepy CGs are downright terrifying. It's like I'm watching a movie, but with the best shots framed at every second. And did I mention the ghost? The ghost is going to haunt you. You're going shut your eyes in midnight, and ungodly and grosteque shapes will naturally float to your imagination and accompany you in your dreams. It is a gorgeous experience.

And the gore. Oh, my god. The gore. I will never wipe the image of poor Isabella from my mind. I personally love Marianne's because of how... immoral it is. Oh, sweet temptation of sins.

This level of quality is going to spoil me. This is already starting to make some Japanese animation graphics look LAME. That's how good it is.

Audio:
First, before everything else related to audio, I must applaud the English VA. How ironic that a Philipines produced Visual Novel has some of THE BEST English voiceacting I've ever heard. This is movie level quality, if not better because movies have the tendency to go absolutely over the board (Batman, seriously, can you fucking speak NORMALLY for once and not like you're going to murder every cat in the house). This also applies to Japanese VA, which while almost always high quality, takes the word 'overboard' and shoots it all the way to the edge of the galaxy. I never understood why people like high pitched loli voices that are so high pitched the piano probably can't fit said pitch. Kids don't talk like that. HUMANS don't talk like that unless they have a fucking condition, jesus.

TL;DR the voiceacting is awesome. This is handsdown the best English VA I've ever heard from a Visual Novel, and among the absolute best even when compared to Japanese VA. Actually, I doubt there's going to be any Japanese dub that's going to outdo the original VA. I do not believe there will.

Now, onto BGM and sound effects. My judgment in BGM is kinda weak, so I'm just going to say that it's beautiful and well designed. The creepy moments are creepy, the touching moments are touching, it's... really good.

The sound effects? The game will spook you (or get you prepared for the horror that it's about to unleash upon you) right from the beginning when you click New Game. For some people, the clicking noise is going to scare them every time they hear something similar for the rest of their lives. To say that the sound effects of this game are top notch is a horrendous understatement.

Gameplay:
As with most visual novels, gameplay elements are few in this game, using QTE elements to enhance the immersion and experience of horror. But if you think that is the weak point of The Letter, I'm sorry not sorry to tell you that the QTE designs of the game put many modern game QTEs out there to shame. Normally you think QTEs as boring. Normally they're just a nuiscance and considered the 'easy' way out of what could've been a satisfying boss fight. In The Letter, when a painfully sharp and well defined mangled head is taking up 90% of your screen and attempting to kill you, the last thing in your mind when dealing with the QTE is ANYTHING but 'boring' or 'easy'. It's hard. You are being fucking murdered by a supernatural being after all, so it's only natural that it's hard.

But it's not hard without purpose either. You'll notice that the QTEs are designed to fit the scenario appropriately. Button mash (there is only 1 button mash QTE in the game as far as I'm aware, and it's at the very start of the game)? Well that's because your character is trying to wrench the bloody door knob open. Metronome? That's because you're trying to control your breathing and heartbeat in a situation where both are out of control. There's even one that pans the camera horizontally non-stop - and literally, because your character is holding a camera - as you attempt to take a photo of a certain thing in complete darkness...

If there's one flaw to the game, it would be that there aren't enough QTE moments. No kidding. I quite wish I could be spooked more often, if only because they are so compelling to play.

Characters:
Let alone visual novels, this level of characterisation puts games, movies and books to SHAME. Most stories have a character stick to one personality trait and stay there forever. Not this game. Every person is complicated, and every person is a living contradiction. The label 'one dimensional' do not exist in the characters of this game. The writers don't hold back on profanity. The writers don't hold back on issues like racism. Some writers hold back when they try to write a racist character for example, but the writer here states it as the character would have had it. It was a pleasure to read. It's even funny to see people get offended by it by the various behaviors displayed by the characters. I can't even come up with the words to describe the cast of this game, just, play it already.

Conclusion:
Before I started to play this game, I jokingly thought that the devs had spent billions in development. After playing the game for thirty minutes billions is still an exeggeration, but I cannot help but think if they did spend millions at least on it. $20 for this game is a fucking STEAL.  How can you even price it this low it's RIDICULOUS. This is WAY BETTER than most of the localised Visual Novels currently present on Steam. Any Visual Novel fan who misses this will be missing out on a major milestone in EVN history, the best EVN to date and one of the best VNs in history.

Keep it up, devs! The Letter is right up there with VA-11 HALL-A, Clannad and a few others in my best Visual Novel of all times list!

[h1] Grudge Ripoff! - Started Good Ended in a Flop![/h1]

Okay my first impression when I looked at the store page of this VN was: this was definitely a vampire romantic visual novel. IDK it just gives filthy diabolic lovers vibes (because of the mansion). That being said this is a visual novel which is created with tons of effort and hard work put by the developers. If you are a horror, thriller, mystery fan or just someone who is bored looking for a good read this is a pickup for you cutie. This is a must add for anyone's collection. 

The story starts all casual paced with Santos Isabella who is a very innocent but fierce filipino girl. She is a real estate agent due to the fact that he needs to send money to her family. She lands a nice deal when a very old creepy mansion is on the market, when she was inspecting the mansion she finds a mysterious letter asking for help. Little did she know that picking up that piece of paper is the start of a fearful nightmare not only for her but for her friends too. 

[b] Story[/b] 
I faced a huge disappointment with the story, probably one of the most insane disappointments I have ever faced in my life. The story started all good, so interesting, so much potential but the ending felt so unfinished or rushed. It is incomplete. The whole story is branched by seven main characters, some of them has completely different variations of their arcs which was very detailed surprisingly. I am satisfied with the normal endings but never ever with the truth endings, I felt that the truth ending which has a huge influence from the previous owners who bought the mansion as well as the villain NEEDS to be narrated, voiced and deserves their own chapter. The logical connection on how the truth ending is related to the characters are there but not the emotional factor. [u]This is one of the few visual novels I have read with so many branching in a single chapter[/u] (namely Luke's), if you are going for the achievements you are up for a nightmare. 

In each chapter you decide the fate of the character and depending on the people whom you choose to keep alive, a new ending will be born.  [i]For the horror factor of the story there is a curse and a villain which is heavily inspired by the hit horror: The Grudge [/i] I named it a rip off because not only the villain but the setting (where the curse begins when the people enter the ghost's house) is also the same.  I am hardcore horror fan who watches movies of very different nationalities. I think the Devs are Chinese and in custom to Chinese movies a logical explanation is always given in their horror and I felt and wished because of the presence of Ashton (who is literally a handsome Sherlock Holmes) there would be a scientific logical ending like the Nancy Drew games or Sherlock Holmes. Unfortunately that was not given and that makes the VN loose some points for excellence. 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2304429757

[b] Characters[/b]
There is a wide variety of characters: Ashton the detective, Isabella the pitiful, Luke the racist, Hannah the sweetie, Rebecca the headfast, Zachary the negotiator and Mary-Anne the conflicted. Each of them in their own chapter addresses the various personal issues they have and how the current curse seem to make things worse. You experience issues such as: Marriage problems, heart aches, sexual identity, racism, intimacy, struggle etc. My personal favorites would be Ashton and Hannah. Ashton seems logical and rational while Hannah is very charming and collected. There is a wide variety of side characters too, my favorite would be the cheeky German Butler who is overflowing with sarcasm. The relationships you maintain with the characters can unlock different types of endings. [u]The characters are of different ethnicities too lol, the couple is British, the Bulter is German, the Architect was Irish and so on[/u] So the visual novel has respected different nationalities and merging of culture because of the characters. 

[b] Gameplay[/b]
This is also one of the few visual novels which I have seen with some creative gameplay which includes various quicktime events, some include pressing certain buttons which are timed or sliding the mouse to a point etc. Ofc for a casual non-horror visual novel this is not fun, but when it is indeed horror you should prepare yourself. There are difficulties for the quicktime events too, I thought the elevator one in the normal mode is quite the nightmare. The game has the auto play, fast skip to the next choice, you can reduce the character voicing if you want for each character, the game was made on par for the ease of access for the gamer. [u]Also there are some known bugs which caused the game to force exit when initiating the super fast forward, those needs to be fixed [/u]

[b] Art and Music [/b] 
So a few words need to be said about the voice acting, it was done very epic. Every voice actor is super talented in this VN. All characters are voiced except the secret characters which is a huge downer for this VN. The music is very good and so are the songs by the actors. The art is quite polished and detailed. The characters have their own animations (blinking, flipping hair, turns etc) 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2186621563 

[b] Achievements [/b] 
Here comes a nasty part lol. This game has some insane achievements which will test your endurance to the max. Despite the torture I had from playing FFXV in the normal mode (hard) [u]I thought I should test this game and get all achievements without any guide or the branches menu[/u] I created my own logical combos of characters which are (lemme count) 60 combinations against 4 versions of endings of Hannah. Therefore, I finished 6 chapters without the branching menu or any guide and in Luke's chapter 84% was done by me. That was the limite of my endurance later I used the guide and branching menu to complete the rest of 16% on my own.  If you are an achievement hunter, take notes of every ending combo you make from the start of the game, it will save a lot of time ngl. 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2310034055 

[b] Overall[/b] 
A fantastic VN with the gameplay, art, voice acting however mediocre story. But, this is a must play for any visual novel fan. Definitely worth the price I paid for it.

I am not one for horror games that rely solely on gore or jump scares, and I was rewarded with trying out this game. The horror aspects in the game are genuinely terrifying and unique due to the addition of QTE. I appreciated the settings option, however, that we are able to turn them off if need be and have the game be treated as simply a visual novel.


The artwork and designs are awesome, and I usually cannot play a game if the artwork is super terrible, however at the price it is--it better have good artwork lol The way they are slightly  animated makes the horror aspects really jump out as well--sometimes literally.

The voice acting is also wonderful. I'm usually the kind of player who prefers Japanese voice acting simply because English voice acting is just usually terrible--but I was pleasantly surprised at how well the VA was executed.

The story is really good so far. I've played around with some of the branching story lines and I really enjoy how varied the endings can be. It is definitely story rich and doesn't rely on just trying to scare you. I'm genuinely curious about the story line and how everything will be resolved. I know the bio states they were inspired by Japanese horror films and they truly hit that nail on the head.


I cannot wait to to see the conclusions to the story, and maybe, just maybe, I can nut up and turn on the QTE again. . . maybe.

Now that I have finally gotten the True Ending (after many replays with various dead body counts) I totally recommend this visual novel :3
Simply put it's a VN where almost every choice counts to shape the story you get with controling a different character on each chapter. They can all live, they can all die, or you just kill some while others live. Each choice is like a ripple effect that impacts the chapters especially the last 2 chapters either in small or big ways.
There are Quick Time Events but you can skip them with TAB if you can't get past them. Various epilouge endings revolving around the character's friendship and even some romantic endings.

It can get a little boring. The story spends a lot of time establishing all the characters' bonds. I wanted Scary Movie, not Friends. But the scary moments are fun. It's a great horror visual novel if you don't mind all the build-up.
