It has a promising start, but it's plagued with to many conversations about house decorations, overly emotional arguments that don't have a proper build up, and generally boring dialogue. Every chapter follows that same pattern of: New character, talk about the mansion being bought, characters getting overly upset for no good reason, and a little be of spooky moments as a hook to keep the player playing. It's super formulaic and way too long. I stopped at chapter 5 out of 7 or so, and after looking up the disappointing ending, very glad I didn't play through the rest.

Disclaimer: I'm one of the lucky guys who supported this project via Kickstarter from day 1 and was able to follow the awesome progression this novel made from an amateurish Unity mockup to the amazing package that was released here on Steam. Only reading the updates was already worth the money and seeing them making their baby professional an unbelievably cool experience, so I cannot exclude being a little bit biased. 

When I started with this novel, I was absolutely tired from VNs and planned to stop reading them altogether. I just couldn't take the clichés anymore and felt like the target audience was limited on teenagers, which is just not the kind of stuff I enjoy anymore. What I expected of this novel was a short little horror experience with its fair share of jumpscares and some amateurish story to let things move forward. What it delivered, however, was pretty much the opposite in every regard.

Although it is being advertised as a horror experience, the heart of the novel actually lies in its characters and "drama aspects", for the lack of a better term. While the cast seems pretty uninteresting at first glance, your opinion of almost any character will probably change once you get to their chapter. I was a bit sceptical if this "Every chapter is from a different perspective" concept would work, but it turned out to be exactly what the novel needed to emphasize its strengths. Every perspective provides a very unique experience: All characters have different ways of thinking and therefore leading the narrative (some are very ego-centered, some can read people like books and act accordingly, etc.). Even if you hate characters first, you usually grow with their chapter for that reason. Additionally, they all have some kind of personal conflict to deal with, leading to each chapter adding to the excitement instead of just driving things forward. Apart from one chapter, I actually was never bored with this novel, which is not often the case. And mostly, the characters get more and more interesting with time, especially Luke is ridiculously well-written and constantly surprises until the very end. 

Since the characters are all in their mid twenties and working, the topics also tend to be about more adult themes, which is a refreshing experience after the 100th "High school boy gets into something with 8 girls wanting his d...direct attention". Be aware though that it tends to get very dark, as expected from the horror genre I guess. Without spoilering too much, it will be about topics like rejection, self-hatred, not being able to move in any direction with your life, what to do in a relationship that seems to fall apart after many years, and so on. If you are into these kind of dark topics and maybe enjoyed works like Saya no Uta, you are definitely in for a treat. The writing turned out to be surprisingly profound surrounding these and can be quite intense if you have any personal experience with any topic.  
  
Another big plus is the level of detail: The devs really did not shy away from anything to make the VN as lively as possible and took quite some risks in doing so. If there's anything in the background that can move, it will. Sprites of the characters actively breath and move around, even smartphones will actively show the call-time. Not to mention this VN is fully voiced in English with lip synchronization. The voice acting is also really top-notch most of the time, although I felt like some actors didn't really find the right tone for more silent scenes and tend to overact a bit. In the first prototype, these attempts seemed to backfire a bit and put it into an uncanny valley direction, but they stuck to their idea and made so many improvements that this feeling is completely gone. This is probably the most lively novel I've seen so far (yes, even with a ghost - oh, the irony). 

On the down-side, the writing was left alone a little bit too much. At the current stage, it's a little bit "from foreigners for foreigners" you could say. If English is not your native language, chances are you will not have too many issues with it and just notice some mistakes here and there. English natives seem to have a lot more issues with it, though. The devs are focusing on improving the narrative now to correct that, but apparently this also includes voiced lines to an extend, so there will be a limit. That does not mean the writing in itself is bad, though, it's just about grammar mostly. 

Additionally, the main story is a little bit lacking. The first endings you reach will probably be a big disappointment, and reaching other endings can at times be a bit tedious because the skipping takes a while and sometimes considered already-known texts to be new because they have some few additional lines. There is a True Ending, however, that adds a lot to lead it to a nice "Make your own theories" direction and gives a more satisfying feeling. However, it's absolutely not a mind-blowing experience in any way and the main story makes very slow progress. So if you only want that out of a novel, this is not the one for you. 

And ironically, I personally found the horror to be the worst aspect. It's a typical "ghost girl" thing with all the typical scenes you can imagine and already know from movies. Most of the time, the scenes are rather short and rely on jump scares. There's no big buildup for a general creepy atmosphere or anything similar. I'm personally only really scared if horror is done in a Silent Hill like way - playing with the unknown basically and making you uncomfortable with disharmonic music and art. It just didn't work at all for me.

tl;dr / verdict: This is for you if you enjoy psychological dramas in a darker direction, but not if you are looking for the next mind-blowing mystery. Apart from a whole lot of grammar mistakes, the quality is top-notch and even a shining example for novels to come. Not only does it not have to hide from professional works, it even outdoes most. It might not be the perfect novel that will stick in your head for years due to lacking an excellent overarching plot, but Yangyang Mobile have shown that they certainly have the talent to do great VNs for an adult audience.

I don't always (or ever) play visual novels, but when I do, it's The Letter. 

Seriously, though, it's one of only two VN I'm in to right now. The Letter is the second of the two and I'm glad I took the plunge.

Seven playable characters greet you as you play through their view of the events of The Letter (there are many more important characters in this story - which is why I didn't say seven "main" characters). You see some common scenes where character routes cross and they meet, but you also see "that character only" content as each one approaches the situation their own way or using their own plans. Each character has a backstory that is reflected in their dialogue throughout the game as well as their initial relationship levels. 

They will have their own unique confrontations with the main antagonist, which are played out in the form of QTE (which are skippable if you wish with no penalty aside from losing a few achievements related to winning QTEs without skips). 

The QTEs are varied, depending on the situation the character is in when the confrontation occurs. Some are more challenging than others and tend to express how the character would deal with the confrontation.

And even then sometimes the confrontation lasts after the QTE and you make choices for the character's actions...and sometimes...those choices ARE the confrontation. 

The characters are voiced and the voice actors did a wonderful job throughout the game. Not once did I think of a voice being annoying or not fitting the lines being conveyed. The voices are diverse, showing the character's different backgrounds and their own mannerisms. 

The unvoiced lines, frequently what the character thinks or feels, are equally important. 

While by no means being a light-hearted story, there are moments of humor and fun. The characters banter and poke fun, other light events occur, and there's just some pleasant moments.  

The game is just well-made from start to finish. It's very polished and stable. I have yet to have any sort of crash or glitch in the game. The art is beautiful. The backgrounds are lively and realistic. The character portraits are expressive, and the CG scenes capture the big moments of the plot with a painting-style look to them and some animation as well. During the epilogues and the journal, you get a nice sketch style as well.

The music is beautiful. Characters have their own themes, which are used in various situations in the game as well as mood/background music even beyond just relating to that character. There's songs in the game that are completely beautiful for the opening, ending, and during one of the routes. The soundtrack is available for this game....highly recommend it.

And those endings. There's several endings and that might be an understatement. So many possibilities and all of them triggered by what you've done in each of the characters' chapters. 

Which brings me to the part of the game I might adore the most...choices matter. Pretty much all of them. They all either alter the route the characters take (including carrying over into other chapters), influence relationship levels (which can impact routes and endings), or shift the plot in a direction (sometimes changing a whole route, sometimes just altering how a scene plays out and the information you gain).

You can try to find the "right" choices, or try to think like the character (I would do this in his/her place so I'll pick this option). The game accomodates it even with the "simple" choices you have available. The options usually convey what the choice is saying about (or to) the character in question.

As far as what I don't like about The Letter...there isn't much I don't like. Maybe the only story issue I have is that I wish more was shown/given about the antagonist's origins or why things turned the way they did to lead to the events of The Letter. After the ending, you get "Memory Fragments" which hint to this (and actually show up in the gameplay during one character's route), but I would have liked to see it play out - I guess a prolouge or even a prequel of sorts. 

True, uncovering the routes can provide information, but I think a full fleshed out prequel would be wonderful.

There's a few typos in the script but it's not jarring and does nothing to detract from the tale. 

Overall, this is a great game, even if you are NOT a visual novel fan. It's an engaging story with likable characters in a well-crafted world that should keep you not just entertained, but replaying frequently to revisit the story and grab all the endings and achievements.

I may or may not end up finishing this at some point but for now it’s being dropped. Good concept, nice artwork, mediocre at best voice acting. What really dragged the game down for me was the writing tho. This is hands down some of the worst writing for a visual novel I’ve read. The first character, Isabella, is a real estate agent, so you’d expect her to be somewhat mature. Instead she’s written like a teenage YA heroine. I found her insufferable and a slog to get through. 

The rest of the writing is so clunky and long winded. This game really really needed a better editor. FYI in case the developers see this review: “The cat that ate the canary” doesn’t mean shy or embarrassed, it means someone proud or pleased with themself. 

What a shame this game was so disappointing, it would have made such a good October game.

I've already seen some let's play of the game before buying it, and honestly i wish this game would had more credits, it really deserves it !
Captivating story with countless endings and beautiful characters. I just love it.
Ashton x Isabella ♥

The Letter is a waste of your time.

I came close to giving up on the game because of its pacing. Every chapter you'll go through the same week as a different character. You'll sit through the same cycle of disbelief before all the characters are on the same page again and again.

The dialogue is ropey at best, and the writers break the show-don't-tell rule at every point. You won't make is through a single line of dialogue without five times as much words spent on exposition.

There's a number of outright weird moments, like the use of a racial slur that fails to draw any criticism from the main characters.

99% of the game is a relationship simulator. Horror is sparse, and the ghost doesn't seem to follow any conventions—something that makes the supernatural in other games and media work well. It's just a plot device to progress the lacklustre story along.

I played the game through to the end hoping for some large reveal at the end that never came. You learn next to nothing about the ghost (unless you let your character's die, at which point you'll get a flashback to show a bit of the history leading up to the game—an odd design choice, as it punishes keeping the characters alive).

If you want a good horror game, this isn't it.

The Letter is a really engrossing VN that I had a great time playing despite some flaws. Although it is definitely a horror VN, the appeal of the game rests much more with the characters and the relationships they can forge or destroy while being afflicted by this mysterious curse. While the story of the mansion itself is certainly a tragic tale, it can feel a bit derivative at times, and it is mostly here to set up the "drama" part of the horror/drama combination, which is top-notch. The writing truly shines in character-driven scenes as you get to know them better. 

The game follows 7 (endearing, irritating, sometimes downright unhinged) characters throughout 7 chapters as they try to figure out the secret at the heart of a mysterious mansion, and along the way, they can fall in love, break up, figure out the mystery, die gruesomely... This is where the game's truly wild branching comes into play: I've played a fair amount of VNs/narrative games and I've rarely seen a game offer that many complex consequences to player choices. Playthroughs can wildly vary from one another, always in a way that feels true to the characters. That also makes the game pretty fun to 100% if that's your thing.

The music and CGs are both excellent and really help establish the atmosphere of the VN. There is also English voice acting (which I enjoyed) and quick time events (which I didn't), but both are optional.

Overall, I'd recommend The Letter to anyone who, at the very least, doesn't mind gore/horror, enjoys character-focused drama and loves choice-heavy narrative games: I had a great time with it.

The art and characters are great. The characters are complex and develop as the game progresses. The endings are very satisfying and rewarding. The overall game has great replayability, however most of the jumpscares feel like an old flash game.

One of the best (if not the best) visual novels I've ever played. The story is so complex that I can't think of how many hours someone must play to see all of it. The story, the way it is told, the voice acting, THE ART and THE LOVE creators put into this game is just... I can't even describe in words how much of it there is. All I can say is "Thank you Yangyang Mobile for making this game" and hope there will be more games like that in the future. 
I recommend this game to all fans of horror games, books or movies, fans of visual novels and everybody who's looking for a good story. :)

Okay so i'm changing my review for this as i have now finished the game. Yes, there are multiple endings to this game which require you to go back into the story and find out more about the past and the ghost lady and other things  but there lies the problem: the story! Ill say the good things first at least, to give the game and the developers a fair shot.

PROS:
Characters: The game has some good relationship building between the characters and how it can change for better or worse depending on the actions you choose over the course of the game. Everyone could be lovey dovey with each other or down right hate each other so its good the the story can change in various ways depending on what you do.

Voice Acting: The voice acting isn't great in English but come on, is there really any game where games like this is better in English than Japanese, but i do like some of the voice acting like Lukes, Ash, Zach or Marianne. They do a good job with them for the most part but with the others, eeeeh not so much.

Visual Affect: Some of the visual affect look nice, especially the food that they show, oooooh i wanted to just take a bite. The visual affect the game has is pleasing to the eye and has good affect for certain situations.

OTEs: As far as i know there were at least 5 OTEs in this game, there's probably more if you play the game in a different story branch but at least 3 of them made me panic to get it done with 2 of them i failed multiple times cause i kept screwing up and they did make me feel like shit when i failed (thank god for continues). They can catch you off surprise if you aren't paying attention and if you are struggling with it, you can always skip it so that's nice.

CONS

Story: The story is dragged on waaay too long for certain things and it can be quite annoying when stuff happens with the characters. They will be told of something unnatural, go about their lives and see the same thing they were told but never, and i mean NEVER, tell the rest of the group till near the end. You just want to slap them and tell them to man up and speak but no, you gotta wait for the end of each character till they do anything. FOR ALL 7 CHARACTERS!! You will get annoyed about this. Another thing is that the story is really boring, by the time i reached the end and we got some sort of ....information about the antagonist, i didn't really give a damn and just wanted the team to kick her teeth in. I didn't feel sad, remorse, pity or anger for her, i just wanted her to crawl back to her hole and be quiet because she was more Ronald McDonald than the The Grudge Girl form the Movie The Ring: she was just a joke and while I am talking about the antagonist.

The Antagonist: SHE IS CAPITAL B: BORING. Her backstory (from what i got from the snippets i got from my first playthrough) made me understand what she was, why she was doing what she was doing and her reason but her entire character was so boring to me that i don't even want to know her full backstory as, like i said earlier, shes boring. You can scan through all the dialogue if you want to quickly find out but still, it is an effort to do so.

Where Is The Horror?: This is meant to be a Visual Horror Novel but where in the Blue Hell is the horror? I only got a couple of jumps but only from the start screen where she screamed really loud and again near the end game but throughout the entire game, i was more laughing at how ridiculous she was at trying to be scary. Maybe if she popped up and screamed at random times i would've got a lot more scared, waiting till she attempts it again but no, no such thing here.

Dialogue: Ill keep this short which the game does not do. The dialogue for the characters are stretched out too much. You will probably get bored soon and will either spam space to make it go quicker or skip it entirely.

Overall this game has got its ups and can be enjoyed in some aspects but the Cons for this is used heavily on this game that you will either get bored and forget it or force yourself to finish it so you can come off it. It could be really interesting if you a sadist and make every bad choice just so you can see everyone die but I've got other games where i can do that in a much flashier sense.

Me before playing: There is no way a visual novel is going to scare me.
Me after playing: There is no way I'm going to sleep with the lights off. In fact, I will wait until the sun rises before going to bed.

10/10 Would get cursed again.

I bought this game on an impulse the second I saw it on steam. Its not very often you find a horror Visual Novel. The features were different from anyother VN I've played and reviews on steam were overly positive. 

I was sucked into this game the moment I started reading it. Theres hardly a thing I don't like about the game. Everything is well done. The art, cgs, music, etc. After completing the the game once in about 35 hours I am looking forward to seeing what other kind of endings and outcomes are possible. Especially with all the outcomes in the Gallery. 

One of the best things about this VN is how you play as each of the main characters in a certain order. Giving you just enough information into what kind of person they are without spoiling any outcomes until you come to the last person. Perfectly piecing it all together. I love being able to dive into another characters mind vs playing the same character the whole time.

While this game is mostly jump scares and builds up right to it the quick action events certainly give it some charm. Only one quick action was a bit of putting, but they're easily skipable or you can just turn them right off. 

Newho thx for reading my review. Bb.

This is an amazing visual novel with an actual flowchart to track which story nodes you've explored. The team behind it is very dedicated and responsive to issues. I also really like that it has almost no jump scares, and the few it does have are well telegraphed so you can prepare yourself. Every character has a very strong backstory and it was really interesting to see how all the different possible relationships played out.

Also, the music is utterly gorgeous.

I will be honest, at first I was very reluctant to buy the game. Of course the visuals looked great but I had no clue if the story would live up to what I was expecting. The game was 20 bucks and to the 30 bucks in my wallet, it was a risk, but it was worth it. It was honestly one of the best games I've played in a very long time. The characters were so distinct from one another and the story line was great. I loved how unique it was. 10/10 would play again.

As you can see from my time played, I tried really hard to give this game a fair shake. I'm willing to invest a lot into VNs even when they don't seem great at first because some of the best ones start out slow, then eventually culminate in incredible ways. Based on the reviews, I expected this game to turn it all around with the true ending. It didn't. The word count is also nothing to brag about—it could have been edited to be a third of its length and been more effective. The characters lean heavily on a couple traits each, which could be fine if you weren't reading their rambling thoughts for hours on end. I really wanted to like the Letter, but ultimately I wish I had paid more attention to critical reviews.

A remarkable visual novel we have here! A good and very detailed horror story with choices and many branches, where every character has the potential to get very different outcomes, where death is one of them for literally everybody. There are love interests, potential relationships (friendship/love) and some mystery. Yet, despite having it all, i think some talks between characters were excessively long and somehow a drag in certain situations. Artwork is nice but some characters have too much of a childish appearance, making them look.. [i]cheap[/i]. There are lots of achievements for you hunters out there, but beware that getting them all is definitely a grind. it's known as one of the best VN, but i found it myself no more than good. Probably the problem is me and my relationship with VN though! :)


https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2845962687

Disclaimer: I have not finished the game, and at the time of writing this review I am mid-way through Chapter 3.

This will be my first ever Steam review. I'm a huge fan of both the visual novel genre and psychological horror, and decided to follow The Letter a few weeks before its release. I generally don't buy games until it has received many reviews, but I am so glad I took a chance on this one.

To list a few of its features:

Pros:
- The choices really DO matter. I love how they included a branching guide to let you know exactly which choice leads to what. It doesn't tell you the actual consequence, so it's spoiler-free. It's the best friend of any completionist, and I fully intend on coming back to it after my first run through.

- Complex characters and character development. People may seem like stereotyped tropes at first, but you slowly realize there is more than meets the eye. I also love the relationship mechanic (and no, it's not otome style), which further fleshes out the characters' personalities, and also affects the branching paths.

- Full voice acting

- Polished graphics and artwork, animated CGs in gallery

Cons(?):

- If you are a horror fanatic, this may not give you the thrill you are looking for. While the horror elements are done quite tastefully, the story seems so far to be mostly slice of life interspersed with a few horror scenes. On the other hand, if you do not have the strongest stomach for thrills, this may be perfect for you.

Final Rating: 9.5/10
If you enjoy visual novels and/or horror, this game is a must-try!

---Foreword---

Let me preface this review by saying that I really, REALLY wanted to like this game.  There was clearly tons of effort put into it.  You don't churn out a story this lengthy or accompany it with hours and hours of recorded dialogue if you're looking to make a quick buck.  You DEFINITELY don't include all of the options for alternate events and routes the way this game has, either.  Undoubtedly, the team who put this together really wanted it to succeed, and I applaud them for the effort.  There were genuinely good points to this.

However, if I had paid full price for this game, I'd have likely been a bit upset by what I received.

---The Bad---

Put simply, for a game that has advertised full English voice acting, I found the frequent grammatical and typographical errors to be very jarring.  There were more than a few times where I genuinely misunderstood what was happening precisely because of these errors.  Nearly every other text entry had at least one such mistake, and to be honest, that became grating pretty quickly.

Regarding the voice acting, it often seemed that either the directions given to the voice actors or the voice actors' interpretation thereof was quite out of touch with the dialogue that was actually taking place.  One of the most bizarre and memorable such moments was one character being annoyed at another for their antics.  Instead of an annoyed tone, however, the dialogue was literally shouted / screamed, as if a life-or-death emergency were transpiring.  It wasn't always that jarring, but there was definitely a heavy trend toward "loud" and "dramatic" delivery.  This made it very hard to truly immerse myself in the story.  Also, the Irish and Scottish accents, in particularly, were inconsistent and even at times seemingly exaggerated (not sure if this was due to actual non-native accents or something else).

Now, on to certain plot developments.  For a group of characters that seems quite modern and prone to using their phones regularly, there are a startling number of situations where they suddenly decide that the only way to find someone is by searching on foot, without even bothering to try phoning or texting first.  There are also at least a couple incidents where tension breaks out between characters in a very inorganic way, making it seem as if it was done purely because the writer decided it would be dramatic to do so right then. Similarly, the way certain comments or developments are handled (or just "waved off") between characters doesn't feel natural; such reactions actually feel very out of place, given both the context, their established personalities, and just general human nature.

Lastly, I'd like to pose a personal gripe about the prolific lack of explanation for most of the Quick Time Events.  Out of the dozen or so that I encountered, maybe one or two actually gave time + an explanation before you were expected to actually do it.  The others gave no explanation whatsoever.  This didn't really negatively impact the play-throughs, per se, as a failed QTE just means you have to do it over.  Still, it seemed very odd that only a couple QTEs would have an explanation, and the rest were just, "good luck!"  It was also genuinely annoying to sometimes have to fail a QTE once or twice in a row just to figure out what you were supposed to do.

---The Good---

For what it's worth, there WAS truly excellent dialogue and plot development here and there.  It wasn't consistent, but it did happen.  That's what kept me playing through to the end, honestly: I wanted to see whether the good outweighed the bad.  Because there genuinely was a lot of writing and voice delivery here that felt on-point and professionally done.  It felt completely natural, like I was simply an observer to real life events.

The art and music were all solid, for the most part.  Though I liked some tracks more than others, I genuinely can't say anything bad about the music.  The soundtrack contributed to the atmosphere well enough, though I don't know that there was anything that completely blew me away.  Art-wise, I especially loved the way that there were even different profile images for some slightly different facial expressions on the characters.  That really did a lot to bring some immersion to the game.  However, one thing that drew me absolutely crazy was this stray pencil mark / line / whatever that marred Luke's face the entire game. I thought at first it was something on my monitor; nope, just a little art imperfection.  None of the other characters seemed to have anything like that, and I couldn't help but wonder each and every time he appeared on screen: "Why were you the only one left with this deformity?"

Finally, the endings.  This was honestly one of the coolest parts about the game.  There are SO MANY different possible endings, and many of them are quite organic and well-written.  The fact that this game went far beyond just, "Did X live or die?" was an ambitious and fantastic component that, in my opinion, delivered a very satisfying extra sense of closure to the story.  It also further contributes to the immersion, in that afterward, people still have lives to live after the horrifying event.

---Should You Buy This?---

I personally wouldn't recommend this within my circle of friends unless it were at a substantial discount.  However, if the problems I saw as "serious" aren't nearly as significant to you, then maybe you'll find it worth it.  I want to reiterate that the team clearly put tons of effort into this game, and that I think they still accomplished a great deal of good things from it.  I just also think that there was a lot that they missed.  A touch-up on even just the text would honestly go a long way toward improving the game.

Moves at a snail's pace with entirely too much dialogue that's either in dire need of trimming down or not even necessary to move the story forward. This was my first real attempt at getting through a VN, so take that with a grain of salt if this kind of verbosity is typical of the genre, but man I just couldn't do it anymore after getting through nearly half the story and realizing how little of the dialogue had felt like anything other than window dressing. I should not be feeling like things are still getting set up halfway through. To boot, I didn't find it scary even during the small handful of times it even tried to be. There are only so many times you can show someone having the *exact same* hallucination and never build on it at all without expecting things to get stale. Lastly I found the voice acting pretty cringey, but luckily was able to turn that off right away and just read.

I really wanted to finish a VN after having briefly tried and fallen off a few others early on without really giving them a chance. But I think I gave this more than enough time to start going somewhere and get me invested and it just never happened.

This game was a surprise gem, and it completely exceeded my expectations. As both a fan of horror and visual novels, I'm used to lowering my standards and giving the creators a pass because these two things are difficult to do RIGHT and you can't acount for everyone's taste but THIS GAME. It went above and beyond. You can see how much its developers cared about it. They put their attention in the details. They put their love into the characters. They put blood and sweat into the writing and it absolutely shows. For a small team, this is an incredible achievement and it's a beautiful game. 

You may question the price point, because generally VN games are cheap and easy to produce, but this game is absolutely worth its cost. The character portraits, CGs and backgrounds are all animated. The game is fully voice acted (and extremely well done). The music is fantastic. The QTE's spice up the story and add a bit of challenge to what is otherwise mostly a passive reading experience. The devs worked their asses off to make this game stand out and it worked. I loved it.

And for me personally, I am so, so happy to see a game with a culturally diverse and queer cast. Three of the seven protags are people of colour. Two of the ladies are bisexual. My utmost favourite character is the scarily competant butler who is happily married to his husband while they raise two kids together. None of this is sensationalised, and yet they're not unimportant or 'tacked on' details. They're just part of what makes these characters who they are: complex and fully realised.

The first chapter may feel a bit slow due to the need to introduce all the main players and the plot, and maybe by chapter 5 you'll find yourself feeling impatient because HOW DOES THIS END, WHAT IS AT THE HEART OF THIS MYSTERY, but it's the kind of impatience that leaves you hungry for more. There's so much to discover among the choices and concequences of each story branch, a single playthrough isn't nearly enough. You'll come back again and again, teasing out a little more of the story each time.  It's the kind of horror you need multiple runs at, picking it apart, unravelling it a bit more each time. 

Obviously if you're not into VNs this probably won't be for you, but definitely if you're a fan of horror and creative story telling I fully reccomend it. At the very least, watch the sweet anime trailer and see if it tickles your interest.

Pros: 
- Awesome non-linear, compelling story
- Fantastic characters that mostly avoid the horror stereotypes and tropes
- Great music and sound design
- Gorgeous art
- Sweet extras. There's a relationship chart, character profiles, image allery, music room, narrative map...lots of tools to hunt down all the achievements or just play around with.

Cons
- I suppose for a 'horror' game it's not overtly scary. This isn't the kind of game that'll have you sitting on the edge of your seat with adrenaline pumping through your veins. Rather, it's the slow, unsettling horror that comes when someone bites into that beautiful looking meat pie and you realise it's full of bloody chunks of hair and gore. 
- Some heavy exposition in places, mostly in the first chapter, but you're much more inclined to forgive it later when you've become attached to the characters and just watching them interact is its own pleasure regardless of what they're doing.

TL;DR: good slice-of-life = yes, good horror = no.

I've had a mixed experience with The Letter, and while nothing about it was bad enough to warrant a thumbs down, my overall impression was more of a "meh". The story starts out strong only to get lost in the characters' ramblings. It's not an exaggeration to say that over half of each character's chapter is spent either on reminiscing or deeply analysing every little step they make. There's a lot of telling instead of showing, and while the writer does succeed at - what I presume was their goal - making the characters feel like complex flawed beings, it comes at the expense of gameplay. Halfway through I got bored of reading walls of text that do little to progress the story, and started skimming over their internal monologues. It doesn't help that half of the cast acts like awkward teenagers around their crushes, despite being functional adults. Seriously, can we get rid of this trope where being next to or talking about your love interest turns you into a mumbling and bumbling mess? It got old ages ago. Nevertheless, I found observing characters' interactions enjoyable, and it's not half bad as a slice of life.

When it comes to the horror part, however, the game is massively underwhelming. The first jumpscare made me jump since I didn't expect the game to so boldly throw it in my face in the first 10 minutes of gameplay. Unfortunately, it was the only time. It's hard to feel even a semblance of fear when all jumpscares get clearly telegraphed by text before they happen, completely destroying the surprise element. The horror subplot never gets a satisfying resolution, either; sure, if you're observant enough and have played through the game a few times, you can kind of piece together what happened and fill the holes in the story with guesswork, but it still looks and feels like a puzzle with a few missing pieces rather than a complete story arc. It felt unoriginal and badly done, leaving a bad taste in my mouth.

Even though a few parts of the game are thoroughly disappointing, it's clearly a labour of love, and a very polished one at that. The graphics are pleasant [spoiler] except the little girl, Kylie, which comes off as creepy instead of cute[/spoiler], the voice acting is well-done, and the soundtrack even has a few original songs. Despite my frustrations, I can't say I've had a bad time playing it. I'd still recommend it to those who are looking for a slice of life visual novel, just don't expect it to be scary. 

P.S. The game is also heavily influenced by your decisions, and one seemingly inconsequential choice can change the course of the whole game so there are [i]a lot[/i] of plot variations to explore. I'm not sure if one can count it as a plus, guess it depends on the person. For me, it wasn't, and while I tried to 100% the game, I soon gave up as I found it to be tedious, and the payoff to not be worth it. But you might enjoy it, who knows.

[b]“Everything is up in flames, up in flames, up in flames. Everything is up in flames, my fair lady.”[/b]

This a visual novel that I have been eagerly waiting to try. I remember buying it months ago but have been putting it off because I wanted to play it during the spooky season and I’m glad it didn't disappoint.

The story revolves around 7 people whose lives take a turn for the worse because of a letter found in the attic of a mansion. The first chapter tells the story from one character's point of view, the second moves on to a different character and so on…but it’s done in a way that doesn’t feel repetitive or tedious. So there are a total of 7 chapters (which take about 2-3 hrs to complete) + an epilogue. It is a pretty lengthy story with high replayability value.

I’ve never played a visual novel that had quick time events, it was a welcoming surprise. The character models/CGs move and it’s fully voiced. The choices aren’t just there for show, each one I made felt meaningful. The main characters can survive, fall in love, or die tragically based on the choices you make. I appreciate the fact that all characters are fully grown adults as the typical cast revolving around teenagers in a horror setting is a bit tiring. All in all, it's a high-quality visual novel. I will definitely be going back to explore different options and reach the true ending.

The only vn I've had the patience to finish and I'm sure glad I did.
I've done 4 total runs looking at different options and endings and I think I'm ready to share my thoughts.

Pros+
Great characters to either love or hate
An interesting story that brings western and eastern horror together
The alternate paths in this game are detailed and worth pursuing
The art style is high quality and holds throughout
No cheap jumpscares

Cons-
Some of the endings feel stilted if you don't go for the "true ending" (Though I still encourage you to play 1 run blind)
The real backstory is hidden behind finding different paths where you get "memory fragments

+/-
The voice acting isn't top tier but the voices grew on me after a while

In conclusion
I would encourage any fan of horror or vns to pick up this game, it is a great ride. If you're still not convinced there is a demo that lets you play the first chapter and I would consider trying that.

[b]Update:[/b] This is my favorite Visual Novel ever.

----

Still at the beginning of this novel, but so far it feels outstanding.

The production value is top-notch for this genre, the ambience is very well constructed, and the narrative format premise (playing from shifting POVs) is something that I tend to enjoy.

One thing I've been noticing about this novel is how I'm not thinking of the characters as "characters", but as people. They are extremely well written.

To be fairly honest. I needed some time before building the courage to write this. I barely ever express my nasty feelings when i dislike a game. Nor i write reviews on steam... But i NEED to take this out of my chest.

The game/visual novel had a good initial plot, good voice acting, enthralling writing and beautiful art. But the more i played, the more i realized i was being deceived. 

I got to a point that i thought that this game had potential to take the Top 10 place away from Corpse Party (AN ACTUAL MASTERPIECE, CHANGE MY MIND.)

But when i reached the end, and saw the endings, TWO of them actually, because i couldn't believe it would be so hollow and anti-climatic as it was. I was simply shocked, taken by rage. I had so many outcomes in my head of how it could end. But it was so meh that i couldn't believe what i was reading.

And the problem doesn't reside only on the ending, but they build up so slowly to it that it makes a person sometimes jumps through the mindless descriptions of a FREAKING CUP OF TEA. * Sigh *.

I understand that the more detail we have, the better we can picture it. But you don't need to be so explicit about EVERYTHING. It feels forced, it feels like a weird flexing of a writer like.. " OH, LOOK AT ME, I KNOW HOW TO WRITE THESE THINGS, AND DISCRIBE THAT. " Like... Stop it, get some help.

I was really invested in the game, and the story, i grew attached to the characters, all of that to be taken away by a mediocre ending.

I felt like the Dev's simply shat on my face. I've never felt so disrespected in my entire life. 

I really was truly devoted to this game, and with it's story to the point i was talking about it to everyone i know. To be, in the end, dissapointed by the main ending. 

If i would recommend it? Probably to someone who is just as passionate with writing as i am. And also if i hate that person, enough to see her/him disheartened by the ending and development. Because that's the only thing that the game provides. 

I'm saddened by the situation, i regret buying this game. I'm sorry for the foul vocabulary, but you brought the worse in me.

I wouldn't give it more than 4/10.

Amazingly made and I will recommend it to everyone [i]except those aiming for 100% achievements because last two will take you so much replaying you'll hate it[/i]. A whole lot of work and love was poured in this game and it shows.

[i]The Letter[/i] is an incredibly large visual novel—my first playthrough alone took around 19 hours to complete. While there certainly is a lot of reading, the whole thing is fully voice-acted (with great actors), which helps a lot. It wasn't until my second playthrough, however, that I truly realized just how much this story could drastically change. A character I assumed to be essential to the plot the first time around was written out in my second playthrough with just a couple of bad choices, and the game didn't abruptly end with a non-standard game over (or gloss over their ensuing absence). Instead, it continued, with their loss factoring into the continuing story in a way that almost felt as if it was meant to happen. I was also convinced at the time that characters I lost the first time around were meant to go with how naturally the story played out without them—only to find out in subsequent playthroughs that their loss was not necessary at all.

Achievement hunters will love finding all the ways they can shape the story, with dozens of achievements to earn for doing so. Completionists, however, will likely find exploring every possible path in the final two chapters to be quite difficult. Fortunately, the developers have included plenty of quality-of-life features to help with doing all of this. An option to [i]instantly[/i] skip all previously-seen text up until the next choice (allowing playthroughs to potentially be completed in mere minutes!), a branching tree to track what choices have been made while showing what choices remain to be made, and a gallery of all that you've seen thus far are all very helpful.

[b]I was looking through the Steam catalog on my phone a late night and I came across this game so called "The Letter". Of course I had to check It out once It got my attention. It took me about 5 seconds to add the game to the cart and then buy it. I basically just took one look and that was it, I had a feeling that it might actually would be worth the money. This game is addicting and I can sit for hours reading and replay the game over and over again. I usually don't like games with a lot of reading but this proved me wrong, I kinda fell In love with it. [/b]

[i]So here is a quick review of the game...[/i]

The game "The Letter" is a horror visual novel with multiple endings, It takes you to another level full of emotions and excitement. You are the key that leads the game through different situations, everything has an end based on your actions durning the gameplay. You play as 7 different people, all connected to each others stories. 

Build a good relationship between people or make them dislike each other, unlock fragments and find out secrets at the end. Keep track on important moments through your journal and read the biography about the different characters, each one has it own that gets unlocked as you play through the game. 

The game even has incredible voice actings that makes it even more fun to play, they are well done and suits the different characters perfectly. It makes each one unique and more personal. Let's not forget the awesome work they put on the music as well, everything is original and touching.

+ Amazing story
+ Original music
+ Good voice acting
+ Smooth animations
+ Content worth the price
+ Over 100 achievements

I would definitely recommend this game If you are a fan of reading and emotional rides. 
Amazing work and I would love to play more games from YangYang in the future.

[b]If I could buy the game again, I would.[/b]

It seems slow at start but once it picks up the character's backstory, you can fully familiarize each character and understand what they are feeling. The story are viewed from each character's perspective.
Tons of choices, and it really has a huge impact on the story. It really catches my eye, especially that i love good stories. 
Really looking forward more to this dev, also because they're from my country.

I am really struggling to get through The Letter, a visual novel best described as "long on effort, short on talent." 

Unfortunately, the writing chops are not there. The vast majority of the dialogue is stilted, unnatural, and not an accurate representation of how people talk. Things like "Oh dear! You're not eating enough. What will I ever do with you?" in a conversation among young adult friends. Aided by mediocre to decent-ish voice acting, some inroads are made towards establishing distinct personalities for each of the seven protagonists. But the unvoiced inner monologues (which are extensive) are all written in an identical voice that does not match how any of the protagonists would think--the voice of the author's. This is a common pitfall of an inexperienced writer and sticks out like a sore thumb. Anytime something interesting does happen (whether horror or plot-wise) that manages to create intrigue despite the author's limitations, the momentum is quickly discarded with a scene change to banal and meandering conversations about something else. 

The art is "mid" at best. Stylistic inconsistencies abound, and various portraits and illustrations of the same character often look like they were created by completely different artists. The game's best look is the darker, more mature style shown in the steam banner, which unfortunately doesn't match up to the actual game. The "monster" is shown too early, too clearly, and too often, so it quickly loses all impact from a horror standpoint and becomes sort of comical. This is Horror 101: you don't give away a full frontal shot of the nemesis in the first act. On the plus side, the number of locations is high and you won't feel like you're viewing the same places over and over.

I can see from the story flowcharts that a lot of effort went into creating alternate scenarios based on player choices, and unlike most VNs, they have a lasting effect on how the narrative played out, rather than being short deviations from the "correct" story that just result in "bad ends." But is it worth it to go through all of those story permutations if the writing doesn't deliver? Only one of the characters is even mildly entertaining, and he is treated as a complete villain. 

Lastly, the wokery borders on cringe-inducing. For example, the black character (we are told from his bio) is an "orphan of a hate crime." This is a highly improbable origin story given that his parents would have been (statistically speaking), hundreds if not thousands of times more likely to be killed for non-race related reasons. Credulity is further strained because this tragedy is played close to the vest--even some of his close friends don't know about it, nor does the media (which is already investigating him to write articles about his film premiere and certainly would have prominently featured this aspect of his background in puff pieces). These incidents are so rare and heavily publicized that he would have been a mini-celebrity on that basis alone. His bio goes on to tell us that he was then traumatized by living in a "predominantly white community," then later in life wrongfully accused of theft and forced to "clear his name." Moving to the plot itself, one of his first scenes features him having a N-bomb dropped on him by a member of high society (a white male of course) with no repercussions (this would have been a highly-reported scandal). Taken together, the whole thing is so ridiculous and implausible that this caricature (spelling intended), appears to be nothing more than a foil to illustrate that White People are Bad. Some may enjoy this sort of thing, but I prefer more depth, wit, and nuance.

*Note that my playtime is inaccurate because it reflects falling asleep with the game idle multiple times. I probably have about 6 real hours in it.

If only there were a mixed rating. The Letter is a horror visual novel. It is nearly fully voice acted, has ample choices that do affect story and is decent in length. I would give this a big thumbs up if it weren't for a few things. 

Namely, too many choices. Trying to complete all achievements and get the true ending is a pain in the damn ass. The other thing that drags it down is that the story ultimately doesn't make any sense. I suppose if you get the true ending it would answer who wrote the letter, why 5 people, how it targets people and why, is it just the letter or has it to do with the mansion? I've done like 30 playthroughs trying to get it all done and most of the guides out there are crap. 

All in all if you have the patience to spend your precious time getting the true ending to understand the whole reason for this game existing, great. Otherwise I'd just get it on sale and play it at your own pace, just don't expect to have your curiousity sated.

Also for steam deck owners, you'll be disappointed that button prompts for events that occur in game are not natively given controller options even though they're on all console platforms. You'll have to configure on your own or play on desktop.

Isabella Santos is a real estate agent trying to sell a haunted house for personal reasons. She stumbles across a Letter and now the curse begins. If her friends don't believe her, then who will? Perhaps they can survive, too. Hopefully. Your choice does matter. There are seven chapters with different perspectives from the main characters. The English voice acting is not amateurish (you can tell when the voice acting is really bad, but this isn't the case here). The cast deliver their roles very well. I love the art style, the soundtrack, and the CGS. The devs found inspiration from classic Asian horror films and it clearly shows that they don't use cheap jumpscares to make you frighten! You get to view the journal of the events and the branching tree to keep track of your progress. Grab a copy of the Letter. Highly recommended!

[b]First of all:[/b] Don't let my small amount of hours into the game fool you. Although I played only a tiny fraction of the game myself due to lack of time, [u]I've watched Let's Plays all of the endings and routes in the game[/u], so I've seen everything it has to offer. Now, with that out of the way...

[b]TL;DR:[/b] I'm honestly 60/40 with this game; for all the good it has going for it, it has some heavy downsides to it as well. I'm recommending the game because I feel that the former outweighs the latter, and I do think it has something worthwhile to offer to those who are willing to be patient and forgiving of the game's faults. If you do get it, I would suggest it on sale. I'd rate it a 6-7/10.

[h1]PROS:[/h1]
[list]
[*] Amazing artwork with animated backgrounds, item sprites and visual effects.

[*] Equally beautiful soundtrack, with 3 vocal pieces and a bunch more of ambient sounds and songs, which overall fit the mood of the game pretty well.

[*] Great voice acting. Some of the performances stand out more than others (Luke's in particular is absolutely STELLAR and by far the best in the whole game), but all of the VAs (some of which you may recognize from Telltale games) carry their weight in my opinion and fit their respective characters well.

[*] The story is very interesting for the most part, even if confusing and vague in some parts. It doesn't spell the whole thing out for you, especially not in the beginning, and leaves some details open-ended and up to your imagination. Not everyone will enjoy that kind of storytelling, but I think any good horror story needs some mystery left to it.

[*] Your choices DO actually matter. Few games that tout your choices having an impact rarely actually live up to the boast, but The Letter actually delivers. While not [i]every[/i] choice has its consequences - or least major ones - you will definitely see the echoes and consequences of your choices, particularly in the first couple chapters, ring through the rest of the game. Just looking through the routes and how much variance there is, even small changes, it gives a lot of weight to how much effort was put into driving home the 'choices' mechanic of this VN.

[*] Unapologetically gorey and, occasionally, controversial. Most horror games have no issue being all bloody, but aside from a few franchises, they rarely get into the nitty-gritty of death and dismemberment. On top of that, most also shy away from more controversial topics like abuse, racism, sexism and the like... but The Letter doesn't shy away from broaching those topics. I love that if you get a character killed (one of the main ones, anyway), you actually SEE their death in other routes, and it is not a pretty sight.

[*] In-depth and overall likable characters with distinct personalities, even if most of them tend to be tropes of either the horror or anime variety. Some are less developed or interesting than others, but aside from one or two of them, the majority of the cast are interesting and nuanced. Not exactly 'unique', per se, but varied enough.

[*] The timeline can be a bit confusing at first, but the game features a journal to keep track of it and all the events that transpire throughout the various routes. It is extremely helpful in keeping track of what happens when, where and with whom, and the game keeps it fairly straightforward.
[/list]


[h1]NEUTRAL:[/h1]
[list]
[*] Excluding the occasional typos, repetitive dialogue, grammatical errors and awkwardly structured sentences, the written part of the game is generally well done. It's kind of weird sometimes, that the game's writers seem to have a solid grasp on English language, but at the same time, some very obvious and elementary grade level typos and errors are left in, which could have been easily fixed when spell checking. So as good as the writing itself can be sometimes, the repeated mistakes can really put you off, even if you can fill in the blanks in your head.

[*] The plot, while interesting, is also a bit deceiving. [spoiler]The idea of a "medieval chain letter" that curses people is a unique spin on an otherwise familiar trope, but it turns out not to be that way after all. It instead decides to outright borrow (and, at least in one scene, almost identically lift) from its inspirations - JuOn / The Grudge and The Ring, with a bit of The Shining thrown in there as well - and becomes another typical revenge-obsessed evil spirit haunting you gig.[/spoiler] After that, it kind of loses any unique charm it once had, and becomes a bit more predictable, although still enjoyable.

[*] It can be a bit repetitive when it comes to events, especially during the middle of the game when you're replaying the first few days over and over with a different character. It does help to cement the order of events, and the skip button is available for repeat scenes (assuming the text is the same), but it is annoying to have to play and observe the same scenes from a different perspective. Thankfully, the latter end of the game doesn't make you rehash it a final time.

[*] The Letter is [i]long[/i] (20+ hrs for one playthrough) - admittedly longer than it has a right to be, and some of the content is definitely repetitive and at least a third of the game's total runtime is padding. That said, there is a lot of content to go through, artwork to see and routes / endings to explore. The time spent often doesn't feel rewarding enough, but there is at least a skip function, if you're willing to retrace your steps.
[/list]

[h1]CONS:[/h1]

[list]
[*] By far this game's biggest sin is the pacing. While the beginning doesn't waste too much time introducing the story and its characters, by the middle of the game some routes really drag thanks to the characters' inner monologues and unneeded, repetitive observations (especially Ash's route, which is probably the most word / fluff heavy out of them all). It doesn't help that The Letter will jump from a slow inner monologue or fluff sequences into a scare, and then a minute or two later, back to the fluff again. Even for a VN, there is way too much unnecessary text in the game, and it could easily be half its length and not lose anything for it.

[*] In conjunction with the terrible pacing, for a horror game, the amount of scares are few and far between, with most being jumpscares, and relatively predictable ones. Thanks to the absurd amount of fluff, even the amazing art and sound effects don't make the things that are supposed to be scary, actually scary. Disturbing and creepy maybe, for the short time you deal with them, but not frightening like they should be. If anything, there's a strange relief in having some form of action taking place between the dull socializing and inner monologues that the game loves to indulge in. The few horror elements of the game shouldn't feel like a reprieve, but they do... although they also get pretty repetitive.

[*] On top of typos and errors that a game of this quality shouldn't have, there is also one particularly annoying bug that exists where the voices won't play for some pieces of dialogue - or at least I've read it's a bug. Either that or those lines, for whatever reason, weren't voiced or put into the game, which makes no sense and further ruins the mood of the game.

[*] For a game that is supposed to focus on STORY (as a VN and as a horror game), The Letter doesn't actually give much of it aside from vague references and brief, unsatisfying explanations. While it can be and is interesting, the devs seemed far too preoccupied with having their characters recount every insignificant detail and thought of their lives, rather than focusing on the plot that is supposed to be the centerpiece of this game. It treats it more as a backdrop than anything, and makes trying to get any story-relevant exposition a chore. The explanations you do get are either too vague or conflicting to be of any real worth, and your interest feels betrayed.
[/list]

This game is amazing! Beautiful art, a great story, fantastic and diverse characters. 

The horror element's had me hiding from my computer screen[spoiler](that elevator mini-game, ugh. *shivers* [/spoiler]

But I'm also really invested in these characters and their story at this point. 

I 100% recommend this game. It is easily one of my favorite games.

The Letter, an interactive horror visual novel, offers you a rare opportunity to take a trip down nostalgia lane. Step into my time machine and let me take you to a bygone era when your aol inbox was flooded with chain letters promising to wrest you of your firstborn unless you sent 30 virtual sheep to a down-on-his-luck Duke from Moldova, an age where Mailer Daemon stood ever-vigilant between you and absolute financial f*ck-uppery. Now rewind the VHS of time even further to when these chain letters were written by hand, in blood. That’s right, kids! Load up the station wagon ‘cause we’re going to Dis… I mean Ermengarde Mansion!
 
Nestled in the hamlet of Anslem Village, a place truly frozen in time, Ermengarde Mansion is a storied structure that is the subject of much gossip and folklore among both the inhabitants of the hamlet, as well as the citizens of the neighboring city of Luxbourne, where 5 of the 7 playable characters reside. In one of the most efficient writerly moves I’ve ever witnessed, you are introduced to the locus of the thrilling horror, Ermengarde Mansion, by way of Isabella Santos, who works for a realty group overseeing the sale of the property. In fact, it is at an open house that everything precipitously goes to sh*t when Isabella hears some things, sees some things, and then ultimately peeps that crusty old piece of paper that turns out to be a chain letter. The chain letter then serves as the catalyst for the all-enveloping horror that grabs hold of all 7 characters and refuses to let go till the bloody, gruesome end.
 
And this brings me to my first and only complaint. Chain letters. I just don’t like them. I don’t find them compelling as a device. Never have, never will. This is not a challenge. Just don’t do it. As I’m ranting, I realize this is not an objective, defensible position. I just don’t like them and I could have done without it. Chain letters are unnecessary and just make horror stories seem puerile and/or corny. End of story. With that out of the way, let’s talk about what worked for me, and settle in, because A LOT worked really well for me here.

First, the story is enthralling. You play as 7 different characters and see the events unfold from each of their perspectives as you slowly piece together just what is haunting Ermengarde Mansion, as well as how each of the characters becomes inextricably tied to the horrors from centuries past. And I must say, while the characters get wrapped up in the mansion’s specific shenanigans, the fact that each character is also haunted by his or her own past just makes this whole story that much more compelling. What an expert writerly move. You can make the spoopiest game in all the wild, wild West, but if your characters aren’t pulling their own weight a little bit, the story can fall flat. For any horror story to be successful, we need characters that have actual stake in the game that is personal to their own fleshed-out lives, and Yangyang Mobile knew this and delivered. Closely related to the story, The Letter offers a branching narrative where choices matter, and boy oh boy do they actually, if my death count serves as any indication!
 
This next one seems like a no-brainer. The writing. I mean, it’s a visual novel. An incredibly text-heavy one at that! The quality of the writing can literally make or break a game in this genre. And boy did the team deliver. I play a lot of … questionable … games in this genre with meh writing. Given that, I honestly didn’t have the highest of expectations, but I was absolutely blown away! The team gives us beautiful renderings of the psychological states of the characters and such vivid, concrete descriptions of pretty abstract things like jealousy and terror. I can’t stress this enough. That is REALLY difficult to do. And every time the team stepped up to the plate, they knocked it out of the park. I honestly could not point to a single weak moment in the writing.
 
Having just said that, it pains me to say that the grammatical errors were just frequent enough to distract me from the beautiful writing going on here. I hate carping on grammar. My honest opinion is that if the writing is good, who cares about all that sh*t? And the devs’ literary prowess affirmed my belief in that; however, the verb tense issues and preposition errors proved to be formidable obstacles during my playthrough of the game on my channel. But honestly, I’ll take stellar writing riddled with grammatical errors over flawlessly mechanical, boring writing any day of the week.
 
Another strong point in favor of this game is that the lines of dialogue are fully voice-acted, which proved to be such a blessing because, and I can’t say this enough, there is A LOT of text. It was such a welcome reprieve to be able to sit back and let the characters speak for themselves while I just had to read the general narration, as well as the character-specific narration, depending on from whose perspective I was seeing the story.
 
Related to the full voice-acting, the character portraits that pop up next to the text boxes are well-drawn and show a range of emotion! This small, yet significant detail allowed for greater immersion, as I had yet another detail to properly interpret how a line was delivered and what was meant to be conveyed. And when various characters are conversing with one another, the character models are actually animated! I am so used to visual novels with static images and a few versions of the characters that cycle back and forth depending on the emotional state of the character. This was not so in The Letter, and what a refreshing feature that sure as sh*t must have taken a lot of painstaking effort.
 
And that’s probably the common thread woven throughout this entire project – painstaking effort. This was obviously a labor of love, and it paid off immensely. From the beautifully designed scenes to the expressive character models to the illustrations of really impactful moments in the narrative, my god everything was such a joy to look at. And don’t get me started on the music. The soundtrack especially excelled at making me spoop my pants. Coupled with some pretty creepy illustrations and some bone-chilling voice-acting, I don’t think I have a clean pair of trousers left. Yangyang Mobile, I’ll be sending you my drycleaning bill.

So far, it's good.  I've been playing on Deck.  I had read the game's QTEs require a full keyboard, so I disabled QTEs in the options.  On Deck it defaults to trackpad as mouse and using A to proceed text, so it's easy to play this as a purely reading visual novel and use the trackpad to save and exit.

This game is beautifully made. The art is phenomenal, and the storyline can change so much based on your choices that it does have a decent amount of replayability. The music for the game is also very well done. I do wish there was a couple more chapters to get a bit more backstory on the mansion, but overall this game was worth it! I loved the amount of work put into the game.
So much more than I expected, I am completely hooked! Great storyline, amazing replayability, and you can really see the care the devs have put in this game. 10/10 would recommend

As a general rule, I dislike horror. Yet [i]The Letter[/i] did a fabulous job at entertaining me and making me feel emotions that extend beyond what the genre usually offers.

One of the first epithets that come to my mind when thinking about this game is « cinematic ». At least during the first playthrough (which has an average length of 25-30h), it felt like watching a serial, where you navigate in unknown waters during the first episodes before the pieces of the puzzles finally assemble. The division of the game into 7 chapters helped ensure that the reader would stay hooked for the next one, and learn how the new character would react to already known events and new ones alike. 
Some might find it tedious (because it involves re-reading some scenes several times), but I personally loved the introspective, almost psychological approach the developers chose with this game, allowing the player to delve into the mind of each protagonist while being privy to elements the character themself is yet to discover.

While the plot might not offer anything that could be deemed « ground-breaking », the characters clearly are the highlight of the game, as they feel palpable and real: they all come with their qualities and flaws (though one of them is *extremely* flawed, but is written as such on purpose), their aspirations, secrets and fears. It was a pleasure to navigate into each chapter and get to know each of them by learning their thought process and analysing the situation from their point of view. The relationships between them, mainly the friendship between Isabella, Rebecca, Zachary and Ashton, transpired genuineness. One of the quieter moments in which they reflect upon their bonds even brought me close to tears.

One could argue that the writing needs a bit more polishing, especially in the second half of the game (pertaining mainly to a couple grammatical errors), but these flaws are minor enough, and overall the game’s writing style is compelling and often shines throughout the dialogues. I especially appreciated the fact that the « bad choices » were not obvious to guess at all and often felt less like « X character is being mean because of unknown reasons » (something that I find grating in a lot of VNs) and more like « this character actually has good motivations to say something that might be badly received by their interlocutor ». Overall, these choices managed to give a better understanding of the characters at large. It is something that I feel is rare enough in visual novels in general, and deserves to be emphasised.

As far as the horror aspect is concerned, I found it extremely efficient and borderline nightmarish at the beginning. But since the jumpscares tend to use similar mechanics during the entire game, one might inevitably become desensitised to it on the long run. On the other hand, this allows the reader to focus more on the actual plot and characters, which is a win for me. This might also be an unpopular opinion, but I think the chapters that made the most use of psychological horror (especially Marianne’s) instead of simply relying on gore and creepy sounds were the best. 

I’d like to give a special mention to two key aspects of [i]The Letter[/i]. First, the art: the semi-realistic/semi-anime style mingled with some animation effectively breathes life into the game and is just beautiful to look at (at least in the scenes that don’t involve horror and gore, hm).
And last but certainly not least, the voice acting in this game is simply amazing. It’s lively, nuanced, and downright impressive at some key points of the game. I confess I have loaded several of my favourite moments several times after having finished the game just to listen to some of the dialogue again. Props to the cast and audio director!

TL;DR: [i]The Letter[/i] is a must for fans of visual novels: the writing of the characters, art and voice-acting are top-notch. And there is a LOT of content to read and unlock (CGs, achievements, epilogues), as well as a ton of variations, which gives it great replay value. While the plot could at some moments seem a bit dragged out, the outcome of the story and each character’s individual arc is still very satisfying, particularly when the True Ending is reached.
Especially recommended for people who favour character development, well-defined relationships and quality dialogue. Pure horror fans can give it a try, but I think it will mostly satisfy people who prefer a well-set creepy atmosphere and a general feeling of uneasiness to jumpscares and gore.

I don't write reviews often, if at all! I'm not a huge fan of VNs in general either. To tell the truth I was kinda forced to play 'The Letter' by a friend of mine who played it first but boy oh boy...

The animated backgrounds as well as the sprites are well done to say the least. It's not hard to see that there's been a lot of effort put into making this game. I have no complaints about the voice acting either - I didn't even expect the game to have it. The animated intro is a cool addition too. It's almost like people have enjoyed making this game. I was positively surprised with the quality and the writing of the game.

I'm quite happy with the writing for about 65-75% of the game. I find the last two chapters, or at least certain parts of them, questionable. While I understand that some aspects were made this way (possibly) for a dramatic effect or due to a heightened emotional level, I fail to see a justification for a few of them.

For example:

1. [spoiler] Rebecca's death - jumper - why would our cuddly wraith simply wait for Becca to finish phoning Ash and have their past from the 5th grade up discussed? I mean.. was she sipping on a cuppa while waiting on Becca or something? [/spoiler]
2. [spoiler] Ash's death - bolt through the chest - where did it come from? It's a bit too much out of the blue for me.[/spoiler]
3. [spoiler] When everybody's alive and Ashton infiltrates the mansion, he's overpowered by our beloved German butler, Isabella arrives first and then you have this love scene and a confession (if you played your cards right that is). I understand that I'm being quite nitpicky however I cannot let this one go. I mean the whole group moved in to assist Ash but Isabella (somehow) was first to reach him and make out with Ash right in front of passed out German who's handcuffed to a bed frame. Personally it's a bit of a stretch even though I found it rather hilarious. [/spoiler]

Quick, biased and very personal character overview:
[h1] Isabella: [/h1]
Nice character, positive and easy going. Her reactions to the predicament and issues she faces seem quite natural. The only problem I have with her is how she copes with [spoiler] the death of her father as well as being haunted[/spoiler]. After all that she should be rather shaken, right? And yet she can be positive and keep on cracking jokes.
[h1] Hannah: [/h1]
Hated her guts at first but in the end I grew to like her. I was strongly pushing for [i] my happy ending [/i] and I was quite satisfied with the turn out. High five to developers for her personality and choices offered in her path! [spoiler] offering a cuppa to a whipping stranger in the cellar was absolutely brilliant - I still laugh every now and again [/spoiler]
[h1] Zachary: [/h1]
A people-pleaser. Positive to the bone, a big walking cuddly teddy bear. Hard not to like him.
[h1] Marianne: [/h1]
Lovely Irish accent. A huge brownie point for the voice actress. I loved her character as a whole. [spoiler] Especially the drinking and drunk karaoke sessions. And there's this nice CG with Luke too. Mint is alright. [/spoiler]
[h1] Rebecca: [/h1]
Has 99 problems... Ashton is all of them.
[h1] Ashton: [/h1]
Portrayed well enough as a man of reason. Stays in character throughout the story. I would love to see what would have happened with him had he encountered [spoiler] miss cuddles [/spoiler] before everybody else. The one big issue I have with Ash is the fact that he has a [spoiler] damn firearm [/spoiler] and yet doesn't use it at all with an exception of [spoiler] shooting Luke if the player chooses to do so[/spoiler].
[h1] Luke: [/h1]
I absolutely love him. Best character by far! Well maybe Johannes can compete with him a little but they are most effective as a duo anyway! - the bromance is 10/10. I cannot praise the voice acting as well as the writing enough - I'm beyond satisfied with Mr Luke.

The game is massive and considering its size as well as the possible paths one can choose to go through I'm happy to overlook the issues listed above. I highly recommend 'The Letter' to people who enjoy VNs or are VN-curious and would like to start with something -  'The Letter' is that something.

The content is definitely worth the price this game is listed at. Go ahead and buy it.

Score: 7.5/10

In short, "the game 'Until Dawn' wishes it had been."

The Letter is amazing. The characters are all vividly portrayed, their backstories moving and the relationships between all of them are so well written you wont be able to help but smile, flinch and cry every step of the way.  As far as the illustrations go, while the artstyle is a tad inconsistent in places, every drawing is lovingly made bringing this visual novel to life. While some of the music can be a tad forgettable in places, that is by no means the norm; the soundtrack is utterly unforgettable when it really kicks in.

When it comes to the story, and the actual horror elements, that is where this game shines. A combination of the artwork, sound design and music, your love of the characters all blend perfectly together creating an atmosphere that will leave your heart racing and emotions soaring. 

I cannot recommend this game enough, full price and all.

wow.  just... wow.
I've completed two full playthroughs with two completely different outcomes.
I am a looong way from done.

The depth of game play and replayability is mind boggling and reminded me of The Cinderella Phenomenon.
